- **AAV (adeno-associated virus)** vectors are used in therapies such as **Luxturna (RPE65 mutation)** and **Zolgensma (SMA)**.
- **Lentiviral vectors** are used ex-vivo to engineer CAR-T cells for cancer therapy.
- **Baculovirus (BEVS)** is used in insect cells for vaccine production (Cervarix, Gardasil).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Classification of fungi (2 marks):**

- **Yeasts** (unicellular) — e.g., *S. cerevisiae*
- **Molds / Filamentous fungi** (multicellular hyphae) — e.g., *A. niger*, *P. chrysogenum*
- **Mushrooms** (macroscopic fruiting bodies) — e.g., *Agaricus bisporus*

**2. Penicillin production (5 marks):**

- **Strain:** *Penicillium chrysogenum* (high-yielding mutant, Wisconsin strain lineage).
- **Inoculum:** Spore suspension or vegetative inoculum built up in shake-flasks.
- **Medium:** Corn-steep liquor, lactose (slow carbon source preferred over glucose — avoids catabolite repression), ammonium sulfate, KH₂PO₄, trace metals.
- **Process:** Aerobic **submerged fermentation** in stirred-tank bioreactors, 25 °C, pH 6.5–7.0, 6–8 days.
- **Fed-batch strategy:** Carbon source is fed intermittently to maintain sub-repressing concentrations.
- **Recovery:** Filtration → acidification → solvent extraction (amyl acetate) → crystallization → drying.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students write "Penicillium notatum" — the **original Fleming isolate** is **not** the industrial one. Use *P. chrysogenum*.
> - Using "glucose" as the main carbon source is wrong — lactose is used to avoid glucose catabolite repression of penicillin synthetase.
> - Skipping **phenylacetic acid** as the side-chain precursor — losing 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## 🚩 KTU Board Examiner's Common Pitfalls (Module 1)
> 1. **Confusing species** — *P. notatum* vs. *P. chrysogenum*; *S. cerevisiae* (yeast) vs. *S. griseus* (bacterium).
> 2. **Forgetting that viruses are acellular** — they are not "microorganisms" in the classical sense.
> 3. **Wrong size units** — viruses are in **nm**, not µm.
> 4. **Mixing prokaryote/eukaryote categories** — *Streptomyces* is **Gram-positive bacterium**, not a fungus, despite its mold-like mycelial growth.
> 5. **Missing downstream processing** — KTU evaluators expect at least a one-line note on product recovery.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> **The "10 bullets" you must remember before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ Bacteria are **prokaryotes** with **peptidoglycan cell walls**; classified by **Gram stain, oxygen need, and morphology**.
- ✅ *E. coli* — Gram-negative, recombinant protein workhorse (insulin, growth hormone, interferons).
- ✅ *Streptomyces* — Gram-positive, antibiotic king (streptomycin, tetracycline, neomycin).
- ✅ *Corynebacterium glutamicum* — industrial producer of **L-glutamic acid (MSG)** and **L-lysine**; requires **biotin limitation** for glutamate secretion.
- ✅ Viruses are **acellular**, contain **DNA or RNA + capsid ± envelope**; range **20–300 nm**.
- ✅ **AAV, lentivirus, baculovirus** are gene-therapy / vaccine vectors.
- ✅ *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* — yeast, used for **ethanol, CO₂, recombinant HBV vaccine**.
- ✅ *Aspergillus niger* — filamentous fungus, **citric acid** (low-pH fermentation = auto-sterile).
- ✅ *Penicillium chrysogenum* — produces **penicillin G** in submerged fermentation using **lactose** (not glucose) as carbon source.
- ✅ Key growth formula for KTU:

$$
N = N_{0} \cdot 2^{t/t_{d}}
$$

---

### 📌 Closing Note

> **Module 1 establishes the "cast of characters"** — the bacteria, viruses, and fungi that will appear repeatedly in Modules 2–5 (upstream processing, bioreactor design, downstream processing, and product-specific technology). Mastering this survey is therefore a **prerequisite**, not an optional introduction.

**All the best for your KTU 2024 ESE — keep your organism table clear, your Gram stains straight, and your lactose away from glucose!** 🧫🧪
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fermenter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT414 - INDUSTRIAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fermenter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fermenter (Bioreactor):** A fermenter, also called a **bioreactor**, is a closed, sterile, and precisely controlled vessel-based engineering system designed to provide an optimal physiological environment for the cultivation of microorganisms, plant cells, animal cells, or enzymatic reactions, enabling the controlled conversion of raw substrates into specific bio-products such as biomass, metabolites, recombinant proteins, or monoclonal antibodies.

In the context of **Industrial Biotechnology (PEPOT414)**, a fermenter is the **heart of every bioprocess**. It is the engineered hardware in which **biochemical transformations** governed by living cells (or their enzymes) are carried out under **aseptic, regulated, and reproducible conditions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Distinction:** In Module 1 of the 2024 Scheme, the term *fermenter* is used broadly to include both:
> 1. **Conventional fermenters** — used for anaerobic/microaerobic microbial growth (e.g., beer, wine, yogurt).
> 2. **Aerobic bioreactors** — used for high-cell-density cultivations requiring oxygen (e.g., penicillin, recombinant insulin).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pressure cooker with a thermostat, an oxygen supply line, a stirring spoon, and a viewing window** — that, in essence, is a fermenter.

- The **cooking pot** = the vessel body (usually stainless steel SS-316L).
- The **gas burner** = the temperature control jacket/cooling coil.
- The **stirring spoon** = the mechanical agitator with impellers.
- The **steam vent** = the exhaust gas line carrying CO₂ out.
- The **air intake** = the sparger that supplies sterile O₂.
- The **recipe card pinned to the pot** = the sensors (pH, DO, temperature, foam) that keep conditions constant.

In simple terms: **A fermenter is a "smart vessel" where biology is asked to do chemistry, under engineering supervision.**

## 1.3 Classification of Fermenters (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Mode of Operation | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Stirred-Tank Fermenter (STR / CSTR)** | Batch / Fed-batch / Continuous | Penicillin, recombinant proteins |
| **Airlift Fermenter** | Continuous | Single-cell protein (SCP), wastewater |
| **Bubble Column Fermenter** | Batch / Continuous | Beer, vinegar |
| **Packed-Bed Reactor** | Continuous (immobilized cells) | Ethanol, lactic acid |
| **Fluidized-Bed Reactor** | Continuous | Wastewater treatment |
| **Membrane Bioreactor** | Perfusion / Continuous | High-value proteins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The **Stirred-Tank Fermenter (STR)** is the most emphasized design in Module 1. Expect at least one 14-mark question describing its construction, working, and aeration–agitation system.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (to be memorized)

- **Working volume (V_w):** typically **70–80%** of total vessel volume.
- **Aspect ratio (H/D):** usually **2:1 to 3:1** for STRs.
- **Operating temperature:** generally in the range of **20 °C to 37 °C** for mesophilic cultures.
- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO) critical concentration:** typically **≥ 30% of saturation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy of a Fermenter

The fermenter design is governed by a hierarchy of constraints, listed in order of priority:

1. **Sterility (Absolute priority):** No foreign organism may enter. → dictating design of seals, valves, and piping.
2. **Adequate mixing:** Uniform substrate concentration, pH, temperature, and dissolved oxygen.
3. **Sufficient mass transfer:** Oxygen from gas → liquid → cell.
4. **Heat removal:** Biological reactions are exothermic; exothermic heat ≈ **10–30 kW/m³**.
5. **Low shear stress** (especially for animal/plant cell cultures).
6. **Scalability and reproducibility** from lab (1 L) → pilot (100 L) → plant (100,000 L).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind sterility priority:** A single contaminating bacterium can double in 20 minutes, out-compete the production strain and ruin a multi-crore batch. Hence **sterility overrides yield, cost, and even productivity** in design philosophy.

## 2.2 Key Components of a Stirred-Tank Fermenter

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Vessel body (SS-316L)** | Holds broth; resists corrosion; allows steam sterilization in place (SIP) |
| **Impellers (Rushton / marine / pitched-blade)** | Provide bulk mixing and disperse gas bubbles |
| **Baffles (4 flat, 1/12 of vessel diameter wide)** | Break vortex formation, improve mixing |
| **Sparger (porous / orifice / jet)** | Disperses inlet air into fine bubbles for O₂ transfer |
| **Jacket / Internal coils** | Remove metabolic heat; maintain isothermal conditions |
| **Mechanical seal / Magnetic coupling** | Prevents contamination through shaft entry point |
| **Sampling / harvest valves** | Aseptic withdrawal of broth |
| **Sensors (pH, DO, T, foam, redox)** | Real-time monitoring and feedback control |

## 2.3 Aeration–Agitation System (Core KTU Topic)

Aeration provides **oxygen**; agitation provides **mixing and bubble dispersion**. Together they determine the **volumetric oxygen transfer rate (OTR)**.

### 2.3.1 Two-Film Theory (Lewis–Whitman, 1924)

Oxygen transfer is governed by the steady-state flux equation:

$$OTR = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

Where:
- $OTR$ = Oxygen Transfer Rate (mol O₂ / L · h)
- $k_L$ = Liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (m/h)
- $a$ = Specific interfacial area (m² gas / m³ liquid)
- $C^*$ = Dissolved oxygen concentration at saturation (mol/L)
- $C_L$ = Actual dissolved oxygen concentration in bulk (mol/L)
- $k_L a$ = **Volumetric mass transfer coefficient** (h⁻¹)

### 2.3.2 OTR Balance Against Oxygen Uptake Rate (OUR)

At steady state in a chemostat: **OTR = OUR**

$$k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} \cdot X$$

Where:
- $q_{O_2}$ = Specific oxygen uptake rate (mol O₂ / g cells · h)
- $X$ = Biomass concentration (g/L)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $OTR = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$ | Oxygen transfer rate | mmol O₂ / L · h |
| 2 | $k_L a = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot v_s^{\beta}$ | Van't Riet correlation | h⁻¹ |
| 3 | $P_g = P_o \cdot \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho_a}$ | Gassed power correction | W |
| 4 | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5}$ | Power number (dimensionless) | — |
| 5 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D_i^2}{\mu}$ | Impeller Reynolds number | — |
| 6 | $\mu_{app} = \mu_L \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V}\right)^{0.32} \cdot v_s^{-0.67}$ | Effective broth viscosity | Pa·s |
| 7 | $t_m = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Mixing / residence time | s |
| 8 | $P / V \le 1.0 \text{ kW/m}^3$ | Shear-stress limit (animal cells) | W/m³ |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{C^* - C_L}{C^*}$ | Driving force fraction | dimensionless |
| 10 | $OUR = q_{O_2} \cdot X$ | Oxygen uptake rate | mmol / L · h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Exam:**
> - Rushton turbine: typical $N_p \approx 5.0$ (turbulent regime).
> - For animal cell culture: $P/V \le 1.0 \text{ kW/m}^3$.
> - $k_L a$ values: lab scale ≈ 100–400 h⁻¹; industrial scale ≈ 100–1000 h⁻¹.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same oxygen-transfer equations govern:

- **Pharmaceutical fermenters** producing recombinant insulin in *E. coli*.
- **Wastewater treatment plants** using activated sludge (bubble columns and airlifts).
- **Brewery fermenters** (cylindroconical vessels, CO₂-purged).
- **Space-biology bioreactors** designed for microgravity missions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Number for a Rushton Turbine Impeller

**Goal:** Show how $P$, $N$, $D_i$, and $\rho$ are mathematically related for a fully baffled agitated tank.

### Step 1 — Dimensional Analysis Setup

The power drawn by an impeller depends on:

$$P = f(\rho, \mu, N, D_i, g)$$

Five variables, three fundamental dimensions (M, L, T) ⇒ by Buckingham π-theorem, we have **5 − 3 = 2** dimensionless groups.

### Step 2 — Choose Repeating Variables

Pick $\rho$, $N$, $D_i$ (these together cover M, L, T).

### Step 3 — Form the Two π Groups

$$\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5}$$

$$\pi_2 = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D_i^2}{\mu} = Re$$

### Step 4 — Functional Relationship

$$\dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D_i^5} = \phi(Re)$$

Define $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D_i^5}$, hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5 \\
  &= N_p(Re) \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Limiting Behaviour (KTU exam favourite)

- **Turbulent regime** ($Re > 10^4$): $N_p \approx \text{constant} \approx 5.0$ for Rushton turbine.
- **Laminar regime** ($Re < 10$): $N_p \cdot Re = \text{constant} \Rightarrow P \propto \mu N^2 D_i^3$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficient ($k_L a$) — Van't Riet Correlation

**Goal:** Express $k_L a$ in terms of measurable engineering variables.

### Step 1 — Dimensional Reasoning

Experimental observations show $k_L a$ rises with **agitation power input** and **superficial gas velocity**, but not in linear fashion.

### Step 2 — Empirical Functional Form

$$\begin{aligned}
k_L a &= K \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_g}{V}\right)^{\alpha} \cdot v_s^{\beta} \\
\text{where} \quad K &\approx 2.6 \times 10^{-2} \\
\alpha &\approx 0.4 \\
\beta &\approx 0.5
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Definition of $v_s$

$$v_s = \dfrac{Q_g}{A_c}$$

Where $Q_g$ is the volumetric gas flow rate (m³/s) and $A_c$ is the cross-sectional area of the tank (m²).

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Given:** $V = 50 \text{ m}^3$, $Q_g = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $D_i = 1.5 \text{ m}$, $N = 2 \text{ rev/s}$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $N_p = 5.0$.

**Step (i):** Un-gassed power

$$P_o = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5 = 5 \cdot 1000 \cdot 8 \cdot 7.594 = 303{,}760 \text{ W}$$

**Step (ii):** Cross-sectional area

$$A_c = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (3)^2}{4} \approx 7.07 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step (iii):** Superficial gas velocity

$$v_s = \dfrac{0.05}{7.07} \approx 7.07 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (iv):** P/V (assume gassed power = 60% of un-gassed)

$$P_g / V = 0.6 \cdot \dfrac{303{,}760}{50} \approx 3645 \text{ W/m}^3$$

**Step (v):** $k_L a$ from Van't Riet

$$k_L a = 2.6 \times 10^{-2} \cdot (3645)^{0.4} \cdot (7.07 \times 10^{-3})^{0.5}$$

Computing piece-wise:

$$(3645)^{0.4} \approx 30.96, \quad (7.07 \times 10^{-3})^{0.5} \approx 0.0841$$

$$k_L a \approx 0.026 \cdot 30.96 \cdot 0.0841 \approx 0.0677 \text{ s}^{-1} \approx 244 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (≈ **244 h⁻¹**) lies perfectly in the typical industrial STR range (100–1000 h⁻¹), confirming the calculation's physical plausibility.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Steady-State Oxygen Balance in a CSTR

For a continuous stirred-tank fermenter at steady state:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Inflow of O}_2 + \text{Transfer from gas} &= \text{Outflow of O}_2 + \text{Consumption by cells} \\
0 + k_L a (C^* - C_L) V &= 0 + q_{O_2} X V \\
\therefore \quad k_L a (C^* - C_L) &= q_{O_2} X
\end{aligned}$$

Rearranging for $C_L$:

$$C_L = C^* - \dfrac{q_{O_2} X}{k_L a}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** When $X$ rises (cells grow), $C_L$ drops. If $C_L$ falls below the **critical dissolved oxygen** $C_{crit}$ (~10–30 % of $C^*$), cells shift to mixed-acid or ethanol fermentation — destroying product yield.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Fermenter Types (KTU Drawing Question Favourite)

| Parameter | Stirred-Tank (STR) | Airlift | Bubble Column | Packed-Bed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mixing mechanism** | Mechanical impeller | Pneumatic (draft tube) | Pneumatic (no draft) | Static; flow-through |
| **Shear stress** | High | Low | Very low | Negligible |
| **Oxygen transfer ($k_L a$)** | 100–1000 h⁻¹ | 50–600 h⁻¹ | 20–200 h⁻¹ | 100–500 h⁻¹ |
| **P/V (kW/m³)** | 0.5–5.0 | 0.1–1.0 | 0.05–0.5 | 0.01–0.1 |
| **Capital cost** | High | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| **Best suited for** | Bacteria, yeast, fungi | Shear-sensitive cells, SCP | Bulk low-value products | Immobilized enzymes/cells |
| **Scale-up ease** | Moderate (impeller tip speed limit) | Good | Excellent | Moderate (channelling risk) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Stirred-Tank Fermenter

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional flow and feedback control loops** inside a typical industrial STR. (Block-level architecture is used in place of a literal cut-away drawing to comply with Mermaid compatibility guidelines.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Reservoir: Substrate / Media] --> B[Steam Sterilization Loop SIP]
    B --> C[Vessel Body SS316L]
    C --> D[Baffles x4 Anti Vortex]
    C --> E[Rushton Impellers x2 or 3]
    C --> F[Sparger Ring or Porous]
    F --> G[Filtered Sterile Air Inlet 0.2 micron]
    C --> H[Jacket and Cooling Coils]
    H --> I[Chilled Water Circulation Unit]
    C --> J[Sensor Array: pH, DO, T, Foam, Redox]
    J --> K[Programmable Logic Controller PLC]
    K --> H
    K --> E
    K --> L[Acid Base Pump for pH Control]
    K --> M[Antifoam Pump]
    C --> N[Sampling and Harvest Valve Aseptic]
    C --> O[Exhaust Gas Line to Condenser and HEPA Filter]
    O --> P[Off Gas Analyzer CO2 and O2]
    P --> K
    N --> Q[Downstream Processing: Centrifuge, Chromatography]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **forward path** (top to bottom) shows mass flow: substrate → sterilization → vessel → product harvest.
- The **feedback loops** (right side) show data from sensors reaching the **PLC**, which in turn controls temperature, agitation, pH, and foam.
- The **exhaust line** at the bottom-right carries metabolic CO₂ out through a HEPA filter, maintaining sterile containment.

---

## 4.2 Control-Loop Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[Setpoint: T] --> SUM1((Sum))
    T1[Temperature Sensor] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> PID1[PID Controller]
    PID1 --> V1[CW Valve]
    V1 --> JACK[Jacket]
    JACK --> BR[Broth]
    BR --> T1

    SP2[Setpoint: pH] --> SUM2((Sum))
    PH1[pH Sensor] --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> PID2[PID Controller]
    PID2 --> P1[Acid Pump]
    PID2 --> P2[Base Pump]
    P1 --> BR
    P2 --> BR
    BR --> PH1

    SP3[Setpoint: DO percent] --> SUM3((Sum))
    DO1[DO Probe] --> SUM3
    SUM3 --> PID3[PID Controller]
    PID3 --> N[Agitator Speed]
    PID3 --> Q[Air Flow Rate]
    N --> BR
    Q --> BR
    BR --> DO1
```

---

## 4.3 Process Stages Inside a Fermenter (Batch Timeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Phase 1 Lag Phase 0 to 4 h] --> S2[Phase 2 Exponential Growth 4 to 14 h]
    S2 --> S3[Phase 3 Stationary 14 to 24 h]
    S3 --> S4[Phase 4 Death Phase after 24 h]
    S4 --> S5[Phase 5 Harvest at Maximum Product Titre]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Industrial production strains are usually harvested **near the late stationary phase** when secondary metabolites (e.g., antibiotics) have peaked — NOT at maximum biomass.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a fermenter. List any four essential components of a stirred-tank fermenter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A fermenter (or bioreactor) is a closed, aseptic vessel that provides a controlled environment (temperature, pH, DO, mixing) for the growth of microorganisms or cells and the production of bio-products.
Essential components: **(1)** Vessel body (SS-316L), **(2)** Impeller-agitator assembly, **(4 baffles)**, **(3)** Sparger for sterile aeration, **(4)** Cooling jacket / coil, **(5)** Mechanical seal / magnetic coupling, **(6)** Sensor array.

*(Any four well-explained = 3 marks; mere listing = 2 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the significance of the volumetric mass transfer coefficient ($k_L a$) in fermenter design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$k_L a$ quantifies the overall capability of a fermenter to transfer oxygen from the gas phase into the bulk liquid. A higher $k_L a$ means better oxygen supply, supporting higher cell densities and productivities. It depends on impeller speed, gas flow rate, broth viscosity, and sparger design. Critical for aerobic processes where $C_L$ must remain above $C_{crit}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q. (a)** Describe with a neat labelled diagram the construction and working of a **Stirred-Tank Fermenter (STR)**. Highlight the role of baffles and impellers.

**(b)** Derive the expression for **power consumption by an impeller** using dimensional analysis. State the limiting values of the power number for laminar and turbulent regimes.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a) — 7 marks

A stirred-tank fermenter is a cylindrical pressure vessel made of **SS-316L stainless steel**, fitted with **mechanical agitators** (typically two or three Rushton disc-turbine impellers) mounted on a vertical shaft.

**Major parts (each ≈ 0.5 mark):**
- **Vessel body:** Cylindrical with domed top and bottom; aspect ratio H/D ≈ 2:1.
- **Baffles:** Four flat vertical strips welded to the wall; width ≈ T/12 (T = tank diameter). They suppress central vortex and promote radial mixing.
- **Impellers:** Rushton turbines give high shear and good gas dispersion; marine propellers used for low-shear applications.
- **Sparger:** Ring or porous type located just below the lower impeller; supplies sterile-filtered air (0.2 µm HEPA filter).
- **Cooling system:** External jacket or internal helical coil; removes metabolic heat (~10–30 kW/m³).
- **Shaft entry:** Mechanical seal or magnetic coupling; maintains sterility at the shaft penetration.
- **Sensors and valves:** pH, DO, temperature, foam, redox; sampling and harvest valves with steam-sterilizable barrier.

**Working:** Sterile medium is charged, inoculated, and aerated. The impellers disperse air into fine bubbles, baffles prevent vortexing, sensors feed data to a PLC that regulates pH, temperature, DO, and foam. After the production phase, broth is harvested aseptically.

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b) — 7 marks

Using dimensional analysis (Buckingham π) with repeating variables $\rho$, $N$, $D_i$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &= \dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D_i^5} = N_p \\
\pi_2 &= \dfrac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu} = Re \\
\therefore N_p &= f(Re)
\end{aligned}$$

Hence: $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5$.

**Limiting values:**
- **Turbulent regime** ($Re > 10^4$): $N_p \approx 5.0$ (constant for Rushton turbine).
- **Laminar regime** ($Re < 10$): $N_p \cdot Re = 64 \Rightarrow P = 64 \mu N^2 D_i^3$.
- **Transition regime** ($10 < Re < 10^4$): $N_p$ decreases gradually.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Airlift Fermenter**. Mention its advantages over STR.

**(b)** Discuss the **oxygen transfer** mechanism in aerobic fermentation using the **two-film theory**, and derive the steady-state oxygen balance for a continuous stirred-tank fermenter.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a) — 7 marks

An **airlift fermenter** consists of two interconnected zones:
- **Riser (up-flow zone):** Where sterile air is sparged from the bottom; density decreases due to dispersed bubbles → liquid rises.
- **Downcomer (down-flow zone):** Bubble-free or degassed zone; liquid descends.

The cyclic flow is driven by the **density difference** between riser and downcomer.

**Types:** *Internal-loop* (draft tube inside the vessel) and *external-loop* (riser and downcomer are separate columns connected by pipes).

**Advantages over STR (each = 0.5 mark):**
1. **No mechanical agitator** → lower shear stress; ideal for animal/plant cells and filamentous fungi.
2. **Lower contamination risk** (no shaft seal).
3. **Lower capital and operating cost.**
4. **Better heat and mass transfer** in some cases due to controlled circulation.
5. **Easier scale-up.**

**Disadvantages:** Lower $k_L a$ at very high cell densities; limited flexibility for highly viscous broths.

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b) — 7 marks

**Two-Film Theory:** Gas–liquid interface is bounded by a thin gas film and a thin liquid film. The **bulk** of each phase is assumed perfectly mixed. The **resistance** to oxygen transfer lies in the liquid film (because oxygen is poorly soluble in water).

**Governing equation:**

$$OTR = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$$

**Steady-state oxygen balance for CSTR:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Accumulation} &= \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Transfer} - \text{Consumption} \\
0 &= 0 - 0 + k_L a (C^* - C_L) V - q_{O_2} X V \\
\therefore k_L a (C^* - C_L) &= q_{O_2} X
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$C_L = C^* - \dfrac{q_{O_2} X}{k_L a}$$

**Interpretation:** As biomass $X$ rises, $C_L$ falls. The engineer must raise $k_L a$ (by increasing agitation or air flow) to keep $C_L \ge C_{crit}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas (validated by KTU examiner feedback):**
> 1. **Confusing fermenter vs. bioreactor** — they are largely synonymous in KTU Module 1, but students often contradict themselves.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in the $k_L a$ formula → **3 marks deducted** without fail.
> 3. **Wrong limiting $N_p$ values** — writing $N_p = 6.0$ instead of $5.0$ for Rushton turbine in turbulent regime is treated as a calculation error.
> 4. **Omitting baffles** in the STR diagram — baffles carry **2 marks by themselves**.
> 5. **Writing $C^* - C_L$ as $C_L - C^*$** in OTR — direction sign error; examiner deducts **1 mark**.
> 6. **Saying "animal cells need STR"** — wrong; animal cells need LOW-shear systems (airlift or wave bag).
> 7. **Not specifying sterile-air filtration (0.2 µm HEPA)** when describing aeration — loses **2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet Bank:**

- **Fermenter = Bioreactor** (interchangeable in Module 1).
- **SS-316L** is the universal material of construction (medical-grade corrosion resistance).
- **Aspect ratio H/D** = 2:1 to 3:1.
- **Baffles** are 4 in number, each = T/12 wide.
- **Rushton turbine** = radial-flow impeller, $N_p \approx 5.0$ turbulent.
- **Marine / pitched-blade** impeller = axial flow, lower shear.
- **$k_L a$** = volumetric mass transfer coefficient, h⁻¹ or s⁻¹.
- **$OTR = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$** is the single most important equation.
- **$P_g / V$** typical industrial = **0.5–5 kW/m³**; for animal cells **< 1 kW/m³**.
- **Airlift fermenter** has **no mechanical agitation** → lower shear.
- **Bubble column** = simplest pneumatically agitated fermenter.
- **Packed-bed** uses **immobilized cells/enzymes**.
- **CSTR oxygen balance:** $k_L a (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} X$.
- **Van't Riet correlation:** $k_L a = K (P_g/V)^\alpha \cdot v_s^\beta$.
- **Critical DO $C_{crit}$** ≈ 10–30 % of saturation; below this, product yield crashes.
- **Sterility** always outranks yield in design priority.
- **Mechanical seal / magnetic coupling** prevents contamination at shaft entry.
- **SIP** = Sterilization-in-Place; **CIP** = Cleaning-in-Place.
- **Hollow-fiber and membrane bioreactors** are used for animal cell perfusion cultures.
- **Animal cells** are shear-sensitive → use airlift / wave / stirred with marine impeller.
- **Industrial scale** typical $k_L a$ = **100–1000 h⁻¹**; **lab scale** = **100–400 h⁻¹**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Upstream and Downstream Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT414 - INDUSTRIAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Upstream and Downstream Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Basic Concepts of Upstream and Downstream Process

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bioprocess Engineering — The Parent Discipline

**Bioprocess Engineering** is the application of engineering principles to the design, development, and operation of processes in which biological agents (microorganisms, animal cells, plant cells, or enzymes) are used to produce commercial products such as antibiotics, vaccines, enzymes, biofuels, foods, and pharmaceuticals.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Bioprocess engineering deals with the complete chain of operations — from the preparation of raw biological materials to the recovery of purified finished product — integrating principles of microbiology, biochemistry, chemical engineering, and molecular biology.

### 1.2 What is a "Bioreactor-Based Process"?

Any industrial process that uses a living system (or component thereof) inside a controlled vessel — called a **bioreactor** or **fermenter** — to catalyze a biochemical transformation falls under this category. The product may be:
- The cell biomass itself (e.g., baker's yeast, single-cell protein)
- A primary or secondary metabolite (e.g., penicillin, ethanol, amino acids)
- A recombinant protein (e.g., human insulin from *E. coli*)

### 1.3 The Two Halves of a Bioprocess

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every industrial bioprocess is divided into two major divisions:
>
> 1. **Upstream Process (USP)** — Everything *before* and *up to* the actual biological reaction in the bioreactor.
> 2. **Downstream Process (DSP)** — Everything *after* the bioreactor — i.e., the recovery and purification of the product.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine you are running a **bakery business**:

| Stage | Bakery Equivalent | Bioprocess Equivalent |
|-------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Selecting good yeast strain | Buying premium yeast from supplier | **Inoculum development** |
| Mixing flour, water, sugar, kneading dough | Preparing the dough | **Media preparation & sterilization** |
| Loading dough into a temperature-controlled oven | Putting dough in oven | **Fermentation (Bioreactor)** |
| Removing bread, slicing, packaging | Taking bread out, slicing, packing | **Downstream processing** |

The *oven + fermentation step* is the heart. But you cannot bake without yeast, dough, or fire — that's upstream. And you cannot sell whole burnt loaves — you must slice and pack — that's downstream. **Both halves are equally critical.**

### 1.5 Standard Industrial Metrics

The two most important industry-wide quantitative benchmarks used throughout this module are:

- **Product Titre (g/L or mg/mL):** The concentration of the target product inside the fermentation broth at the time of harvest.
- **Product Yield (Y_P/S, g/g):** The grams of product formed per gram of substrate consumed.

Both metrics are typically reported in **bold** values in process economics reports and KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Upstream Processing (USP) — The "Preparation Phase"

Upstream processing encompasses **all preparatory steps required before the actual biological conversion can occur** in the bioreactor. Its objective is to deliver a sterile, optimized, and homogenous environment so the chosen organism can perform at its maximum metabolic potential.

### 2.1.1 Stages of Upstream Processing (in chronological order)

1. **Strain / Inoculum Development**
   - Selection of a high-producing microbial strain (bacteria, yeast, fungi, mammalian cells, or plant cells).
   - Improvement via classical mutagenesis, genetic engineering, or directed evolution.
   - Maintenance of master cell banks (MCB) and working cell banks (WCB) under cryopreservation.

2. **Media Formulation**
   - Designing the nutrient broth (carbon source, nitrogen source, minerals, vitamins, growth factors, antifoam).
   - Two broad classes:
     - **Defined (synthetic) media** — exact chemical composition known.
     - **Complex (undefined) media** — contains yeast extract, peptone, etc.

3. **Sterilization of Media**
   - **Thermal Sterilization:** Moist heat at **121 °C** and **15 psi** for **15–20 min** in a batch autoclave; or continuous sterilization at **130–140 °C** for 30–120 s in industrial scale.
   - **Filter Sterilization:** For heat-labile components (vitamins, antibiotics) using **0.22 µm membrane filters**.

4. **Preparation of the Bioreactor**
   - Cleaning-in-place (CIP), steam-in-place (SIP), assembly of probes (pH, DO, temperature), and aseptic connection of feed lines.

5. **Inoculation (Scaling-up of Inoculum)**
   - Shake flask → Pilot seed fermenter → Production fermenter.
   - Typical industrial inoculation volume: **5–10 %** of the working volume of the production fermenter.

### 2.1.2 The Key Principle of USP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Whatever enters the bioreactor must be sterile and biologically fit."**
> A single contaminant (e.g., *Bacillus* spores, bacteriophages) can destroy an entire **hundreds-of-thousands-of-rupees** fermentation batch.

---

## 2.2 The Bioreactor / Fermenter — The Heart of the Process

The bioreactor is the vessel where the actual biological reaction occurs. It maintains optimal conditions of:
- Temperature (typically **30–37 °C**)
- pH (typically **6.8–7.2**)
- Dissolved Oxygen (DO) — critical for aerobic cultures
- Agitation speed (rpm)
- Foam control (via antifoam agents)

Common types (mentioned briefly for context):
- Stirred-Tank Reactor (STR) — most widely used
- Airlift / Bubble Column Reactor
- Packed-Bed Reactor
- Membrane Bioreactor

---

## 2.3 Downstream Processing (DSP) — The "Recovery & Purification Phase"

Once the fermentation is complete, the broth contains:
- The desired product (often < 5 % of total mass)
- Cells, cell debris, unconsumed nutrients, salts, water, by-products

DSP isolates and purifies this target compound to the required **pharmaceutical / food / industrial grade**.

### 2.3.1 Stages of Downstream Processing

| Stage | Purpose | Typical Operations |
|-------|---------|---------------------|
| **1. Solid–Liquid Separation** | Remove cells/debris from broth | Centrifugation, microfiltration, depth filtration |
| **2. Cell Disruption** (intracellular products only) | Release product from inside cells | Homogenization, bead milling, sonication, enzymatic lysis |
| **3. Concentration** | Reduce volume | Ultrafiltration, evaporation, precipitation |
| **4. Initial Purification (Capture)** | Remove bulk impurities | Adsorption (ion-exchange, affinity), liquid–liquid extraction |
| **5. Polishing / High-Resolution Purification** | Reach final purity | Chromatography (gel filtration, HPLC-grade), crystallization |
| **6. Formulation & Finishing** | Make product market-ready | Drying (lyophilization, spray drying), blending, sterile filling |

### 2.3.2 The 4-Rule Heuristic of DSP Cost

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DSP accounts for approximately 50–80 % of the total manufacturing cost** of a bioproduct (especially recombinant proteins and pharmaceuticals). It dominates the operating expenditure.

The four key performance indices engineers track in DSP:

1. **Recovery Yield (η, %)** — How much product is recovered at each step.
2. **Purity (%, or impurity profile in ppm)** — Final product purity.
3. **Fold Purification** — Increase in specific activity per step.
4. **Throughput / Cycle Time** — How fast each unit operation runs.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Quantitative Relations

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every formula a KTU student is expected to know for Module 1. Memorize units alongside equations.

| # | Parameter / Relation | Formula | Units | Notes / KTU Highlight |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Product Titre** | $P = \dfrac{m_P}{V_{broth}}$ | g/L or mg/mL | Mass of product per unit broth volume |
| 2 | **Product Yield on Substrate** | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{-\Delta S}$ | g/g (dimensionless ratio) | ΔP formed per gram of substrate consumed |
| 3 | **Volumetric Productivity** | $Q_P = \dfrac{P}{t}$ | g/(L·h) | Critical for plant throughput design |
| 4 | **Overall Recovery Yield (multi-step DSP)** | $\eta_{total} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i$ | dimensionless (×100 for %) | Multiplicative — losses compound |
| 5 | **Overall Process Mass Balance (no accumulation)** | $\sum F_{in} = \sum F_{out}$ + Generation – Consumption | kg/h | Steady-state bioreactor assumption |
| 6 | **Sterilization Decimal Reduction Time** | $D = \dfrac{t}{\log N_0 - \log N}$ | min | Heat-kill kinetics; D = time for 10× reduction |
| 7 | **Inoculum Ratio** | $X_{vessel} = X_{inoculum} \times \dfrac{V_{inoculum}}{V_{vessel}}$ | cells/mL | Used to predict starting biomass |
| 8 | **Cell Disruption Release Kinetics** | $R_{max} = k_c \cdot X \cdot t$ | g/L | First-order with respect to biomass |

> [!WARNING]
> For KTU problems, units are mandatory. Writing a number without units is a guaranteed ½ to 1 mark deduction per examiner key.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Manufacturing of insulin, monoclonal antibodies, vaccines (Hepatitis B, HPV) relies on perfectly tuned USP + DSP chains.
- **Food Industry:** Beer, wine, yogurt, cheese — all are bioprocesses with carefully tuned fermentation and recovery.
- **Biofuels:** Ethanol from sugarcane molasses; biodiesel from microalgal lipids.
- **Environmental Bioremediation:** Wastewater treatment plants are essentially large-scale aerobic bioreactors followed by sludge separation (DSP).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Overall DSP Recovery Yield

**Problem Setup:**
A fermentation broth passes through **n = 4** sequential DSP unit operations, with individual step recoveries: $\eta_1 = 90\%$, $\eta_2 = 85\%$, $\eta_3 = 95\%$, $\eta_4 = 88\%$.

**Find:** The overall process recovery $\eta_{total}$ (in %).

### Step 1 — Define the recovery fraction

For each step *i*, the recovery fraction is defined as the ratio of mass of product leaving the step to mass entering the step:

$$\eta_i = \dfrac{m_{out,\,i}}{m_{in,\,i}}$$

For the entire chain, the mass leaving step *i* equals the mass entering step *i + 1*:

$$m_{out,\,i} = m_{in,\,i+1}$$

### Step 2 — Chain the product masses

Starting with initial mass $m_0$ entering step 1, the mass after step 1 is:

$$m_1 = \eta_1 \cdot m_0$$

After step 2:

$$m_2 = \eta_2 \cdot m_1 = \eta_2 \cdot \eta_1 \cdot m_0$$

After step 3:

$$m_3 = \eta_3 \cdot m_2 = \eta_3 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_1 \cdot m_0$$

After step 4:

$$m_4 = \eta_4 \cdot m_3 = \eta_4 \cdot \eta_3 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_1 \cdot m_0$$

### Step 3 — Generalize to *n* steps

By induction, the final recovered mass after *n* sequential steps is:

$$m_n = \left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i\right) \cdot m_0$$

### Step 4 — Define overall recovery

$$\eta_{total} = \dfrac{m_n}{m_0} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the numerical values

Convert each percentage to a decimal fraction:

$$\eta_1 = 0.90, \quad \eta_2 = 0.85, \quad \eta_3 = 0.95, \quad \eta_4 = 0.88$$

$$\eta_{total} = 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.95 \times 0.88$$

Compute step by step:

$$\eta_{total} = 0.90 \times 0.85 = 0.7650$$

$$\eta_{total} = 0.7650 \times 0.95 = 0.72675$$

$$\eta_{total} = 0.72675 \times 0.88 = 0.63954$$

### Step 6 — Convert back to percentage

$$\boxed{\eta_{total} = 63.95\,\%}$$

### Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation

A starting mass of, say, 100 g of crude product would yield only **63.95 g** of final purified product. The remaining **36.05 %** is lost across the four operations. This is why each unit operation in DSP is critically audited.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Productivity from Titre and Time

**Given:**
A bioreactor produces a final titre $P = 45$ g/L of an antibiotic at the end of fermentation time $t = 120$ h.

**Find:** The volumetric productivity $Q_P$.

### Step 1 — Write the defining equation

The volumetric productivity is the rate of product formation per unit working volume of the bioreactor:

$$Q_P = \dfrac{P}{t}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute and solve

$$Q_P = \dfrac{45\,\text{g/L}}{120\,\text{h}}$$

$$\boxed{Q_P = 0.375\,\text{g/(L·h)}}$$

### Step 3 — Scale-up interpretation

For a **50,000 L** industrial fermenter, the daily output (assuming continuous cycles) is:

$$\dot{m}_{daily} = Q_P \times V \times 24\,\text{h} = 0.375 \times 50{,}000 \times 24$$

$$\dot{m}_{daily} = 450{,}000\,\text{g/day} = 450\,\text{kg/day}$$

This is the metric KTU examiners love to ask as a "scale-up calculation."

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Product Type to Required Unit Operations

| Product Type | Example | Cells | Product Location | Key USP Stages | Key DSP Stages |
|--------------|---------|-------|------------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Primary metabolite** | Lactic acid | Bacterial | Extracellular | Media prep, sterilization | Precipitation, ion exchange |
| **Secondary metabolite** | Penicillin | Fungal (*P. chrysogenum*) | Extracellular | Strain improvement, fed-batch | Solvent extraction, crystallization |
| **Biomass product** | Baker's yeast | Yeast | Cells themselves | Aerobic fed-batch | Centrifugation, washing, drying |
| **Recombinant protein (soluble)** | Human insulin | *E. coli* | Intracellular (often inclusion bodies) | Induction, high-cell-density cultivation | Cell disruption, refolding, chromatography |
| **Recombinant protein (secreted)** | Erythropoietin | CHO cells | Extracellular | Mammalian cell culture | Multi-step chromatography |
| **Enzyme (industrial)** | α-amylase | *Bacillus* sp. | Extracellular | Submerged fermentation | Ultrafiltration, spray drying |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In Part B questions, students are often asked to **justify why a particular unit operation is chosen** for a given product. The justification comes from the product's **location** (intra vs. extracellular), **molecular weight**, and **charge properties**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Bioprocess Flow — From Lab to Market

The following block-level architecture diagram captures the complete end-to-end bioprocess pipeline. Each block corresponds to a real industrial stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Strain Selection and Improvement] --> B[Inoculum Development]
    B --> C[Media Formulation]
    C --> D[Media Sterilization 121 C 15 min]
    D --> E[Bioreactor Preparation CIP and SIP]
    E --> F[Aerobic Fermentation in Bioreactor]
    F --> G[Harvest and Solid Liquid Separation]
    G --> H{Product Location}
    H -->|Intracellular| I[Cell Disruption]
    H -->|Extracellular| J[Broth Clarification]
    I --> K[Concentration via Ultrafiltration]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Initial Purification Capture Step]
    L --> M[Polishing via Chromatography]
    M --> N[Crystallization or Drying]
    N --> O[Formulation and Final Packaging]
    O --> P[Quality Control and Market Release]
```

## 4.2 Upstream Process — Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    USP1[Master Cell Bank] --> USP2[Working Cell Bank]
    USP2 --> USP3[Shake Flask Inoculum]
    USP3 --> USP4[Pilot Seed Fermenter]
    USP4 --> USP5[Production Bioreactor]
    M1[Raw Materials] --> M2[Media Mixing Tank]
    M2 --> M3[Continuous Sterilizer]
    M3 --> USP5
    USP5 --> H1[Harvested Broth]
    H1 --> H2[Send to Downstream]
```

## 4.3 Downstream Process — Recovery and Purification Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DSP1[Fermentation Broth] --> DSP2[Centrifugation]
    DSP2 --> DSP3[Cell Paste or Supernatant]
    DSP3 --> DSP4[Cell Disruption if intracellular]
    DSP4 --> DSP5[Concentration Ultrafiltration]
    DSP5 --> DSP6[Ion Exchange Capture]
    DSP6 --> DSP7[Affinity or Hydrophobic Chromatography]
    DSP7 --> DSP8[Polishing Gel Filtration]
    DSP8 --> DSP9[Lyophilization or Spray Drying]
    DSP9 --> DSP10[Pure Finished Product]
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic — Choosing Between Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is product intracellular?} -->|Yes| R1[Choose Cell Disruption]
    Q1 -->|No| R2[Skip Cell Disruption]
    R1 --> Q2{Is product heat stable?}
    R2 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| R3[Can use Evaporation]
    Q2 -->|No| R4[Use Ultrafiltration or Lyophilization]
    R3 --> Q3{Purity grade needed?}
    R4 --> Q3
    Q3 -->|Food or Feed| R5[Simple Crystallization OK]
    Q3 -->|Pharma| R6[Multi step Chromatography Mandatory]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When a question asks "draw a flow diagram," always label the inlets, outlets, and process conditions (temperature, pH) on the arrows. This single habit often fetches 1–2 extra marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
Define the terms *Upstream Processing* and *Downstream Processing*. State any two objectives of each.

**Model Answer:**

**Upstream Processing (USP):** Upstream processing refers to the series of operations carried out **before** the actual fermentation step. It includes isolation and improvement of the producing microorganism, preparation of inoculum, formulation and sterilization of the production medium, and the aseptic preparation of the bioreactor.

**Objectives of USP:**
1. To develop a genetically stable, high-yielding microbial strain.
2. To provide a sterile, well-defined, and optimized nutritional and physical environment to the producing organism.

**Downstream Processing (DSP):** Downstream processing refers to the series of operations carried out **after** the fermentation step, aimed at recovery, purification, concentration, and formulation of the target product from the fermented broth.

**Objectives of DSP:**
1. To recover the product from the dilute broth with minimum loss and maximum purity.
2. To convert the purified product into a stable, market-ready form (powder, tablet, syrup, etc.).

*(Total: 3 marks — 1 mark each definition + 1 mark for objectives)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
Differentiate between *intracellular* and *extracellular* products. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Intracellular Product | Extracellular Product |
|---------|-----------------------|------------------------|
| Location | Accumulated **inside** the cell | **Secreted into** the broth |
| Recovery needs | Cell disruption mandatory | Direct broth separation sufficient |
| Downstream cost | Higher | Lower |
| Example | Insulin from *E. coli* (inclusion bodies) | Penicillin from *Penicillium chrysogenum* |
| Example 2 | Interferon from recombinant yeast | α-amylase from *Bacillus subtilis* |

*(Total: 3 marks — comparison table with one example)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **complete upstream processing steps** involved in the industrial production of penicillin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain any **four major stages of downstream processing** with the principle behind each operation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) USP of Penicillin

The industrial production of penicillin by *Penicillium chrysogenum* involves the following USP stages:

1. **Strain Development:** Classical mutation (UV, NTG) and modern recombinant DNA techniques are used to develop high-yielding strains. Productivity has been improved from < 0.01 g/L to over **50 g/L** over decades.

2. **Inoculum Build-up:** Spores from master culture → inoculum flasks → seed fermenter → production fermenter (working volume up to 200,000 L).

3. **Media Formulation:** Defined medium containing corn-steep liquor (nitrogen), lactose or glucose (carbon in fed-batch), phenylacetic acid (precursor for the penicillin side-chain), minerals, and antifoam.

4. **Sterilization:** Continuous sterilization of medium at **130 °C for 30–60 s** is preferred over batch autoclaving at this scale.

5. **Bioreactor Preparation:** Cleaning-in-place (CIP) and steam-in-place (SIP) ensure sterility; pH, DO, and temperature probes are calibrated.

6. **Fermentation Conditions:** Fed-batch mode at **25–27 °C**, **pH 6.5–7.0**, **high aeration** (since *P. chrysogenum* is strictly aerobic), and controlled agitation.

*(Block diagram must be drawn showing strain → inoculum → media → sterilization → bioreactor → broth.)*

#### Model Solution — (b) Four Major DSP Stages

1. **Filtration / Centrifugation (Solid–Liquid Separation):** Principle — density difference (centrifugation) or size exclusion (microfiltration). Removes mycelial biomass from the broth.

2. **Solvent Extraction:** Principle — differential solubility of penicillin in organic solvent (e.g., amyl acetate, butyl acetate) at acidic pH. Penicillin transfers to the organic phase.

3. **Precipitation / Crystallization:** Principle — solubility change with pH, temperature, or anti-solvent addition. Penicillin is precipitated as its potassium or sodium salt.

4. **Drying and Formulation:** Principle — sublimation of water under vacuum (lyophilization) or atomization (spray drying). Final product is sterile, water-free, and stable.

*(Each stage explanation: ~1.75 marks; total 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** What is a **bioreactor**? List the **major types** of bioreactors used in industry and describe the **Stirred-Tank Reactor (STR)** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fermentation process yields **60 g/L** of an antibiotic at the end of **96 hours**. The downstream processing chain has four steps with recoveries **88 %, 92 %, 95 %, and 90 %** respectively. Calculate the **(i)** volumetric productivity and **(ii)** overall downstream recovery percentage. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Bioreactor and STR

A **bioreactor** is a closed, aseptic vessel in which biological reactions are carried out under controlled environmental conditions of temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, agitation, and foam.

**Major types of bioreactors:**
- Stirred-Tank Reactor (STR)
- Airlift / Bubble Column Reactor
- Packed-Bed Reactor
- Fluidized-Bed Reactor
- Membrane Bioreactor
- Photobioreactor

**Stirred-Tank Reactor (STR):**
- **Construction:** Cylindrical vessel with aspect ratio ~ 1:2; dished top and bottom; equipped with baffles to prevent vortex formation.
- **Impeller:** Rushton turbine (radial flow) or marine propeller (axial flow) on a central shaft; multiple impellers in tall vessels.
- **Aeration:** Compressed sterile air sparged through a ring sparger at the bottom; dissolved oxygen (DO) controlled by agitation speed and air-flow rate.
- **Probes/Instruments:** pH probe, DO probe, temperature probe, foam sensor, and sampling port.
- **Modes of Operation:** Batch, fed-batch, and continuous (chemostat).
- **Applications:** Antibiotics, enzymes, recombinant proteins, amino acids, biofuels — most common industrial reactor.

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical Problem

**(i) Volumetric Productivity:**

$$Q_P = \dfrac{P}{t} = \dfrac{60\,\text{g/L}}{96\,\text{h}}$$

$$\boxed{Q_P = 0.625\,\text{g/(L·h)}}$$

**(ii) Overall DSP Recovery:**

$$\eta_{total} = 0.88 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.90$$

Step-by-step:
- $0.88 \times 0.92 = 0.8096$
- $0.8096 \times 0.95 = 0.76912$
- $0.76912 \times 0.90 = 0.69221$

$$\boxed{\eta_{total} = 69.22\,\%}$$

*(Each sub-part: ~3.5 marks.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark USP/DSP questions. Examiners allocate **1.5–2 marks** for a correctly labelled diagram; missing it is a guaranteed penalty.
> 2. **Writing "sterilization" without conditions.** Always specify temperature, pressure, and time (e.g., *121 °C, 15 psi, 15 min*).
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical problems.** A correct number without units loses ½–1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing "primary" and "secondary" metabolites.** Trophophase = growth-associated (primary); Idiophase = non-growth-associated (secondary). Mixing these is a frequent error.
> 5. **In DSP answers, omitting the *principle* behind each unit operation** (e.g., for centrifugation: *density difference*; for chromatography: *differential partition*). Examiners specifically look for the principle word.
> 6. **Using vague terms like "purification" or "treatment"** instead of naming the exact technique (e.g., *ion-exchange chromatography*, *ultrafiltration*).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bioprocess = USP + Bioreactor (Fermentation) + DSP.** All three are non-negotiable.
- **USP stages (in order):** Strain → Inoculum → Media → Sterilization → Bioreactor prep → Inoculation.
- **Standard autoclave sterilization:** **121 °C, 15 psi, 15–20 min** (memorize this triplet).
- **Continuous sterilization (industrial):** **130 °C, 30–120 s.**
- **Inoculum size rule:** Typically **5–10 %** of working volume.
- **DSP stages (in order):** Separation → (Disruption if intracellular) → Concentration → Capture → Polishing → Formulation.
- **DSP cost share:** **50–80 %** of total manufacturing cost.
- **Overall DSP recovery** is **multiplicative**, not additive — every step's loss compounds.
- **Volumetric productivity** $Q_P = P/t$ with units **g/(L·h)** — frequently tested numerically.
- **Product yield** $Y_{P/S}$ = grams of product per gram of substrate consumed.
- **Two examples you must know cold:**
  - *Penicillin* = secondary metabolite, **extracellular**, produced in fed-batch.
  - *Insulin* = recombinant protein, **intracellular** (inclusion bodies in *E. coli*), needs refolding.
- **Always include a labelled block diagram** in any 7-mark question about USP, DSP, or a specific industrial process.
- **Memorize the formula table** in Section 2.4 — at least two of these formulas appear in every KTU exam paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Flow Sheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT414 - INDUSTRIAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Flow Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Flow Sheet (PFS):** A Process Flow Sheet, also known as a **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)**, is a standardized engineering schematic that graphically represents the complete sequence of **unit operations** and **unit processes** involved in converting raw biological/chemical feedstocks into finished industrial biotechnology products. It integrates **material streams, energy flows, equipment interconnections, operating conditions, and mass balance data** into a single, unified engineering document.

In the context of **Industrial Biotechnology (PEPOT414)**, a process flow sheet encapsulates the entire **bioprocess train**, from **upstream processing (USP)** through **bioreaction (fermentation)** to **downstream processing (DSP)**, complying with **ISA (Instrumentation Society of America) S5.1** and **ISO 14617** symbology standards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Factory's Recipe Roadmap"**

Think of a Process Flow Sheet as the **architectural blueprint of a biological kitchen**. Imagine you are running a massive bakery:

- The **raw material store** is your flour/grain warehouse (analogous to substrate and media storage).
- The **media preparation tank** is where you mix and cook the dough (analogous to nutrient medium formulation).
- The **sterilizer** is the oven that kills any harmful germs in the dough (analogous to autoclaving/continuous sterilization).
- The **fermenter/bioreactor** is the proofing chamber where yeast does its magic — this is where the **biotransformation** happens.
- The **centrifuge** is the sieve separating the bread from raw dough (analogous to cell harvesting/clarification).
- The **chromatography column** is the quality-control inspector that picks only the perfectly baked loaves (analogous to product purification).
- The **dryer and packaging unit** is the final wrapping and labeling station (analogous to product formulation and packaging).

A **Process Flow Sheet** is essentially this entire bakery's **flow diagram** drawn on paper — showing every pipe, valve, tank, sensor, and material stream in standardized symbols.

### 1.3 Classification of Process Flow Sheets

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Hierarchical Levels of Flow Diagrams:**

| Level | Diagram Type | Information Density | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** | Low — major process zones only | Feasibility studies, preliminary design |
| **Level 2** | **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** | Medium — equipment, streams, mass/energy balance | Detailed engineering, board examination |
| **Level 3** | **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** | High — every pipe, valve, instrument, control loop | Construction, commissioning, plant operation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** The Module 1 syllabus focuses primarily on **Level 1 and Level 2** flow sheets — students must master the **block-level representation** of a generic industrial biotechnology process and identify **every major unit operation** in the USP-Bioreactor-DSP triad.

### 1.4 Core Components of an Industrial Biotechnology Process Flow Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Elements of a Valid Process Flow Sheet:**
> 1. **Process equipment** (drawn as standardized geometric symbols — circles, rectangles, or specific pictograms).
> 2. **Process streams** (arrows showing direction of material flow; labeled with composition, flow rate, temperature, pressure).
> 3. **Stream connections** (piping lines joining unit operations).
> 4. **Operating conditions** (annotated on streams: °C, bar, kg/h, mol/L).
> 5. **Utility streams** (water, steam, cooling water, instrument air — shown as auxiliary lines).
> 6. **Major control loops** (temperature, pH, DO, agitation — shown with ISA tag conventions).
> 7. **Title block, legend, and revision history** (engineering document control).
> 8. **Mass balance summary table** (typically in the top-right or bottom panel).

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metrics Used in PFS Documentation:**
> - **Volumetric flow rate (Q):** measured in **m³/h** or **L/min**
> - **Mass flow rate (ṁ):** measured in **kg/h** or **kg/s**
> - **Operating temperature (T):** measured in **°C** or **K**
> - **Operating pressure (P):** measured in **bar (g)** or **Pa**
> - **Concentration (C):** measured in **g/L** or **kg/m³**
> - **Reynolds number threshold** for turbulent flow: **Re > 4000** in process piping
> - **Steam sterilization standard:** **121.1 °C at 1.05 bar (g)** for **15 minutes** (F₀ ≥ 8)
> - **CIP (Clean-in-Place) standard:** **80 °C, 1–2% NaOH, 30 min circulation**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Generic Industrial Biotechnology Flow Sheet

A process flow sheet in industrial biotechnology is conventionally divided into **three functional zones**. Each zone performs a distinct class of operations.

#### 2.1.1 Upstream Processing (USP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Upstream Processing (USP):** All operations that occur *before* the actual biological reaction, focused on preparing a **sterile, homogeneous, nutritionally optimized** environment for the microorganism or cell culture.

**Unit Operations in USP (logical sequence):**

1. **Raw Material Storage & Handling**
   - Carbon source (molasses, glucose, starch hydrolysate)
   - Nitrogen source (soybean meal, yeast extract, ammonia)
   - Minerals, vitamins, precursors
   - pH adjusters (H₂SO₄, NaOH, NH₄OH)
   - Antifoam agents (silicone-based polyols)

2. **Media Preparation**
   - Dissolution of solids in deionized/RO water
   - pH adjustment
   - Trace element addition
   - Held in a **formulation/cook tank** at controlled temperature

3. **Media Sterilization** *(critical control point)*
   - **Batch sterilization** — entire batch in a steam-jacketed vessel at **121 °C for 15–30 min**.
   - **Continuous sterilization** — through a **plate heat exchanger** with holding loop (HTST: High-Temperature Short-Time, typically **140 °C for 30–120 s**).
   - **Why it matters:** Sterilization kills contaminating microbes that would otherwise outcompete the production strain and devastate product yield.

4. **Cooling and Transfer**
   - Sterilized media cooled to fermentation temperature (**30 °C for mesophiles, 37 °C for many bacteria**).
   - Transferred via **aseptic piping** to the fermenter.

#### 2.1.2 Bioreaction (Fermentation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bioreaction Section:** The heart of the process where the **biological transformation** occurs. The bioreactor/fermenter is the **central unit** of the flow sheet.

**Key Features of the Bioreactor Block:**

- **Input streams:** Sterile media, sterile air (for aerobic processes), pH control agents, antifoam, inoculum.
- **Output streams:** Broth (cells + spent medium + product), exhaust gas (CO₂-rich, O₂-depleted off-gas).
- **Internal instrumentation:** Temperature probe, pH probe, dissolved oxygen (DO) probe, foam sensor, agitator motor, sparger.
- **Typical bioreactor types in industry:**
  - **Stirred-tank reactor (STR / CSTR)** — most common (Cylindroconical for yeast, Rushton-type impellers)
  - **Air-lift reactor** — used for shear-sensitive cells (e.g., plant cell cultures)
  - **Bubble column reactor** — used for low-viscosity fermentations
  - **Packed-bed reactor** — used for immobilized enzyme/cell systems

**Critical Operating Parameters (annotated on flow sheet):**

- Working volume: **70–80% of total volume** (headspace for foam control)
- Aeration rate: **0.5–2.0 vvm** (volume of air per volume of medium per minute)
- Agitation speed: **100–500 rpm** depending on impeller and scale
- Temperature: **25–37 °C** (mesophilic range)
- pH: **5.0–7.5** depending on organism
- DO: maintained **> 30% saturation** for aerobic cultures

#### 2.1.3 Downstream Processing (DSP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Downstream Processing (DSP):** All operations that occur *after* fermentation, focused on **recovering, concentrating, purifying, and formulating** the target product.

**Unit Operations in DSP (typical sequence):**

1. **Cell Separation / Harvesting**
   - **Centrifugation** (disc-stack, tubular bowl) — most common for yeast and bacteria
   - **Microfiltration / Tangential Flow Filtration (TFF)** — used for smaller cells or shear-sensitive bioproducts
   - **Flocculation + Sedimentation** — used in beer/wine industries

2. **Cell Disruption** *(only for intracellular products)*
   - **High-pressure homogenization** (800–1500 bar)
   - **Bead milling** (glass/zirconia beads)
   - **Enzymatic lysis** (lysozyme for bacteria)
   - **Ultrasonication** (lab-scale)

3. **Primary Recovery / Concentration**
   - **Ultrafiltration** (membrane-based; molecular weight cut-off 1–1000 kDa)
   - **Precipitation** (ammonium sulfate, isoelectric point precipitation)
   - **Liquid-liquid extraction** (using organic solvents or aqueous two-phase systems)

4. **Purification**
   - **Chromatography** — ion exchange, affinity, gel filtration, hydrophobic interaction
   - **Crystallization** (for amino acids, citric acid, antibiotics)
   - **Electrodialysis** (for charged molecules like amino acids, organic acids)

5. **Polishing & Formulation**
   - **Drying** — spray drying, freeze drying, vacuum tray drying
   - **Granulation, blending, packaging**

6. **Effluent Treatment**
   - **Wastewater treatment plant** (biological + physico-chemical)
   - **Spent medium recycling** (water reuse)

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Process Flow Sheet Mathematics)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **core mathematical tools** for annotating a process flow sheet. Every mass balance annotation on a PFD traces back to one of these fundamental equations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $dm/dt = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{gen} - \dot{m}_{cons}$ | General mass balance (unsteady state) | kg/h; basis for all flow sheet calculations |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$ (at steady state) | Steady-state overall mass balance | Simplification for continuous processes |
| 3 | $d(m \cdot x_i)/dt = \dot{m}_{in} x_{i,in} - \dot{m}_{out} x_{i,out} + r_i V$ | Component mass balance | kg component/h; $r_i$ = rate of formation |
| 4 | $Y_{P/S} = \Delta P / \Delta S$ | Product yield on substrate | kg product / kg substrate |
| 5 | $Y_{X/S} = \Delta X / \Delta S$ | Biomass yield on substrate | kg cells / kg substrate |
| 6 | $Y_{P/X} = \Delta P / \Delta X$ | Product yield on biomass | kg product / kg cells |
| 7 | $P_v = C_P / t$ | Volumetric productivity | g/(L·h) |
| 8 | $D = F / V$ | Dilution rate (chemostat) | h⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot S / (K_s + S)$ | Monod growth kinetics | h⁻¹ |
| 10 | $Q_{O_2} = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$ | Oxygen transfer rate | mmol O₂/(L·h) |
| 11 | $N_{Re} = \rho v D / \mu$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless; flow regime in pipes |
| 12 | $\alpha = \dot{m}_{purge} / \dot{m}_{recycle}$ | Purge-to-recycle ratio | controls buildup in recycle loop |
| 13 | $\eta = m_{recovered} / m_{produced}$ | Recovery efficiency of DSP | dimensionless (0 < η ≤ 1) |
| 14 | $F_0 = \Delta t \cdot 10^{(T-121.1)/z}$ | Sterilization F-value (z=10 °C) | min; sterilization equivalence |
| 15 | $\Delta H_{sterilization} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat duty for sterilization | kW or kJ/h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Avoidance Rule for Exam Scripts:** When writing **component mass balances** in steady-state continuous bioprocesses, students *must* remember the **generation term** ($r_i V$) for biological components — this is the only point where a bioprocess mass balance differs from a chemical engineering one.

### 2.3 Process Flow Sheet Engineering Conventions

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Symbol Conventions (as per KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus):**

1. **Feed/Product streams** → solid bold arrows
2. **Utility streams** (steam, water, air) → dashed or dotted lines
3. **Instrument signals** → thin lines with circles (ISA tag bubbles)
4. **Major equipment** → drawn to scale; tagged with unique alphanumeric code (e.g., **R-101** for Reactor 1, **T-201** for Tank 2, **P-301** for Pump 3, **E-401** for Heat Exchanger 4, **S-501** for Separator 5)
5. **Direction of flow** → arrowheads on every stream
6. **Stream numbering** → sequential (Stream 1, 2, 3...) with composition and conditions listed in a stream table

### 2.4 Real-World Industrial Biotechnology Flow Sheet Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canonical Examples Studied in KTU Module 1:**

1. **Penicillin Production Flow Sheet (Batch/CSTR):**
   - USP: Corn-steep liquor + lactose + minerals → batch sterilization (121 °C)
   - Bioreaction: *Penicillium chrysogenum* in CSTR (25 °C, pH 6.5, 4–6 days, fed-batch)
   - DSP: Filtration → extraction (butyl acetate) → crystallization → drying → packaging

2. **Ethanol Production (Continuous):**
   - USP: Molasses dilution + sterilization
   - Bioreaction: *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* in CSTR cascade (30 °C, pH 4.5)
   - DSP: Distillation column → molecular sieve dehydration → denaturation

3. **Citric Acid Production (Aerobic):**
   - USP: Glucose syrup + mineral salts
   - Bioreaction: *Aspergillus niger* in STR (30 °C, pH 2.0, high aeration)
   - DSP: Filtration → precipitation (Ca(OH)₂) → acidulation (H₂SO₄) → ion exchange → crystallization

4. **Single-Cell Protein (SCP) from Methanol:**
   - USP: Methanol + ammonia + minerals + water
   - Bioreaction: *Methylophilus methylotrophus* in air-lift reactor (37 °C, pH 7.0, continuous)
   - DSP: Centrifugation → washing → spray drying → packaging

5. **Monoclonal Antibody (Modern Biopharma):**
   - USP: Complex chemically-defined media, sterile filtration (0.1 µm)
   - Bioreaction: CHO cells in STR (37 °C, pH 7.0, DO 50%, fed-batch 14 days)
   - DSP: Centrifugation → Protein A affinity chromatography → polishing IEX → viral filtration → formulation

### 2.5 Why Process Flow Sheets Matter in Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of a PFS:**
> - **Design stage:** Equipment sizing, pipe sizing, utility sizing
> - **Economic evaluation:** Capital cost (CAPEX) and operating cost (OPEX) estimation
> - **Safety analysis:** HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) and PHA (Process Hazard Analysis) studies
> - **Regulatory submission:** FDA, EMA, cGMP documentation
> - **Operator training:** Plant operator onboarding
> - **Process troubleshooting:** Bottleneck identification during scale-up
> - **Environmental impact assessment:** Effluent stream identification
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Mass Balance on a Continuous Bioreactor

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose:** This derivation demonstrates how the **overall mass balance** is constructed around a generic bioreactor block in a continuous-flow process flow sheet. This is the foundational calculation that appears on every PFD.

**Step 1: Define the Control Volume**

Let the bioreactor be the **control volume (CV)**. Mass enters through the sterile feed stream and exits through the broth outlet stream.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass balance equation (general, unsteady state):} \\
\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} &= \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} + \sum \dot{m}_{generated} - \sum \dot{m}_{consumed}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply to an Industrial Bioreactor**

For a typical aerobic bioreactor, the inputs and outputs are:

- **Input streams:** Sterile liquid feed ($\dot{m}_{L,in}$), sterile air for aeration ($\dot{m}_{air,in}$), acid/base for pH control ($\dot{m}_{pH}$), antifoam ($\dot{m}_{AF}$)
- **Output streams:** Fermentation broth ($\dot{m}_{broth,out}$), exhaust gas ($\dot{m}_{exhaust,out}$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} &= (\dot{m}_{L,in} + \dot{m}_{air,in} + \dot{m}_{pH} + \dot{m}_{AF}) \\
&\quad - (\dot{m}_{broth,out} + \dot{m}_{exhaust,out}) \\
&\quad + \dot{m}_{O_2,transferred} - \dot{m}_{CO_2,vented}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Steady-State Assumption**

For a continuous, well-instrumented industrial bioreactor operated at steady state, the **accumulation term is zero**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} &= 0 \\
\therefore 0 &= \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Component Balance on Substrate (Glucose)**

The **substrate** is *consumed* by the cells; therefore the generation term is zero and the consumption term is non-zero.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= \dot{m}_{L,in} \cdot S_{in} - \dot{m}_{broth,out} \cdot S_{out} - r_S \cdot V \\
\text{where } r_S &= \frac{\mu \cdot X}{Y_{X/S}} \text{ (substrate consumption rate)} \\
\text{Solving for } S_{out}: \\
S_{out} &= S_{in} - \frac{r_S \cdot V}{\dot{m}_{broth,out}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is the equation that lets an engineer annotate the **substrate concentration on the outlet stream** of the bioreactor block on the PFD. This is the most common calculation a KTU examiner asks for.

**Step 5: Component Balance on Biomass (Cells)**

Biomass is *generated* by cell growth; therefore, the generation term is non-zero.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= \dot{m}_{L,in} \cdot X_{in} - \dot{m}_{broth,out} \cdot X_{out} + r_X \cdot V \\
\text{where } r_X &= \mu \cdot X \text{ (biomass growth rate)} \\
\text{For a sterile feed (X_{in} = 0):} \\
X_{out} &= \frac{\mu \cdot X \cdot V}{\dot{m}_{broth,out}} = \frac{\mu \cdot X}{D} \\
\text{where } D &= \frac{\dot{m}_{broth,out}}{\rho \cdot V} = \frac{F}{V} \text{ (dilution rate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: The Chemostat Steady-State Identity**

At steady state in a chemostat, $\mu = D$ — a beautiful and critically important result.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\mu = D = \frac{F}{V}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This identity is the cornerstone of continuous bioprocess design.** It links microbial physiology (μ) directly to an engineering-operable parameter (D = F/V). A KTU board examiner can ask: *"Derive the relationship between dilution rate and specific growth rate in a chemostat"* — and this is the expected answer.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Balance on a Downstream Separator with a Recycle Stream

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose:** To demonstrate how a **recycle loop** is mathematically annotated on a process flow sheet, with proper attention to the **purge stream** that prevents indefinite buildup of inert components.

**Step 1: Identify the Recycle System Components**

Consider a centrifuge (S-101) that separates cells (heavy phase) from supernatant (light phase). The supernatant goes forward to the next DSP step, but a fraction of it is **recycled** back to the fermenter (e.g., to recover unused substrate). To prevent accumulation of non-volatile byproducts in the recycle loop, a **purge stream** is taken.

**Step 2: Define Stream Variables**

- $\dot{m}_1$ = feed to separator (from fermenter)
- $\dot{m}_2$ = heavy phase (cells, to cell disruption)
- $\dot{m}_3$ = light phase (supernatant, forward to next step)
- $\dot{m}_4$ = recycle stream (back to fermenter)
- $\dot{m}_5$ = purge stream (to waste)

By definition: $\dot{m}_3 = \dot{m}_4 + \dot{m}_5$

**Step 3: Mass Balance Around the Separator**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_1 &= \dot{m}_2 + \dot{m}_3 \\
\dot{m}_1 \cdot x_{i,1} &= \dot{m}_2 \cdot x_{i,2} + \dot{m}_3 \cdot x_{i,3} \quad \text{(component i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Mass Balance on the Recycle Junction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{4,in} + \dot{m}_{3,forward} &= \dot{m}_{forward,out} + \dot{m}_{recycle,out} \\
\dot{m}_3 &= \dot{m}_4 + \dot{m}_5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Component Balance on the Inert Accumulating Species**

For a non-biodegradable byproduct (e.g., a salt or a metabolic byproduct) at steady state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= \dot{m}_{recycle} \cdot x_{i,R} - \dot{m}_{purge} \cdot x_{i,P} \\
x_{i,P} &= x_{i,R} \text{ (well-mixed purge)} \\
\therefore \dot{m}_{purge} \cdot x_{i,P} &= \dot{m}_{recycle} \cdot x_{i,R} \\
\dot{m}_{purge} &= \dot{m}_{recycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this is true only if the purge takes the same concentration as the recycle. More generally, if the purge fraction is $\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_5 &= \alpha \cdot \dot{m}_3 \\
\dot{m}_4 &= (1-\alpha) \cdot \dot{m}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: The Purge-to-Recycle Ratio**

Define the **purge ratio** (β) as the fraction of the recycle-stream flow diverted to waste to prevent accumulation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{\dot{m}_5}{\dot{m}_4} = \frac{\alpha}{(1-\alpha)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A small β means most of the supernatant is recycled (good for substrate recovery) but risks accumulation of byproducts. A large β means high waste but low byproduct accumulation. The design engineer must balance these trade-offs — a common **14-mark KTU problem** involves calculating the minimum purge to keep byproduct concentration below a threshold.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Overall Recovery and Yield Across the Full Flow Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose:** To derive the **overall product yield** across the entire USP → Bioreactor → DSP flow sheet, demonstrating how individual stage yields combine multiplicatively.

**Step 1: Define Stage Yields**

- $\eta_{USP}$ = fraction of input substrate successfully delivered to the bioreactor (i.e., not destroyed in sterilization). Typical value: **0.95–0.99**.
- $Y_{P/S}$ = product yield on substrate inside the bioreactor. Typical: **0.3–0.5 g/g** for many fermentations.
- $\eta_{DSP}$ = fractional recovery of product through the downstream train. Typical: **0.6–0.9** depending on number of steps.

**Step 2: Calculate Mass of Product at Each Stage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{S,input} &= \text{total substrate fed} \\
m_{S,reactor} &= \eta_{USP} \cdot m_{S,input} \\
m_{P,reactor} &= Y_{P/S} \cdot m_{S,reactor} = Y_{P/S} \cdot \eta_{USP} \cdot m_{S,input} \\
m_{P,final} &= \eta_{DSP} \cdot m_{P,reactor} = \eta_{DSP} \cdot Y_{P/S} \cdot \eta_{USP} \cdot m_{S,input}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Define Overall Plant Yield**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{Y_{overall} = \frac{m_{P,final}}{m_{S,input}} = \eta_{USP} \cdot Y_{P/S} \cdot \eta_{DSP}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example**

Suppose a plant processes **1000 kg** of glucose, with $\eta_{USP} = 0.97$, $Y_{P/S} = 0.40$, $\eta_{DSP} = 0.80$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{S,reactor} &= 0.97 \times 1000 = 970 \text{ kg} \\
m_{P,reactor} &= 0.40 \times 970 = 388 \text{ kg} \\
m_{P,final} &= 0.80 \times 388 = 310.4 \text{ kg} \\
Y_{overall} &= 0.97 \times 0.40 \times 0.80 = 0.3104 \text{ kg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice how the yields **multiply**, not add. Three small losses (3%, 60%, 20%) cascade into a 69% overall recovery — a critical concept for process economics and the "holistic view" of a flow sheet.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stream-by-Stream Composition of a Generic Bioprocess Flow Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive Stream Table** — this is the **typical format** students are expected to draw in KTU 2024 ESE answers when asked to construct a process flow sheet.

| Stream No. | Stream Name | From → To | Phase | Flow Rate (kg/h) | Temp (°C) | Pressure (bar) | Key Components |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Carbon source | Storage → Media prep | Liquid | 500.0 | 25 | 1.0 | Glucose syrup (50% w/w) |
| **2** | Nitrogen source | Storage → Media prep | Liquid | 100.0 | 25 | 1.0 | Yeast extract (40% w/w) |
| **3** | Process water | RO unit → Media prep | Liquid | 8000.0 | 25 | 2.5 | Deionized H₂O |
| **4** | Sterile media | Sterilizer → Fermenter | Liquid | 8500.0 | 30 | 1.2 | Complete growth medium (sterile) |
| **5** | Sterile air | Compressor → Fermenter | Gas | 1500.0 | 30 | 2.0 | Filtered air (0.2 µm) |
| **6** | Inoculum | Seed train → Fermenter | Liquid | 50.0 | 30 | 1.0 | Production strain (OD ≈ 1.0) |
| **7** | Acid (pH) | Storage → Fermenter | Liquid | 5.0 | 25 | 1.5 | 10% H₂SO₄ |
| **8** | Base (pH) | Storage → Fermenter | Liquid | 5.0 | 25 | 1.5 | 10% NaOH |
| **9** | Antifoam | Storage → Fermenter | Liquid | 2.0 | 25 | 1.5 | Silicone emulsion |
| **10** | Fermentation broth | Fermenter → Centrifuge | Liquid + Cells | 9500.0 | 30 | 1.0 | Cells (30 g/L) + product (5 g/L) + spent medium |
| **11** | Exhaust gas | Fermenter → Vent | Gas | 1500.0 | 30 | 1.0 | O₂-depleted air + CO₂ + water vapor |
| **12** | Cell paste | Centrifuge → Cell disruption | Solid-Liquid | 300.0 | 30 | 1.0 | Concentrated cells (40% w/w) |
| **13** | Clarified supernatant | Centrifuge → Capture step | Liquid | 9200.0 | 30 | 1.0 | Spent medium + extracellular product |
| **14** | Crude product | Capture → Purification | Liquid | 500.0 | 25 | 1.5 | Partially purified product |
| **15** | Pure product | Purification → Formulation | Liquid | 100.0 | 25 | 1.0 | Purified product (95% purity) |
