| **16** | Final product | Drying → Packaging | Solid | 45.0 | 25 | 1.0 | Dried, formulated product |
| **17** | Wastewater | Centrifuge → ETP | Liquid | 9000.0 | 30 | 1.0 | Spent medium, salts, organics |
| **18** | Cell debris | Cell disruption → Waste | Solid-Liquid | 320.0 | 35 | 1.0 | Disrupted cell mass |
| **19** | Steam | Boiler → Sterilizer | Vapor | 800.0 | 165 | 4.0 | Saturated steam (utility) |
| **20** | Cooling water | Cooling tower → Sterilizer | Liquid | 12000.0 | 25 | 3.0 | CW supply (utility) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mass Balance Check (overall):** Sum of all inputs (Streams 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9) = Sum of all outputs (Streams 11, 16, 17, 18) + Accumulated in process. The student should verify conservation in any custom-designed flow sheet.

### 4.2 Instrumentation and Control Loops (ISA Tag Convention)

> [!NOTE]
> A **Level 3 P&ID** (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram) supplements the PFD with detailed instrumentation. The following block diagram shows the **major control loops** that a designer must include on a complete process flow sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIO[Bioreactor R-401 with Control Loops]
        TT["TT-401<br/>Temperature Transmitter"]
        TIC["TIC-401<br/>Temperature<br/>Indicator Controller"]
        PT["PT-402<br/>Pressure Transmitter"]
        PIC["PIC-402<br/>Pressure<br/>Indicator Controller"]
        pHT["AT-403<br/>pH Transmitter"]
        pHIC["AIC-403<br/>pH<br/>Indicator Controller"]
        DOT["AT-404<br/>Dissolved Oxygen<br/>Transmitter"]
        DOIC["AIC-404<br/>DO<br/>Indicator Controller"]
        FT["FT-405<br/>Flow Transmitter<br/>Air Inlet"]
        FIC["FIC-405<br/>Flow<br/>Indicator Controller"]
        FT2["FT-406<br/>Flow Transmitter<br/>Broth Outlet"]
        LS["LSH-407<br/>Level Switch High<br/>Foam Sensor"]
    end

    TT --> TIC
    TIC -->|Cooling Water Valve| BIO
    PT --> PIC
    PIC -->|Exhaust Backpressure| BIO
    pHT --> pHIC
    pHIC -->|Acid Pump| BIO
    pHIC -->|Base Pump| BIO
    DOT --> DOIC
    DOIC -->|Agitator Speed| BIO
    DOIC -->|Air Flow Setpoint| FIC
    FT --> FIC
    FIC -->|Air Inlet Valve| BIO
    FT2 --> FT2
    LS -->|Antifoam Pump| BIO
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISA Tag Convention Decoded:** The first letter indicates the **measured variable** (T = Temperature, P = Pressure, A = Analytical, F = Flow, L = Level), the second letter is **Indicator**, the third is **Controller**, and the number is a **loop identifier**. This is the universal language of industrial biotechnology process flow sheets.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define a Process Flow Sheet. List the three hierarchical levels of process flow diagrams used in industrial biotechnology with one-line descriptions of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Process Flow Sheet (PFS) is a standardized engineering schematic that graphically represents the complete sequence of unit operations and unit processes involved in converting raw biological/chemical feedstocks into finished industrial biotechnology products, integrating material streams, energy flows, equipment, and mass balance data.

The three hierarchical levels are:

1. **Block Flow Diagram (BFD):** Shows only major process zones as labeled blocks; used in feasibility and preliminary design.
2. **Process Flow Diagram (PFD):** Shows all major equipment, process streams with composition, flow rate, temperature, and pressure; includes overall mass and energy balance; used for detailed engineering.
3. **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID):** Shows every pipe, valve, instrument, and control loop; used for construction, commissioning, and plant operation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*List the major unit operations in the Upstream Processing (USP) section of an industrial biotechnology process flow sheet, in their correct sequence.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major unit operations in USP, in correct sequence, are:

1. **Raw material storage and handling** (carbon source, nitrogen source, minerals, pH adjusters, antifoam).
2. **Media preparation** (dissolution, pH adjustment, trace element addition in the cook/formulation tank).
3. **Media sterilization** (batch at 121 °C/15 min OR continuous HTST at 140 °C/30 s).
4. **Cooling** (of sterilized media to fermentation temperature, ~30 °C).
5. **Aseptic transfer** (to the seed fermenter and subsequently to the main production fermenter).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Convention:** Students must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** (full 14 marks, no sub-choice within). Both are provided below as required alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]*

**(a) Draw a complete Process Flow Diagram (Level 2 PFD) for an industrial aerobic fermentation process producing an extracellular enzyme (e.g., amylase) using *Bacillus subtilis*. Label all major equipment (with ISA-equivalent tags), all input streams (substrate, water, air, inoculum, pH agents, antifoam), and all output streams (broth, exhaust gas, waste). Include a stream table showing flow rate, temperature, and pressure for at least 8 streams. (7 marks)**

**(b) For the same process, the fermenter operates in fed-batch mode. Given: total glucose fed = 2000 kg, sterilization loss = 3%, biomass yield $Y_{X/S}$ = 0.45 g/g, product yield $Y_{P/S}$ = 0.30 g/g, overall downstream recovery $\eta_{DSP}$ = 0.75. Calculate: (i) mass of substrate entering the fermenter, (ii) mass of cells produced, (iii) mass of crude product before DSP, (iv) mass of final pure enzyme product, and (v) overall plant yield $Y_{overall}$. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-A:**

**Part (a) — Process Flow Diagram (7 marks):**

The student should draw a Level 2 PFD with the following elements (each correctly identified/labelled = ~1 mark):

| Element | Equipment Tag | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Glucose storage tank | T-101 | Carbon source reservoir |
| Yeast extract tank | T-102 | Nitrogen source reservoir |
| Process water tank | T-103 | Deionized water reservoir |
| Media preparation tank | M-201 | Mixing and formulation |
| Continuous sterilizer (HTST) | E-301 | Sterilization at 140 °C |
| Holding tube | V-302 | 30-second residence |
| Sterile cooler | E-303 | Cool to 30 °C |
| Seed fermenter | F-401 | Inoculum development |
| Main fermenter (CSTR) | R-501 | Production bioreactor (50 m³) |
| Air compressor + sterile filter | C-601 | Sterile air supply |
| Acid/base/antifoam tanks | T-701/702/703 | pH and foam control |
| Disc-stack centrifuge | S-801 | Cell separation |
| Microfiltration unit | UF-901 | Clarification/concentration |
| Chromatography column | C-1001 | Purification |
| Spray dryer | D-1101 | Drying to powder |
| Packaging line | PK-1201 | Final packaging |
| ETP | ETP-1301 | Effluent treatment |

**Streams to label (8 minimum):** Glucose feed, nitrogen feed, water feed, sterile media to fermenter, sterile air inlet, broth outlet, cell paste, clarified enzyme solution, exhaust gas, wastewater.

**Stream table (sample):**

| Stream | From → To | kg/h | T (°C) | P (bar) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | T-101 → M-201 | 400 | 25 | 1.0 |
| 2 | T-102 → M-201 | 80 | 25 | 1.0 |
| 3 | T-103 → M-201 | 7500 | 25 | 2.5 |
| 4 | E-303 → R-501 | 7950 | 30 | 1.2 |
| 5 | C-601 → R-501 | 1200 | 30 | 2.0 |
| 6 | R-501 → S-801 | 8800 | 30 | 1.0 |
| 7 | S-801 → UF-901 | 8500 | 30 | 1.0 |
| 8 | R-501 → Vent | 1200 | 30 | 1.0 |

**Part (b) — Mass Balance Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $m_{S,input} = 2000$ kg glucose
- $\eta_{USP} = 1 - 0.03 = 0.97$ (3% sterilization loss)
- $Y_{X/S} = 0.45$ g cells/g glucose
- $Y_{P/S} = 0.30$ g product/g glucose
- $\eta_{DSP} = 0.75$

**(i) Mass of substrate entering the fermenter:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{S,reactor} &= \eta_{USP} \times m_{S,input} \\
&= 0.97 \times 2000 \\
m_{S,reactor} &= 1940 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mass of cells produced:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_X &= Y_{X/S} \times m_{S,reactor} \\
&= 0.45 \times 1940 \\
m_X &= 873 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Mass of crude product before DSP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{P,crude} &= Y_{P/S} \times m_{S,reactor} \\
&= 0.30 \times 1940 \\
m_{P,crude} &= 582 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Mass of final pure enzyme product:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{P,final} &= \eta_{DSP} \times m_{P,crude} \\
&= 0.75 \times 582 \\
m_{P,final} &= 436.5 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Overall plant yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{overall} &= \frac{m_{P,final}}{m_{S,input}} = \eta_{USP} \times Y_{P/S} \times \eta_{DSP} \\
&= 0.97 \times 0.30 \times 0.75 \\
Y_{overall} &= 0.21825 \text{ kg product/kg glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-A):** The most common mistake students make is **adding the yields instead of multiplying them** (e.g., writing 0.97 + 0.30 + 0.75 = 2.02). This is a **3-mark loss** for applying the wrong mathematical operation. Always remember: **stage yields multiply** in a series process.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a) With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the three major zones of an industrial biotechnology process flow sheet — Upstream Processing (USP), Bioreaction, and Downstream Processing (DSP). For each zone, list at least four unit operations and explain the function of each. (7 marks)**

**(b) A continuous stirred-tank bioreactor (CSTR) operates at steady state with feed flow rate F = 500 L/h, working volume V = 2500 L, sterile feed substrate concentration $S_{in}$ = 20 g/L, and outlet substrate concentration $S_{out}$ = 2 g/L. Biomass concentration in the outlet $X$ = 4.5 g/L. The yield coefficient $Y_{X/S}$ = 0.50 g/g. Calculate: (i) the dilution rate D, (ii) the specific growth rate μ, (iii) the volumetric productivity $P_v$ (if product concentration in outlet = 1.2 g/L), and (iv) verify the steady-state mass balance around the reactor. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-B:**

**Part (a) — Three Major Zones (7 marks):**

**1. Upstream Processing (USP)** — *All operations before fermentation*
- Raw material storage: Holds glucose, nitrogen sources, minerals, vitamins, antifoam, pH adjusters.
- Media preparation: Dissolution of nutrients in process water, pH adjustment, trace element addition.
- Media sterilization: Eliminates contaminant microbes via batch (121 °C/15 min) or continuous HTST (140 °C/30 s) methods.
- Cooling: Sterilized media cooled to mesophilic fermentation temperature (30 °C).
- Aseptic transfer: Sterile piping maintains integrity until media enters the fermenter.

**2. Bioreaction (Fermentation)** — *The biological transformation zone*
- Seed fermenter train: Sequential scale-up (lab → pilot → production inoculum) to generate sufficient biomass.
- Main production fermenter (CSTR/STR): Where the production organism carries out the desired bioconversion under controlled T, pH, DO, agitation.
- Aeration system: Sterile air supply through compressor, filter, and sparger; provides O₂ for aerobic metabolism.
- Agitation system: Rushton or pitched-blade impellers ensure mixing, mass transfer, and heat removal.
- Instrumentation: Probes for T, pH, DO, foam; control loops maintain setpoints.
- Exhaust gas handling: Off-gas (CO₂-rich, O₂-depleted) vented after biosafety filtration.

**3. Downstream Processing (DSP)** — *All operations after fermentation*
- Cell separation: Centrifugation or microfiltration to separate biomass from broth.
- Cell disruption: For intracellular products — homogenization, bead milling, or enzymatic lysis.
- Primary recovery: Ultrafiltration, precipitation, or liquid-liquid extraction to concentrate the product.
- Purification: Chromatography (ion exchange, affinity, gel filtration) to achieve target purity.
- Crystallization: For crystalline products (e.g., amino acids, antibiotics, citric acid).
- Drying: Spray drying, freeze drying, or vacuum drying to produce stable solid product.
- Formulation and packaging: Final blending, granulation, and packaging for market.

**Part (b) — CSTR Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given:** $F = 500$ L/h, $V = 2500$ L, $S_{in} = 20$ g/L, $S_{out} = 2$ g/L, $X = 4.5$ g/L, $Y_{X/S} = 0.50$ g/g, $P = 1.2$ g/L.

**(i) Dilution rate D:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{F}{V} = \frac{500}{2500} \\
D &= 0.20 \text{ h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Specific growth rate μ:**

At steady state in a chemostat, $\mu = D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\mu = 0.20 \text{ h}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Volumetric productivity $P_v$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_v &= \frac{P_{out}}{t} = \frac{C_P}{\tau} \\
\text{where } \tau &= \frac{V}{F} = \frac{2500}{500} = 5 \text{ h} \\
P_v &= \frac{1.2 \text{ g/L}}{5 \text{ h}} \\
P_v &= 0.24 \text{ g/(L·h)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Verification of steady-state mass balance:**

For substrate at steady state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Component balance: } 0 &= F \cdot S_{in} - F \cdot S_{out} - r_S \cdot V \\
r_S &= \frac{F(S_{in} - S_{out})}{V} = \frac{500 \times (20 - 2)}{2500} \\
r_S &= \frac{500 \times 18}{2500} = 3.6 \text{ g/(L·h)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification using $Y_{X/S}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_X &= Y_{X/S} \times r_S = 0.50 \times 3.6 = 1.8 \text{ g/(L·h)} \\
\text{Also, } r_X &= \mu \cdot X = 0.20 \times 4.5 = 0.9 \text{ g/(L·h)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **MISMATCH DETECTED!** The two computed values of $r_X$ (1.8 vs 0.9 g/(L·h)) **do not agree** — this indicates that the given parameters are internally inconsistent in this KTU-style question. Students should note this and discuss possible reasons: (a) maintenance metabolism, (b) product formation from substrate, (c) experimental error in one of the values, or (d) cells are not in pure exponential growth. **In a 14-mark answer, a student who identifies this inconsistency and provides a correct interpretation scores higher** than one who blindly forces agreement.

**Correct interpretation:** $r_X = \mu \cdot X = 0.9$ g/(L·h) is the **true** growth rate because it derives from the chemostat steady-state identity (μ = D). The 1.8 g/(L·h) from $Y_{X/S} \times r_S$ overestimates because it assumes **all** consumed substrate becomes biomass, ignoring maintenance energy and product formation. The "true" observed yield is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{X/S}^{obs} &= \frac{\mu \cdot X}{r_S} = \frac{0.9}{3.6} = 0.25 \text{ g/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is half the theoretical $Y_{X/S}$ of 0.50, confirming significant substrate diversion to maintenance and product.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Topic-Wide Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in Process Flow Sheet Questions (PEPOT414):**
>
> 1. **Confusing USP with DSP:** Students often swap the order — fermentation ALWAYS comes *between* USP and DSP, not after DSP.
> 2. **Missing utility streams:** A complete flow sheet must show steam, cooling water, and instrument air as utility lines; omitting them loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the inoculum stream:** The seed fermenter and its transfer to the main fermenter is a mandatory stream; many students omit it.
> 4. **Adding yields instead of multiplying them:** The single most common computational error in mass-balance questions.
> 5. **Confusing PFD with P&ID:** Don't draw every instrument and valve on a PFD — those go on the P&ID. PFDs show only major control indicators.
> 6. **Neglecting the chemostat identity (μ = D):** In continuous bioprocess problems, this is the **single most important identity** to invoke.
> 7. **Missing the purge stream in recycle loops:** Forgetting the purge is a common error in recycle mass-balance problems; it leads to non-physical accumulation of inert species.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Process Flow Sheet (PEPOT414 Module 1):**

- ✅ A **Process Flow Sheet (PFS)** is a graphical document showing the **complete sequence of unit operations** in a bioprocess.
- ✅ The **three hierarchical levels** are **BFD → PFD → P&ID**, with increasing information density.
- ✅ The **three functional zones** of any industrial biotech flow sheet are **USP → Bioreaction → DSP**.
- ✅ **USP unit operations:** Raw material storage → Media prep → Sterilization (121 °C batch or 140 °C HTST continuous) → Cooling → Aseptic transfer.
- ✅ **Bioreaction:** CSTR (most common), air-lift, bubble column, or packed-bed; controlled T, pH, DO, agitation.
- ✅ **DSP unit operations:** Cell separation (centrifugation/TFF) → Cell disruption (homogenizer) → Concentration (UF/precipitation) → Purification (chromatography) → Crystallization → Drying → Packaging.
- ✅ **Key equations to remember:**
  - Steady-state mass balance: $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$
  - Chemostat identity: $\mu = D = F/V$
  - Component balance: $0 = F S_{in} - F S_{out} - r_S V$
  - Overall plant yield: $Y_{overall} = \eta_{USP} \cdot Y_{P/S} \cdot \eta_{DSP}$ (**multiplicative, not additive!**)
  - Dilution rate: $D = F/V$ (units: h⁻¹)
  - Volumetric productivity: $P_v = C_P / \tau$ (units: g/(L·h))
  - F-value (sterilization): $F_0 = \Delta t \cdot 10^{(T - 121.1)/z}$ with $z = 10$ °C
- ✅ **ISA Tag Convention:** First letter = variable (T, P, A, F, L), middle letter = function (I = Indicator, C = Controller), number = loop ID.
- ✅ **Standard sterilization reference point:** 121.1 °C, 1.05 bar (g), 15 min (F₀ = 8 min).
- ✅ **Standard process metrics:** Aeration 0.5–2.0 vvm; working volume 70–80% of total; Reynolds number > 4000 for turbulent flow in pipes.
- ✅ **Always include** utility streams (steam, cooling water, instrument air) and effluent handling in a complete flow sheet.
- ✅ **Always verify** overall mass balance (sum of inputs = sum of outputs at steady state).
- ✅ **Purge streams** are mandatory in any recycle loop to prevent indefinite buildup of inert or toxic byproducts.

> **END OF NOTES — Process Flow Sheet (PEPOT414, Module 1)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT414 - INDUSTRIAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Block Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Block Diagrams in Industrial Biotechnology

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Biotechnology** and **Bioprocess Engineering**, a **Block Diagram** is a standardized schematic representation in which each major functional unit of a bioprocess is depicted as a labelled rectangular "block" connected by directional arrows that represent the flow of **materials** (feed streams, product streams, recycle streams) and **information** (control signals). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEPOT414, Module 1 treats the block diagram as the foundational visual grammar for representing a complete **bioprocess plant** — from the receipt of raw biomass to the dispatch of a purified product.

Mathematically, a block diagram can be abstracted as a **directed graph** $G = (V, E)$ where each vertex $v_i \in V$ represents a process block (e.g., a fermenter, centrifuge, chromatography column) and each directed edge $e_{ij} \in E$ represents a stream flowing from block $i$ to block $j$. The flow on any stream is characterized by:

$$
F_{ij} = \{(x_k, \dot{n}_k, T, P, \rho) \mid k = 1, 2, \dots, N_c\}
$$

where $x_k$ is the mass fraction of component $k$, $\dot{n}_k$ its molar flow rate, $T$ the stream temperature, $P$ the pressure, and $\rho$ the bulk density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A block diagram is **not** a Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID). It omits individual valves, instruments, and pipe specifications, retaining only the **functional skeleton** of the process. It is the first diagram an Industrial Biotechnologist draws before detailed engineering.

## 1.2 The Three-Canonical-Blocks Framework

Every industrial bioprocess, regardless of whether the product is a **recombinant protein**, a **biofuel**, an **organic acid**, or a **secondary metabolite**, can be decomposed into **three canonical blocks**:

| Block # | Canonical Name | Engineering Function | Typical Equipment |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **USP** | Upstream Processing | Raw-material preparation, sterilization, inoculum build-up | Media tanks, autoclaves, shake flasks, seed fermenters |
| **BR** | Bioreaction Block | Catalytic conversion of substrates into products via living cells/enzymes | Stirred-tank bioreactor, airlift, packed-bed, membrane bioreactor |
| **DSP** | Downstream Processing | Separation, concentration, and purification of the product | Centrifuges, filters, chromatographs, crystallizers, dryers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization Aid (KTU Examiner Favourite):** "**U**se **B**ugs **D**iligently" — **U**pstream → **B**ioreaction → **D**ownstream. This three-block model is the single most-tested concept in Module 1.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Restaurant" Model

Imagine you are running a restaurant. The **Block Diagram** of your restaurant has three rooms:

- **The Pantry (USP block):** You receive raw vegetables, wash them, chop them, and store them under refrigeration (sterile conditions). Nothing "happens" here chemically — you are only *preparing* the inputs.
- **The Kitchen (Bioreaction block):** A chef (the *microbe* or *enzyme*) takes the prepared ingredients and, using heat and time, transforms them into a cooked dish (the *product*). This is where the **value addition** occurs.
- **The Dining Hall (DSP block):** The dish is plated, garnished, and presented to the customer. The chef is now irrelevant — only the finished product matters. This corresponds to *separation, purification, and formulation*.

The block diagram of a bioprocess is the floor plan of this restaurant, drawn so that a regulator, an investor, or an engineer can understand the entire operation in a single glance.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Block Diagrams

The following engineering metrics are routinely annotated on bioprocess block diagrams:

- **Volumetric productivity:** $Q_P = \dfrac{r_P}{V}$ with units of **kg · m⁻³ · h⁻¹**
- **Aeration rate / VVM:** **0.1 – 2.0 vvm** (volume of air per volume of medium per minute)
- **Sterilization temperature:** **121 °C** at **15 psi** for **15 – 20 minutes**
- **Typical fermentation pH range:** **4.5 – 7.5**
- **Typical fermentation temperature:** **25 °C – 37 °C**
- **Standard inoculum size:** **5 % – 10 % (v/v)** of working volume

> [!TIP]
> These standard values are the *numerical vocabulary* of bioprocess block diagrams. Committing them to memory is essential for answering KTU Part A 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic of Block Decomposition

The KTU 2024 Scheme adopts an **input → process → output** logical flow. Each block in the diagram is characterized by the following five attributes:

1. **Input streams** — what enters the block (substrates, air, utilities).
2. **Internal state** — the conditions maintained inside (pH, T, dissolved oxygen, agitation).
3. **Transformation function** — what the block does (e.g., biological conversion, phase change, size exclusion).
4. **Output streams** — what leaves the block (product-rich broth, spent air, waste biomass).
5. **Control loops** — feedback signals (e.g., pH probe → controller → acid/base pump).

## 2.2 Why the Block-Diagram Abstraction is Powerful

The block-diagram approach offers three engineering advantages:

- **Modularity:** Each block can be designed, optimized, and scaled **independently** of the others. The yield of a chromatography column is improved without redesigning the fermenter.
- **Mass-balance tractability:** A block diagram allows the engineer to write a **steady-state mass balance** around each block, leading to a system of linear equations that is solvable by matrix inversion.
- **Capital-cost estimation:** Each block can be assigned a cost function, and the **total plant CAPEX** is the sum of individual block costs.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** the KTU 2024 scheme expects you to know for questions on Block Diagrams and bioprocess mass/energy balances. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | General Mass Balance (steady state) | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} \;=\; \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ | Accumulation = 0 at steady state | 3-mark / 7-mark derivations |
| 2 | Component Mass Balance | $\sum (\dot{m}_{in,i} \cdot x_{in,i}) \;=\; \sum (\dot{m}_{out,i} \cdot x_{out,i})$ | Tracks each species through a block | 7-mark derivations |
| 3 | Volumetric Productivity | $Q_P \;=\; \dfrac{r_P}{V}$ | Product per unit reactor volume per unit time | 3-mark numericals |
| 4 | Specific Growth Rate (Monod) | $\mu \;=\; \dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S}$ | Microbial growth kinetics inside BR block | 7-mark derivations |
| 5 | Biomass Doubling Time | $t_d \;=\; \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | Time for cell mass to double | 3-mark short questions |
| 6 | Dilution Rate (CSTR block) | $D \;=\; \dfrac{F}{V}$ | Inverse of residence time | 7-mark on chemostat block |
| 7 | Sterilization $F_0$ Value | $F_0 \;=\; \Delta t \cdot 10^{(T - 121)/\vert z \vert}$ | Equivalent time at 121 °C; $z$ = 10 °C | 3-mark USP questions |
| 8 | Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR) | $OTR \;=\; k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$ | Aeration design for BR block | 7-mark aeration |
| 9 | Overall Recovery Yield | $Y_{overall} \;=\; Y_{R/x} \cdot Y_{S/P} \cdot Y_{D/P}$ | Multiplies yields across all blocks | 14-mark full-plant problems |
| 10 | Resolved-block signal flow | $G(s) \;=\; \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \dfrac{G_1 G_2}{1 + G_1 H_1}$ | Block-diagram reduction rule for control blocks | 7-mark (instrumentation overlap) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **process-industry practice**, the block diagram is the first document generated during the **conceptual design phase** of a biotech plant. It serves as:

- The basis for **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)** and **P&IDs**.
- The communication interface between **process engineers**, **microbiologists**, and **regulatory affairs** officers.
- The skeleton for **techno-economic analysis (TEA)** — every block has a capital cost, an operating cost, and a revenue stream.

Industries where block-diagram literacy is non-negotiable include **vaccine manufacturing** (e.g., Bharat Biotech, Serum Institute of India), **enzymatic biofuel production** (e.g., Praj Industries), and **monoclonal antibody plants** (e.g., Biocon).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Mass Balance Around a Generic Block

A general bioprocess block accepts $n$ input streams and releases $m$ output streams. Let $\dot{m}_{in,k}$ and $x_{in,k,j}$ be the mass flow rate and mass fraction of component $j$ in input stream $k$, respectively. Similarly for output streams.

The **general mass balance** at steady state (no accumulation) is:

$$
\frac{dM_{sys}}{dt} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dot{m}_{in,k} \;-\; \sum_{l=1}^{m} \dot{m}_{out,l} \;=\; 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dot{m}_{in,k} \;=\; \sum_{l=1}^{m} \dot{m}_{out,l}
$$

For an individual **component $j$**:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dot{m}_{in,k} \cdot x_{in,k,j} \;-\; \sum_{l=1}^{m} \dot{m}_{out,l} \cdot x_{out,l,j} \;+\; r_j \cdot V \;=\; 0
$$

where $r_j$ is the volumetric rate of generation (or consumption, if negative) of species $j$ inside the block, and $V$ is the effective volume of the block.

> **Logic of the step:** The first two terms are the convective fluxes in and out. The third term, $r_j V$, accounts for *generation by reaction*. If the block is purely physical (e.g., a centrifuge, where $r_j = 0$), this term vanishes, leaving a simple input = output balance.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Mass Balance on the Bioreaction (BR) Block

A stirred-tank fermenter operates at steady state with the following streams:

- **Input stream (1):** Sterile medium, $\dot{m}_{in} = 1000 \; kg/h$, with substrate mass fraction $x_{S,in} = 0.05$ and negligible biomass.
- **Output stream (2):** Fermented broth, $\dot{m}_{out} = 1010 \; kg/h$, with $x_{S,out} = 0.01$ and $x_{X,out} = 0.02$.
- **Output stream (3):** Off-gas, $\dot{m}_{off} = 1.5 \; kg/h$, containing $0.04 \; kg$ of evaporated substrate per kg of off-gas.

The biomass yield on substrate is $Y_{X/S} = 0.5$ g biomass per g substrate consumed. Verify consistency.

### Step 1 — Overall mass balance

$$
\dot{m}_{in} \;=\; \dot{m}_{out} \;+\; \dot{m}_{off}
$$
$$
1000 \;=\; 1010 \;+\; 1.5 \;=\; 1011.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Imbalance} = 11.5 \; kg/h
$$

The imbalance of **11.5 kg/h** must equal the net mass generated by the reaction (mostly oxygen addition during aerobic growth plus water of metabolism). We accept it as a measurement closure error.

### Step 2 — Substrate balance

Substrate entering per hour:

$$
\dot{m}_{S,in} \;=\; 1000 \cdot 0.05 \;=\; 50 \; kg/h
$$

Substrate leaving in the broth:

$$
\dot{m}_{S,out,br} \;=\; 1010 \cdot 0.01 \;=\; 10.1 \; kg/h
$$

Substrate leaving in the off-gas:

$$
\dot{m}_{S,out,off} \;=\; 1.5 \cdot 0.04 \;=\; 0.06 \; kg/h
$$

Substrate consumed by reaction:

$$
\dot{m}_{S,consumed} \;=\; 50 \;-\; (10.1 + 0.06) \;=\; 39.84 \; kg/h
$$

### Step 3 — Predicted biomass production

$$
\dot{m}_{X,predicted} \;=\; Y_{X/S} \cdot \dot{m}_{S,consumed} \;=\; 0.5 \cdot 39.84 \;=\; 19.92 \; kg/h
$$

### Step 4 — Comparison with observed biomass in broth

$$
\dot{m}_{X,observed} \;=\; 1010 \cdot 0.02 \;=\; 20.20 \; kg/h
$$

### Step 5 — Percentage error

$$
\% \text{error} \;=\; \frac{\vert 20.20 - 19.92 \vert}{20.20} \cdot 100 \;=\; 1.39 \;\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** The mass balance closes to within 1.4 %, confirming that the **block-diagram flow rates are thermodynamically consistent**. This is the standard of accuracy expected in a KTU 14-mark full-plant problem.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Block Functions to Process Roles

| Block | Primary Input | Primary Output | Main Transformation | Key Operating Variable | Energy Input | Waste Stream |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **USP-1 Media Prep** | Water, sugars, salts | Sterile medium | Dissolution, pH adjustment | T = 25 °C, P = 1 atm | Steam (low) | None |
| **USP-2 Sterilizer** | Non-sterile medium | Sterile medium | Thermal deactivation of spores | T = 121 °C, t = 15 min | Steam (high) | Condensate |
| **USP-3 Inoculum** | Working culture | Seed culture | Cell multiplication in shake flasks | T = 30 °C, 200 rpm | Mechanical (orbital) | CO₂ + spent medium |
| **BR-1 Fermenter** | Sterile medium + seed | Broth + off-gas | Substrate → biomass + product | pH 6.8, DO 30 % | Air + agitation | Exhaust gas |
| **DSP-1 Centrifuge** | Fermentation broth | Cell paste + supernatant | Density-based separation | ω = 8000 rpm | Centrifugal | Cell waste |
| **DSP-2 Chromatography** | Clarified supernatant | Pure product fraction | Adsorption–desorption selectivity | Linear velocity, gradient | Pumps + buffer | Spent eluent |
| **DSP-3 Formulation** | Pure product | Finished vial | Buffer exchange, sterile filtration | T = 4 °C, ISO 5 | Cold room | Packaging waste |

## 3.4 Derivation — Overall Plant Recovery Yield

Let the product be denoted $P$ and the intermediate substrate be $S$. Define the block-wise yields:

- $Y_{R/X}$ = mass of product $P$ generated per mass of biomass $X$ in the BR block.
- $Y_{X/S}$ = mass of biomass $X$ produced per mass of substrate $S$ consumed.
- $Y_{D/P,1}$, $Y_{D/P,2}$ = recovery yields across the sequential DSP blocks.

For a three-block DSP train:

$$
Y_{overall} \;=\; Y_{X/S} \cdot Y_{R/X} \cdot Y_{D/P,1} \cdot Y_{D/P,2} \cdot Y_{D/P,3}
$$

> **Logic of the step:** The block-diagram abstraction **multiplies** the yields of sequential blocks, exactly like gains in a signal-flow block diagram multiply. This is the formal reason why industrial biotech companies pursue high yields in *every* block simultaneously — a 10 % loss in each of 5 blocks compounds to a 41 % overall loss.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Block Diagram of an Industrial Bioprocess

The following Mermaid flow diagram represents the **complete block-level architecture** of an industrial bioprocess. It is the single most important diagram for Module 1 of PEPOT414.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USP-1 RAW MATERIAL RECEIPT AND MEDIA PREPARATION] --> B[USP-2 CONTINUOUS STERILIZATION OR AUTOCLAVE]
    B --> C[USP-3 INOCULUM BUILD UP IN SHAKE FLASK AND SEED REACTOR]
    C --> D[BR-1 STIRRED TANK BIOREACTOR MAIN FERMENTATION]
    AIR[STERILE COMPRESSED AIR] --> D
    D --> E[BR-2 OFF GAS TREATMENT AND HEAT EXCHANGE]
    D --> F[DSP-1 CELL HARVEST BY CENTRIFUGATION OR FILTRATION]
    F --> G[DSP-2 PRIMARY PURIFICATION BY CHROMATOGRAPHY]
    G --> H[DSP-3 POLISHING AND STERILE FILTRATION]
    H --> I[FINAL FORMULATION FILLING AND PACKAGING]
    F --> W1[BIOMASS WASTE STREAM]
    G --> W2[SPENT BUFFERS AND CHEMICAL WASTE]
    E --> W3[EXHAUST GAS WITH CO2 AND MOISTURE]
```

## 4.2 Information/Control Loops Around the Bioreaction Block

The bioreactor is the only block in which **active control** is critical. The following diagram shows the principal control loops drawn as a feedback block-diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[SET POINT FOR EXAMPLE 37 DEG C] --> SUM[COMPARATOR SUMMING JUNCTION]
    PV[PROCESS VARIABLE FROM TEMPERATURE SENSOR] --> SUM
    SUM --> CTRL[PID CONTROLLER BLOCK Gc OF s]
    CTRL --> ACT[ACTUATOR BLOCK Ga COOLING WATER VALVE]
    ACT --> PROC[BIOREACTOR PROCESS BLOCK Gp OF s]
    PROC --> PV
```

The **closed-loop transfer function** of this control block diagram reduces, using standard block-diagram reduction rules, to:

$$
T(s) \;=\; \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \dfrac{G_c(s) \, G_a(s) \, G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) \, G_a(s) \, G_p(s) \, H(s)}
$$

where $H(s)$ is the sensor block (typically = 1 for a temperature probe).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Although full control theory is taught in Instrumentation courses, the KTU 2024 scheme for PEPOT414 frequently tests the **closed-loop block-diagram reduction** of simple bioreactor control loops in 7-mark questions. You are expected to draw the block, identify forward and feedback paths, and apply Mason's gain formula for simple cases.

## 4.3 Block-Diagram Reduction Rules (Quick Reference)

The following rules are the analytical toolkit for simplifying complex bioprocess block diagrams:

| Rule Name | Operation | Formula / Diagram |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Cascade (Series)** | Two blocks in series | $G_{eq}(s) = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s)$ |
| **Parallel** | Two blocks in parallel | $G_{eq}(s) = G_1(s) + G_2(s)$ |
| **Feedback (Negative)** | Block in a feedback loop | $G_{eq}(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$ |
| **Feedback (Positive)** | Block in a positive loop | $G_{eq}(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s) H(s)}$ |
| **Moving a Takeoff Point** | Beyond a block | Equivalent transfer adjusted by $G(s)$ |
| **Moving a Summing Point** | Beyond a block | Equivalent transfer adjusted by $1/G(s)$ |

## 4.4 Block Diagram of a Typical Recombinant Insulin Process (Application Example)

To crystallize the abstraction, here is the **specific block flow** used to manufacture **recombinant human insulin** in *E. coli* — a flagship product of the biotechnology industry.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[FERMENTATION OF RECOMBINANT E COLI] --> P2[CELL DISRUPTION BY HOMOGENIZER]
    P2 --> P3[INCLUSION BODY ISOLATION BY CENTRIFUGATION]
    P3 --> P4[SOLUBILIZATION IN UREA BUFFER]
    P4 --> P5[REFOLDING BY DILUTION]
    P5 --> P6[ENZYMATIC CLEAVAGE WITH TRYPSIN]
    P6 --> P7[REVERSE PHASE CHROMATOGRAPHY]
    P7 --> P8[ION EXCHANGE CHROMATOGRAPHY]
    P8 --> P9[CRYSTALLIZATION AND LYOPHILIZATION]
    P9 --> P10[FINAL STERILE FILLED VIAL]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define a block diagram as applied to a bioprocess. List the three canonical blocks of an industrial bioprocess with one example of equipment used in each block.*

**Model Answer:**
A block diagram is a schematic representation of an industrial bioprocess in which each major functional unit is represented as a rectangular block connected by directional arrows indicating the flow of materials, energy, and information between units.

The three canonical blocks are:
1. **Upstream Processing (USP):** Raw-material preparation, media formulation, sterilization, and inoculum development. *Example: Continuous sterilizer, seed fermenter.*
2. **Bioreaction (BR) Block:** The vessel where the biological transformation occurs, e.g., a stirred-tank bioreactor converting glucose into ethanol via *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*.
3. **Downstream Processing (DSP):** Separation and purification of the product, e.g., a chromatography column, centrifuge, or crystallizer.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for listing three blocks, 1 for examples.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Write the general steady-state mass balance equation for a generic bioprocess block and explain the meaning of each term.*

**Model Answer:**
$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dot{m}_{in,k} \;=\; \sum_{l=1}^{m} \dot{m}_{out,l}
$$

where $\dot{m}_{in,k}$ is the mass flow rate of the $k^{th}$ input stream (kg/h), and $\dot{m}_{out,l}$ is the mass flow rate of the $l^{th}$ output stream (kg/h). The summation runs over $n$ input and $m$ output streams. At steady state, no mass accumulates inside the block, so total input equals total output. *(3 marks — 1 for the equation, 1 for term definitions, 1 for the steady-state condition.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Draw the complete **block diagram of an industrial bioprocess** for the production of **recombinant therapeutic protein** from *E. coli*. Label every block, every input/output stream, and identify the three canonical process blocks. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A stirred-tank bioreactor (BR block) operates at steady state. It receives $800 \; kg/h$ of sterile glucose medium ($x_{S,in} = 0.08$) and discharges $820 \; kg/h$ of broth containing $x_{S,out} = 0.012$ and $x_{X,out} = 0.025$. Off-gas flow rate is $1.2 \; kg/h$ with $x_{S,off} = 0.05$. Verify the mass balance closure and compute the substrate consumption rate. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The block diagram should contain:

- **USP block:** Media tank → Continuous sterilizer → Seed fermenter.
- **BR block:** Main production bioreactor (with sterile air inlet, pH controller, dissolved oxygen probe).
- **DSP block:** Centrifuge → Cell disruption → Chromatography-1 → Chromatography-2 → Formulation.
- Arrows for material flow (medium, broth, air, waste, product).
- Arrows for control flow (pH probe → controller → acid/base pump; DO probe → controller → agitator speed).

The examiner's checklist (7 marks):
- 3 blocks correctly named — **3 marks**
- Correct sequencing and arrows — **2 marks**
- At least one control loop shown — **1 mark**
- Neatness and labels — **1 mark**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Substrate in feed stream:
$$
\dot{m}_{S,in} \;=\; 800 \cdot 0.08 \;=\; 64 \; kg/h
$$

**Step 2:** Substrate leaving in broth:
$$
\dot{m}_{S,out,br} \;=\; 820 \cdot 0.012 \;=\; 9.84 \; kg/h
$$

**Step 3:** Substrate leaving in off-gas:
$$
\dot{m}_{S,out,off} \;=\; 1.2 \cdot 0.05 \;=\; 0.06 \; kg/h
$$

**Step 4:** Total substrate unaccounted for (consumed by reaction):
$$
\dot{m}_{S,consumed} \;=\; 64 \;-\; (9.84 + 0.06) \;=\; 54.10 \; kg/h
$$

**Step 5:** Mass balance closure — total out:
$$
820 + 1.2 \;=\; 821.2 \quad \text{vs} \quad \text{in} = 800
$$
Imbalance = $21.2 \; kg/h$, attributable to oxygen uptake and water of metabolism. This is physiologically reasonable for an aerobic fermentation.

**Conclusion:** The substrate consumption rate is **54.10 kg/h**, and the mass balance is internally consistent with a 2.6 % closure error.

(7 marks — 2 marks each for substrate-in and substrate-out calculations, 1 mark for consumed rate, 1 mark for closure check, 1 mark for conclusion.)

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*(a)* With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the function of each unit operation in the **downstream processing** of a microbial enzyme produced intracellularly. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Derive the **overall recovery yield** of a bioproduct that passes sequentially through a fermenter (yield $Y_{X/S} = 0.45$ g/g, $Y_{P/X} = 0.30$ g/g), a centrifuge (recovery = 92 %), and a chromatography column (recovery = 85 %). If 1000 kg of substrate is fed, how much pure product is finally obtained? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The DSP block diagram for an intracellular enzyme:

1. **Cell harvesting** — Disc-stack centrifuge or microfiltration; separates cells from spent medium.
2. **Cell disruption** — High-pressure homogenizer or bead mill; releases intracellular contents.
3. **Debris removal** — Centrifugation or depth filtration; clarifies the crude extract.
4. **Concentration** — Ultrafiltration (10 kDa MWCO); reduces volume by 10- to 20-fold.
5. **Capture chromatography** — Ion-exchange or affinity column; bulk purification.
6. **Polishing chromatography** — Size-exclusion or reverse-phase; removes trace impurities.
7. **Formulation** — Buffer exchange, sterile filtration, lyophilization.

Marks distribution: 1 mark per correctly-named unit, 1 mark for arrow direction & sequencing, 1 mark for neatest sketch and labelling.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Biomass produced:
$$
m_X \;=\; Y_{X/S} \cdot m_S \;=\; 0.45 \cdot 1000 \;=\; 450 \; kg
$$

**Step 2:** Product formed intracellularly:
$$
m_{P,intra} \;=\; Y_{P/X} \cdot m_X \;=\; 0.30 \cdot 450 \;=\; 135 \; kg
$$

**Step 3:** Product recovered after centrifuge (assumed to release product from cells or recover cells intact):
$$
m_{P,cent} \;=\; 0.92 \cdot 135 \;=\; 124.2 \; kg
$$

**Step 4:** Product recovered after chromatography:
$$
m_{P,final} \;=\; 0.85 \cdot 124.2 \;=\; 105.57 \; kg
$$

**Step 5:** Overall recovery yield:
$$
Y_{overall} \;=\; 0.45 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 0.92 \cdot 0.85 \;=\; 0.1056
$$

> **Final answer:** **105.57 kg** of pure product is obtained from 1000 kg of substrate, corresponding to an overall yield of **10.56 %**.

(7 marks — 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for numerical substitution, 2 marks for the final boxed answer and the overall yield formula.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the three blocks:** Students frequently swap USP and DSP. **Mnemonic:** USP happens *before* the bugs grow; DSP happens *after* the broth leaves the fermenter.
> 2. **Forgetting the off-gas stream:** A common error is balancing only the liquid streams. Off-gas carries evaporated substrate, water, and CO₂ — failing to account for it produces a 5-15 % imbalance.
> 3. **Treating block yields as additive:** The overall plant yield is the **product** of block-wise yields, **not the sum**. A 90 % yield in each of 4 blocks gives $0.9^4 = 0.66$, not $3.6$.
> 4. **Missing the control arrows:** In 7-mark block-diagram questions, a control loop (e.g., pH, DO, T) carries **1 full mark**. Always draw at least one feedback arrow on the BR block.
> 5. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing kg/h with g/L or % (w/w) without clear conversion is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A block diagram is the **functional skeleton** of a bioprocess — not a P&ID.
- **Three canonical blocks:** **USP → BR → DSP**. Memorize the mnemonic "Use Bugs Diligently."
- **Five block attributes:** input streams, internal state, transformation function, output streams, control loops.
- **Mass balance equation (steady state):**
  $$
  \sum \dot{m}_{in} \;=\; \sum \dot{m}_{out}
  $$
  with reaction term $r_j V$ added for the BR block.
- **Overall yield** = **product** of block-wise yields (not sum).
- **Standard numericals:** VVM = 0.1 – 2.0; sterilization = 121 °C / 15 min; inoculum = 5 – 10 %.
- **Block-diagram reduction rules:** cascade (multiply), parallel (add), negative feedback $\frac{G}{1+GH}$.
- **Off-gas stream** must always be considered in mass balance.
- **Control loops** on the BR block are mandatory for full marks.
- **Mermaid labels** must be plain uppercase text — no markdown formatting inside node labels.
- **The pipe `\|` character** is forbidden in markdown tables — use `\vert` for absolute-value notation.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme trend:** Expect a 7-mark sub-question asking to *draw and label* a complete block diagram, and a 7-mark sub-question asking to *verify a mass balance* on a specific block.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primary Metabolites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT414 - INDUSTRIAL BIOTECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Primary Metabolites ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Metabolites** are low molecular weight organic compounds (typically **< 1500 Da**) that are **direct intermediates or end products of normal microbial metabolism** (e.g., glycolysis, TCA cycle, amino acid biosynthesis). They are produced during the **trophophase** (active growth phase) of microorganisms and are **essential for cell growth, development, and reproduction**. In industrial biotechnology (PEPOT414 Module 2), primary metabolites are classified under the broader category of **microbial primary products** and are central to the economics of the fermentation industry. They include: - **Amino Acids** (L-glutamic acid, L-lysine, L-threonine, L-methionine, L-phenylalanine, L-tryptophan) - **Organic Acids** (citric, lactic, gluconic, itaconic, acetic acid) - **Nucleotides & Nucleosides** (5'-IMP, 5'-GMP as flavor enhancers) - **Vitamins** (Vitamin B12, Riboflavin (B2), Vitamin C / Ascorbic acid) - **Solvents & Alcohols** (Ethanol, Acetone, Butanol) - **Industrial Polymers & Solvents** (1,3-Propanediol, Butanediol) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "Primary Metabolites are the Daily Bread of Microbes"** > Imagine a human family: the **daily groceries (rice, vegetables, milk)** are required every day just to survive and keep working — these are the *primary metabolites* of microbes. The **gourmet cheese or birthday cake** is the *secondary metabolite* — produced only after the family is well-fed, during "luxury" or "feast" phases. Just as you cannot run a household without rice, microbes cannot divide or maintain their central metabolism without producing primary metabolites. **Why this matters industrially:** When primary metabolites are produced in a **growth-associated** manner (Luedeking-Piret type I kinetics, where $\alpha > 0$ and $\beta \approx 0$), their production is tightly coupled to cell multiplication. This means the fermenter design must prioritize **high cell density cultures**, controlled nutrient feeding (especially to avoid catabolite repression), and removal of feedback inhibition through auxotrophic or regulatory mutants. ### 1.3 Key Quantitative Benchmarks (Industry Standard Metrics) - **L-Glutamic acid global market:** **> 3.0 million tonnes/year** (Ajinomoto, Fufeng, Meihua). - **L-Lysine global production:** **> 2.5 million tonnes/year** as **monohydrochloride** form. - **Citric acid global market:** **> 2.0 million tonnes/year** (≈ 70 % produced by *Aspergillus niger*). - **Bio-ethanol global production:** **> 100 billion litres/year** (USA & Brazil lead). - **Typical product titer benchmarks:** Citric acid **100–200 g/L**; L-Glutamic acid **100–150 g/L**; Ethanol **80–120 g/L**; L-Lysine·HCl **100–170 g/L**. - **Theoretical ethanol yield from glucose:** **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** (stoichiometric maximum). - **Theoretical citric acid yield from sucrose:** **≈ 0.70 g/g** (carbon recovery basis). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 2 explicitly requires knowledge of production strains, biosynthetic logic, fermentation process parameters, recovery methods, and industrial applications for the **major primary metabolites** (citric acid, glutamic acid, lysine, ethanol, ABE solvents, lactic acid, gluconic acid, vitamins B12 & C). ### 1.4 The Industrial Triad: Producer Strain × Substrate × Process Regime Every primary metabolite process is engineered by manipulating three levers: 1. **Producer Strain (Microbial Workhorse):** *Corynebacterium glutamicum* for amino acids; *Aspergillus niger* for organic acids; *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* and *Zymomonas mobilis* for ethanol; *Clostridium acetobutylicum* for ABE. 2. **Substrate (Carbon & Nitrogen Source):** Glucose, sucrose, molasses, starch hydrolysates, corn steep liquor (CSL), soybean meal. 3. **Process Regime:** Submerged (SmF) vs. Surface (SSF) fermentation, batch / fed-batch / continuous mode, pH, dissolved oxygen (DO), temperature. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Primary Metabolite Production The industrial logic for any primary metabolite follows five mandatory engineering steps: - **Step 1 — Identify the Precursor Pool:** Trace the carbon flux from the chosen substrate (e.g., glucose) into the central metabolic pathway (EMP / TCA / Pentose Phosphate). Example: Glucose → Pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA → Citrate (for citric acid). - **Step 2 — Eliminate Feedback Regulation:** Wild-type microbes sharply regulate primary metabolite production via **feedback inhibition** (allosteric) and **feedback repression** (transcriptional). Industrial strains are mutated to be **auxotrophic** (e.g., *Corynebacterium glutamicum* homoserine auxotroph for lysine) or **resistant to analogue toxins** (e.g., S-(2-aminoethyl)-L-cysteine = AEC resistance for lysine overproducers). - **Step 3 — Strengthen Precursor Supply:** Amplify key enzymes via genetic engineering (e.g., over-express citrate synthase in *A. niger* for citric acid; amplify *ppsA* and *pyc* in *E. coli* for succinate/threonine). - **Step 4 — Optimize Bioreactor Environment:** Control dissolved oxygen (e.g., high DO for vitamin B12 by *Pseudomonas*; *very low* DO for ethanol by *Zymomonas*), pH shifts (e.g., pH drop to 2.0 for *A. niger* citric acid), and nutrient feeding to avoid **catabolite repression** (e.g., feeding glucose slowly in lysine fermentation). - **Step 5 — Downstream Recovery (DSP):** Centrifugation/filtration → cell disruption (if intracellular) → ion exchange / crystallization / precipitation / extraction. Example: **Crystallization as L-glutamic acid monosodium salt (MSG)** after isoelectric precipitation at pH 3.2. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** > - Why eliminate feedback? Because microbes evolved to *avoid* over-producing intermediates — they are "frugal chemists." > - Why control catabolite repression? Rapid glucose uptake represses enzymes of secondary pathways (e.g., invertase, β-galactosidase) via cAMP. > - Why DSP? Fermentation broth is dilute (~5–15 % w/v product); purification accounts for **30–60 %** of total production cost. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Equations | # | Parameter / Equation | Mathematical Form | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|----------------------|-------------------|-------|-------------------| | 1 | Specific Growth Rate | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | h$^{-1}$ | Rate of biomass increase per unit biomass | | 2 | Monod Kinetics | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_{s} + S}$ | h$^{-1}$ | Growth rate as a function of limiting substrate | | 3 | Doubling Time | $t_{d} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | h | Time for cell mass to double | | 4 | Cell Yield (on substrate) | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | g cells / g substrate | Biomass formed per gram substrate consumed | | 5 | Product Yield (on substrate) | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | g product / g substrate | Conversion efficiency | | 6 | Specific Productivity | $q_{P} = \dfrac{1}{X} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dt}$ | g product / (g cells · h) | Cell-specific production rate | | 7 | Volumetric Productivity | $Q_{P} = \dfrac{dP}{dt} = q_{P} \cdot X$ | g / (L · h) | Plant throughput per fermenter volume | | 8 | Luedeking-Piret Kinetics | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt} + \beta \cdot X$ | g / (L · h) | Growth-associated ($\alpha$) + non-growth ($\beta$) product | | 9 | Mass Balance (Substrate) | $r_{S} = \dfrac{1}{Y_{X/S}} \cdot r_{X} + m \cdot X + \dfrac{1}{Y_{P/S}} \cdot r_{P}$ | g / (L · h) | Substrate split: growth + maintenance + product | | 10 | Theoretical Ethanol Yield | $C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \rightarrow 2 C_{2}H_{5}OH + 2 CO_{2}$ | 0.511 g/g | Stoichiometric max from glucose | | 11 | Respiratory Quotient (Citric Acid) | $RQ = \dfrac{CO_{2} \text{ produced}}{O_{2} \text{ consumed}} \approx 1.0$ | — | Indicates carbon flux to citrate | | 12 | Citric Acid Theoretical Yield | $Y_{P/S}^{\text{theo}} \approx 0.70$ (from sucrose) | g/g | 1 mole sucrose → 1 mole citric acid basis | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** In numerical problems, you will often be asked to compute **volumetric productivity** and **overall yield** for a fed-batch amino-acid fermentation. Memorize the relationship $Q_{P} = q_{P} \cdot X$ and $Y_{P/S} = \Delta P / \Delta S$ — these account for **> 80 %** of the mark allocation in calculation questions. ### 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility - **Food & Beverage Industry:** Citric acid is the **world's most produced organic acid by tonnage**; used as acidulant in soft drinks, jams, and confectionery (≈ 70 % of the 2 Mt/yr market). L-Glutamic acid as **MSG** is the dominant umami enhancer (savory taste). - **Animal Feed:** L-Lysine·HCl is the **first limiting amino acid** in corn-soybean pig and poultry diets — supplementation reduces feed protein requirement and nitrogen excretion. - **Pharmaceuticals & Nutrition:** Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin) — produced by *Pseudomonas denitrificans*; Riboflavin (B2) — *Ashbya gossypii* (now dominates industrial production); Vitamin C — *Ketogulonicigenium vulgare* (two-step modern process, replacing the old Reichstein chemical route). - **Biofuel & Solvents:** Bio-ethanol as gasoline oxygenate; ABE fermentation as a renewable route to butanol (a biofuel and chemical platform, 4-carbon building block). - **Polymer Industry:** Lactic acid → poly-lactic acid (PLA), a **biodegradable bioplastic** (NatureWorks, TotalEnergies Corbion). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Monod Kinetics → Luedeking-Piret Coupling **Problem Setup:** In a primary-metabolite batch fermentation (say, L-lysine), the biomass $X$, substrate $S$, and product $P$ concentrations are tracked over time. We wish to derive the **relationship between product formation rate and cell mass**, starting from Monod growth. **Step 1: Define specific growth rate (experimental observable).** $$\mu = \frac{1}{X} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$$ This equation states that the per-cell rate of biomass increase equals the **slope of $\ln X$ vs. $t$**. Log-linearization of biomass data yields $\mu$ directly. **Step 2: Apply Monod saturation relationship for substrate dependence.** $$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_{s} + S}$$ Here, $K_{s}$ is the half-saturation constant (substrate concentration at which $\mu = \mu_{max}/2$). For *Corynebacterium glutamicum* on glucose, $K_{s} \approx 1$–$10$ mg/L. **Step 3: Substitute the differential form of $\mu$ into the integrated growth equation.** Rearranging Step 1: $$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu \cdot X = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_{s} + S} \cdot X$$ **Step 4: Introduce the Luedeking-Piret equation for product formation (primary metabolites are mostly growth-associated).** $$\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} + \beta \cdot X$$ The Greek letter $\alpha$ is the **growth-associated product formation coefficient** (g product / g new cells); $\beta$ is the **non-growth-associated coefficient** (g product / g cells / h). For *true primary metabolites* (ethanol, lactic acid, citric acid, glutamic acid during trophophase), $\alpha \gg 0$ and $\beta \geq 0$. **Step 5: Convert to volumetric productivity by combining with the cell mass balance.** $$Q_{P} = \frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \mu \cdot X + \beta \cdot X = (\alpha \cdot \mu + \beta) \cdot X$$ This shows that **doubling the biomass at fixed $\mu$ doubles the volumetric productivity** — a key engineering reason to operate amino-acid fermenters as **high-cell-density fed-batch cultures**. **Step 6: Express in terms of substrate consumption (mass balance closure).** For an idealized carbon balance where substrate is partitioned into biomass, product, and $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$: $$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\frac{1}{Y_{X/S}} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} - m \cdot X - \frac{1}{Y_{P/S}} \cdot \frac{dP}{dt}$$ This is the **Pirt equation** for substrate. It underpins all feed-rate calculations in fed-batch lysine/glutamate fermentations. > [!NOTE] > **Why this derivation matters:** A 14-mark KTU question often asks *"Explain the kinetics of product formation in a primary-metabolite fermentation, with reference to a specific example."* Walk through Steps 1–6 in the answer; you will score full marks if you correctly distinguish $\alpha$ (growth-associated) from $\beta$ (non-growth) and apply the Luedeking-Piret framework to a named organism. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Maximum Theoretical Ethanol Yield **Step 1: Write the balanced biochemical reaction (Gay-Lussac, 1815).** $$C_{6}H_{12}O_{6} \rightarrow 2\,C_{2}H_{5}OH + 2\,CO_{2}$$ **Step 2: Compute molar masses.** $$\text{Glucose: } 180.16 \text{ g/mol}, \quad \text{Ethanol: } 46.07 \text{ g/mol}, \quad \text{CO}_{2}: 44.01 \text{ g/mol}$$ **Step 3: Compute grams of ethanol per 180.16 g of glucose.** $$2 \times 46.07 = 92.14 \text{ g ethanol}$$ **Step 4: Form the ratio.** $$Y_{P/S}^{\text{theo}} = \frac{92.14 \text{ g}}{180.16 \text{ g}} = 0.5115 \text{ g ethanol / g glucose}$$ **Step 5: Convert to percentage.** $$Y_{P/S}^{\text{theo}} \approx 51.1\,\% \text{ (weight basis)}$$ **Step 6: Compare to industrial reality.** *S. cerevisiae* typically achieves **90–95 %** of this theoretical maximum in a well-controlled fermentation: $$Y_{P/S}^{\text{industrial}} = 0.46 \text{ – } 0.49 \text{ g/g}$$ The shortfall is due to (a) biomass synthesis, (b) glycerol by-product formation, and (c) maintenance metabolism. ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Citric Acid Mass Balance on Sucrose *Aspergillus niger* converts sucrose to citric acid via the glycolytic-TCA pathway. The **net carbon-skeleton balance** simplifies (with biomass and CO$_{2}$ losses small) to: $$C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + 3\,O_{2} \rightarrow C_{6}H_{8}O_{7} + 4\,CO_{2} + 5\,H_{2}O$$ **Step 1: Molar mass basis.** - Sucrose: **342.30 g/mol** - Citric acid: **192.12 g/mol** **Step 2: Theoretical yield.** $$Y_{P/S}^{\text{theo}} = \frac{192.12}{342.30} = 0.5613 \text{ g citric acid / g sucrose}$$ However, in the *A. niger* process, 1 mole of sucrose is hydrolyzed to 1 mole of glucose + 1 mole of fructose, with **only the glucose carbon** routed effectively into the TCA-derived citrate (the fructose carbon is partly lost as $\mathrm{CO_{2}}$ in the PPP shunt). The **empirical industrial yield** is: $$Y_{P/S}^{\text{industrial}} \approx 0.70 \text{ – } 0.85 \text{ g citric acid / g sucrose consumed}$$ The "yield" being higher than stoichiometry is explained because the **CO₂ released in pyruvate decarboxylation (in PPP) is itself derived from the substrate carbon**, so the mass balance is non-trivial and includes $\mathrm{O_2}$ uptake. Industrial yields of **≈ 80 %** (mass basis) are reported in the Pfizer and Cargill process data. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Major Primary Metabolites | Metabolite | Producer Organism | Carbon Substrate | Key Process Variable | Titer (g/L) | Yield (g/g) | Major Industrial Use | |------------|-------------------|------------------|----------------------|-------------|-------------|----------------------| | **Citric Acid** | *Aspergillus niger* | Sucrose / molasses | pH 2.0, high DO, Mn$^{2+}$ limitation | 100–200 | 0.70–0.85 | Acidulant | | **L-Glutamic Acid (MSG)** | *Corynebacterium glutamicum* | Glucose | Biotin limitation, Tween-40 | 100–150 | 0.50–0.60 | Food flavour | | **L-Lysine·HCl** | *C. glutamicum* (AEC$^R$, Hom$^-$) | Glucose / sucrose | Methionine + Threonine co-limited | 100–170 | 0.40–0.50 | Animal feed | | **Lactic Acid** | *Lactobacillus delbrueckii* | Glucose / whey | pH 5.5–6.0, anaerobic, 50 °C | 80–150 | 0.95–0.98 | PLA bioplastic | | **Gluconic Acid** | *A. niger* | Glucose | High DO, pH 6.5, glucose oxidase | 150–200 | > 0.95 | Pharmaceutical | | **Ethanol** | *S. cerevisiae* / *Z. mobilis* | Cane sugar / glucose | Anaerobic, 30 °C | 80–120 | 0.46–0.49 | Biofuel | | **Butanol (ABE)** | *Clostridium acetobutylicum* | Glucose / starch | Anaerobic, 30–37 °C, two phases | 12–20 (butanol) | 0.20–0.30 | Solvent / biofuel | | **Vitamin B12** | *Pseudomonas denitrificans* | Glucose + 5,6-DMB | High DO, 48 h, anaerobic with nitrate | 0.10–0.20 | — | Nutraceutical | | **Riboflavin (B2)** | *Ashbya gossypii* / *B. subtilis* | Glucose + soy oil | Limited O$_{2}$, vegetable oil | 10–30 | 0.05–0.10 | Vitamin supplement | | **5'-IMP / 5'-GMP** | *C. glutamicum* (recombinant) | Glucose | Adenine auxotroph | 10–20 | — | Flavour enhancer | | **Itaconic Acid** | *A. terreus* | Glucose / sucrose | pH 2.0, 39 °C | 80–130 | 0.50–0.60 | Polymer precursor | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The PEPOT414 Module 2 question paper typically focuses on **3–4 metabolites per paper**; citric acid and L-glutamic acid appear most frequently as 14-mark essay topics, while ethanol and ABE are favoured for numerical/coupled questions. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Classification of Microbial Products (with Primary-Metabolite Branch Highlighted) ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["Microbial Metabolites"] --> nodeB["Primary Metabolites"] nodeA --> nodeC["Secondary Metabolites"] nodeA["Microbial Metabolites"] --> nodeD["Primary Metabolites"] nodeA["Microbial Metabolites"] --> nodeC["Secondary Metabolites"] nodeB["Primary Metabolites"] --> nodeE["Amino Acids"] nodeB["Primary Metabolites"] --> nodeF["Organic Acids"] nodeB["Primary Metabolites"] --> nodeG["Nucleotides"] nodeB["Primary Metabolites"] --> nodeH["Vitamins"] nodeB["Primary Metabolites"] --> nodeI["Solvents and Alcohols"] nodeB["Primary Metabolites"] --> nodeJ["Industrial Polymers"] nodeE["Amino Acids"] --> nodeE1["L-Glutamic Acid MSG"] nodeE["Amino Acids"] --> nodeE2["L-Lysine HCl"] nodeE["Amino Acids"] --> nodeE3["L-Threonine"] nodeE["Amino Acids"] --> nodeE4["L-Phenylalanine"] nodeE["Amino Acids"] --> nodeE5["L-Tryptophan"] nodeF["Organic Acids"] --> nodeF1["Citric Acid"] nodeF["Organic Acids"] --> nodeF2["Lactic Acid"] nodeF["Organic Acids"] --> nodeF3["Gluconic Acid"] nodeF["Organic Acids"] --> nodeF4["Itaconic Acid"] nodeG["Nucleotides"] --> nodeG1["5 Prime IMP"] nodeG["Nucleotides"] --> nodeG2["5 Prime GMP"] nodeH["Vitamins"] --> nodeH1["Vitamin B12"] nodeH["Vitamins"] --> nodeH2["Riboflavin B2"] nodeH["Vitamins"] --> nodeH3["Vitamin C Ascorbic"] nodeI["Solvents and Alcohols"] --> nodeI1["Ethanol"] nodeI["Solvents and Alcohols"] --> nodeI2["ABE Acetone Butanol Ethanol"] nodeJ["Industrial Polymers"] --> nodeJ1["1 3 Propanediol"] nodeJ["Industrial Polymers"] --> nodeJ2["Polylactic Acid PLA"] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Citric Acid Production by *Aspergillus niger* ```mermaid graph LR feedstock["Feedstock Preparation

28 to 30 C 7 to 10 days"] fermenter --> filt["Filtration
Rotary Vacuum
Mycelium Removal"] filt --> lime["Calcium Hydroxide
Precipitation
pH 7.0 to 8.5"] lime --> acidify["Sulfuric Acid Wash
Citric Acid Liberation"] acidify --> crystal["Crystallization
Evaporator
Cooling Crystallizer"] crystal --> product["Citric Acid Crystals
Food Grade 99.5 percent"] fermenter --> co2["CO2 Off gas
RQ about 1.0"] filt --> biomass["Waste Mycelium
Animal Feed"] acidify --> gypsum["Gypsum By product"] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: L-Glutamic Acid (MSG) Fed-Batch ```mermaid graph TD stage1["Stage 1 Inoculum Buildup
C glutamicum
Biotin Sufficient"] --> stage2["Stage 2 Production Bioreactor

Glucose Molasses"] --> hydrolyze["Hydrolysis Cooking
