KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] > **Differentiate between primary and secondary metabolites with two examples each.** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** | Feature | Primary Metabolites | Secondary Metabolites | |---|---|---| | Production phase | **Trophophase** (active growth) | **Idiophase** (post-growth) | | Relation to growth | Growth-associated | Non-growth associated | | Function | Essential for cell survival | No clear role in growth | | Molecular weight | Low (< 1500 Da) | Variable, often higher | | Examples | L-Glutamic acid, citric acid, ethanol | Penicillin, streptomycin, gibberellins | | Production scale | Very high tonnage (Mt/yr) | Low tonnage (kg to few t) | (1 mark definition + 1 mark example pair + 1 mark table — typical 3-mark answer.) --- > **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] > **Why is *Corynebacterium glutamicum* preferred for amino acid production? List two specific examples.** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** - *C. glutamicum* is a **Gram-positive, non-sporulating, biotin-auxotrophic coryneform bacterium** that naturally over-produces and **exports L-glutamate** into the medium when its cell envelope is altered (biotin limitation + Tween-40 → leaky membrane). - It grows on **cheap substrates** (glucose, sucrose, molasses, CSL) at high cell densities (OD$_{600}$ > 30) under aerobic conditions. - **Regulatory mutants** (auxotrophs + analogue-resistant) of *C. glutamicum* are the industrial workhorses for L-lysine, L-threonine, L-isoleucine, L-phenylalanine, L-tryptophan, and L-valine. - **Examples:** (1) *C. glutamicum* ATCC 13032 for L-glutamic acid (MSG); (2) *C. glutamicum* B-6 (Homoserine auxotroph, AEC-resistant) for L-lysine·HCl. (1 mark organism + 1 mark rationale + 1 mark examples.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark ESE Question (Module Internal Choice) > **Q3A.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Modified for PEPOT414] > **(a) Describe the production of L-glutamic acid by *Corynebacterium glutamicum* using submerged fermentation. Discuss the strain improvement strategies and the role of biotin limitation. (7 Marks)** > **(b) Explain the downstream processing for recovery of MSG crystals from fermentation broth. What are the key engineering challenges in scaling up this process? (7 Marks)** ### 5.3 Model Answer for Q3A #### Part (a) — Production of L-Glutamic Acid (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Organism and inoculum (1 Mark):** - *Corynebacterium glutamicum* (synonym: *Brevibacterium lactofermentum*) — Gram-positive, biotin-auxotroph, non-motile, rod-shaped. - Working culture bank → shake-flask seed → seed fermenter (5–10 % v/v inoculum) → production fermenter (50–500 m³). **Step 2 — Medium composition (1 Mark):** - **Carbon source:** Glucose 100–150 g/L (or cane molasses, beet molasses, CSL-hydrolysed starch). - **Nitrogen source:** Ammonium sulfate, urea, or aqueous ammonia (used simultaneously as pH adjuster). - **Other nutrients:** CSL (corn steep liquor) for amino acids and vitamins; KH₂PO₄; MgSO₄; trace metals (Fe, Mn, Zn). - **Critical control:** Biotin concentration maintained at **≤ 5 µg/L** (deliberately sub-optimal) to induce the altered membrane. **Step 3 — Fermentation conditions (2 Marks):** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | pH | 7.0–8.0 (controlled by NH₃ feed) | | Temperature | 30–33 °C | | Dissolved O₂ | 30–50 % saturation (aerobic) | | Mode | Fed-batch (glucose fed to avoid catabolite repression) | | Duration | 36–60 h | | Final titer | 100–150 g/L MSG | | Yield | 0.50–0.60 g/g glucose | **Step 4 — Biotin-limitation mechanism (2 Marks):** - *C. glutamicum* has a rigid, mycolic-acid-containing cell wall whose **fatty-acid composition depends on biotin** (it is a cofactor for acetyl-CoA carboxylase, the first step of fatty-acid synthesis). - Under biotin limitation, the cell wall becomes **leaky** to small molecules and the membrane-bound **efflux carrier MscCG (mechanosensitive channel)** opens, exporting L-glutamate to the broth. - **Tween-40 (polyoxyethylene sorbitan palmitate)** is added to mimic this effect by changing membrane fluidity — a patented Ajinomoto enhancement. **Step 5 — Strain improvement (1 Mark):** - Auxotrophic mutants (e.g., oleate-requiring) and **analogue-resistant strains** (e.g., resistant to DL-ethionine, S-methylcysteine) derepress key enzymes (citrate synthase, isocitrate dehydrogenase, GDH). - Modern r-DNA strains over-express NADP-dependent **glutamate dehydrogenase (gdh)** and the **efflux carrier (yggB/mscCG)**. --- #### Part (b) — Downstream Processing (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Cell removal (1 Mark):** - Centrifugation (disc-stack) or membrane microfiltration removes *C. glutamicum* biomass. - Cell paste is washed and sold as animal feed (added revenue). **Step 2 — Decolourization and clarification (1 Mark):** - Activated carbon treatment (0.5–1.0 % w/v) removes coloured impurities and residual media components. - Filtration through polish filter. **Step 3 — Concentration (1 Mark):** - Multi-effect evaporation or reverse osmosis raises the MSG concentration from **~ 100 g/L** to **~ 400–500 g/L**. **Step 4 — Crystallization (2 Marks):** - **Isoelectric crystallization:** pH adjusted to the isoelectric point of L-glutamic acid (pI ≈ 3.22) using sulfuric acid; α-form crystals of L-glutamic acid precipitate. - Crystals are redissolved, decolourized, and **neutralized with NaOH** to form **monosodium L-glutamate (MSG)** — the commercial form. - Cooling crystallization yields the final product. **Step 5 — Drying and packaging (1 Mark):** - Spray-drying or fluidized-bed drying to **< 0.5 % moisture**; sieving to uniform crystal size; packaging in food-grade bags. **Step 6 — Scale-up challenges (1 Mark):** - Maintaining uniform **biotin gradient** in 200 m³ fermenters is hard (dead zones, mixing time). - **Viscosity** rises as titer climbs; impeller power input must be scaled (P/V ≈ 1–3 kW/m³). - **Effluent treatment:** mother liquor is high in (NH₄)₂SO₄ — biological effluent treatment (activated sludge) is mandatory to meet discharge norms (BOD < 100 mg/L). --- ### 5.4 Alternative Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice) > **Q3B.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Modified for PEPOT414] > **(a) Describe in detail the production of citric acid by *Aspergillus niger* via submerged fermentation. Discuss the role of pH, dissolved oxygen, and trace metal control. (7 Marks)** > **(b) With the help of a process flow diagram, describe the recovery of citric acid crystals. Compare *A. niger* fermentation with the *Candida* yeast process. (7 Marks)** ### 5.5 Model Answer for Q3B (Concise but Complete) #### Part (a) — Citric Acid Production by *A. niger* (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Organism (1 Mark):** - *Aspergillus niger* (ATCC 11414, NRRL 2270) is the industrial workhorse; tolerant of low pH and high sugar concentrations. - Spore inoculum (10⁷–10⁸ conidia/mL) from agar slants → seed flask → production fermenter. **Step 2 — Medium (1 Mark):** - Sucrose (12–18 % w/v) or cane molasses; ammonium sulfate, KH₂PO₄, MgSO₄; trace Fe, Zn. - **Mn²⁺ concentration must be kept below 0.02 ppm** — high Mn²⁺ shifts metabolism from citrate to biomass formation (this is the most critical "knob" in citric acid fermentation). **Step 3 — Process parameters (2 Marks):** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | pH | 1.8 – 2.2 (auto-acidifying; no pH control) | | Temperature | 28 – 30 °C | | Dissolved O₂ | > 30 % saturation (aerobic) | | Morphology | Pelleted growth preferred (Ø 1–3 mm pellets) | | Duration | 5–10 days | | Titer | 100–200 g/L | | Yield | 0.70–0.85 g/g sucrose | **Step 4 — Metabolic logic (2 Marks):** - The low pH and Mn²⁺-deficiency **deplete the intracellular pool of Mn-dependent enzymes** (e.g., PEP carboxykinase) and cripple alternate pathways, **funnelling acetyl-CoA into citrate via citrate synthase** (amplified under these conditions). - **TCA cycle is broken** because of low aconitase and isocitrate dehydrogenase activity, so citrate **accumulates** and is exported. - High DO is needed to reoxidize NADH (citrate is an oxidative product). **Step 5 — Fermenter design (1 Mark):** - Stainless-steel reactors (50–200 m³) with Rushton turbines; P/V ≈ 2–3 kW/m³; sterile air supply 0.5–1.0 vvm; foam control with silicon-based antifoam. --- #### Part (b) — Recovery and Process Comparison (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Recovery (3 Marks):** 1. Filtration (rotary vacuum) removes mycelial biomass. 2. **Precipitation:** Ca(OH)₂ added → insoluble **calcium citrate** forms at pH 7.0–8.5. 3. Calcium citrate is filtered, washed, and re-acidified with **H₂SO₄** to liberate citric acid + form **CaSO₄ (gypsum)** byproduct. 4. **Activated carbon** treatment removes colour; ion exchange removes residual metals. 5. Evaporation + cooling crystallization yields **citric acid monohydrate** (below 36.6 °C) or **anhydrous** (above 36.6 °C). **Step 2 — *A. niger* vs. *Candida* (Yeast) Process (3 Marks):** | Feature | *A. niger* (Fungal SmF) | *Candida* sp. (Yeast) | |---|---|---| | pH | Very low (2.0) — auto-acidifying | pH 4.0–5.0; pH control needed | | Substrate | Sucrose / molasses (often unsterile) | Glucose / n-alkanes (must be sterile) | | Process length | 5–10 days | 4–6 days | | Yield | 0.70–0.85 g/g | 0.85–0.95 g/g (on alkanes) | | By-products | Oxalic acid, gluconic acid | Isocitric acid (low) | | Byproduct problem | Trace **oxalate** requires purification | Lower oxalate | | Modern preference | Dominates ≈ 70 % world market | Used where alkanes are cheap | | Sterility | Inherent (low pH) | Required | **Step 3 — Diagram requirement (1 Mark):** Refer to the block flow diagram in Section 4.2 above; ensure the answer explains the sequence: Fermenter → Filter → Lime → Acidify → Crystallize → Product. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Conflating primary and secondary metabolites** in essays; always anchor the answer in *trophophase vs. idiophase* and *growth-association* — these are the keywords examiners search for. > 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems on yield ($Y_{P/S}$) and productivity ($Q_{P}$); a missing unit can cost 1–2 marks. > 3. **Missing the "limiting nutrient" insight** in amino acid fermentations — always mention biotin (glutamate) or Mn²⁺ (citrate) or methionine + threonine (lysine). > 4. **Confusing the two ABE ratios** — the historic ratio is 3:6:1 (acetone : butanol : ethanol); many students write 1:1:1. > 5. **Skipping downstream processing** — 14-mark questions always require DSP discussion (precipitation, crystallization, ion exchange). > 6. **Failing to draw or describe a process flow diagram** — a labelled block diagram in Section 4.2–4.4 is worth at least 2 marks on its own. --- ### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** Primary metabolites are *low-molecular-weight*, *growth-phase* (trophophase) products *essential* for microbial life — e.g., amino acids, organic acids, nucleotides, vitamins, solvents. - **Organism-Metabolite Map (must memorize):** - *Aspergillus niger* → **Citric acid, Gluconic acid, Itaconic acid**. - *Corynebacterium glutamicum* → **L-Glutamic acid, L-Lysine, L-Threonine, L-Isoleucine, L-Phenylalanine**. - *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* / *Zymomonas mobilis* → **Ethanol**. - *Clostridium acetobutylicum* → **Acetone, Butanol, Ethanol (ABE 3:6:1)**. - *Lactobacillus delbrueckii* / *L. bulgaricus* → **Lactic acid (D/L optical isomers)**. - *Ashbya gossypii* / *Bacillus subtilis* → **Riboflavin (B2)**. - *Pseudomonas denitrificans* / *Propionibacterium shermanii* → **Vitamin B12**. - **Critical process knobs:** - **Biotin limitation** + Tween-40 for glutamate. - **Mn²⁺ < 0.02 ppm + pH 2.0** for *A. niger* citric acid. - **AEC-resistance + Homoserine-auxotroph** for lysine. - **DO < 5 %** for *Z. mobilis* ethanol. - **pH switch 5.5 → 4.5** for solventogenic phase in ABE. - **Key kinetics equations:** Monod ($\mu = \mu_{max} S/(K_s + S)$); Luedeking-Piret ($dP/dt = \alpha dX/dt + \beta X$); Pirt mass balance on substrate; volumetric productivity $Q_{P} = q_{P} \cdot X$. - **Theoretical yields to memorize:** - Ethanol from glucose: **0.511 g/g**. - Citric acid from sucrose: **0.70 g/g (industrial average)**. - **Industrial tonnages (writeable in answers as scale-of-production):** - Citric acid **> 2 Mt/yr**; Glutamic acid **> 3 Mt/yr**; L-Lysine·HCl **> 2.5 Mt/yr**; Bio-ethanol **> 100 BL/yr**. - **DSP shortcuts:** Calcium precipitation (citrate); Isoelectric precipitation at pH 3.2 (glutamic acid); Cooling crystallization (MSG, citric acid monohydrate); ABE distillation train. - **Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, structure the answer as: (1) Organism & inoculum → (2) Medium → (3) Process parameters table → (4) Metabolic logic → (5) Fermenter design → (6) DSP → (7) Industrial applications. Use the keyword "growth-associated / Luedeking-Piret" wherever primary metabolites are discussed.

topics.title: Production of Commercially Important Primary Metabolites like Amino Acids, Organic Acids and Vitamins; Production of Alcohol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT414 - INDUSTRIAL BIOTECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Production of Commercially Important Primary Metabolites like Amino Acids, Organic Acids and Vitamins; Production of Alcohol ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What are Primary Metabolites? **Primary metabolites** are low-molecular-weight compounds that are produced by microorganisms as direct intermediates or end-products of their normal growth metabolism. They are essential for the cell's own physiological functions — such as building proteins (amino acids), generating energy (organic acids via TCA cycle), or acting as coenzymes (vitamins). Because they are tied to the **trophophase** (the growth phase), their production is usually coupled with cell growth. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Board Expected):** Primary metabolites are the substances synthesized during the active growth (trophophase) of microorganisms and are directly involved in the normal metabolic pathways of the cell. Examples — amino acids, nucleotides, organic acids, vitamins, alcohols, and polysaccharides. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bakery vs. Cake-Decorator" View Think of a microorganism as a busy bakery that is open from 9 AM to 5 PM (the **trophophase / growth phase**). During those working hours, the bakery produces its **daily essentials** — bread, butter, jam, eggs (these are *primary metabolites*). They are made in large quantities because customers need them, and the bakery needs them too for its own sandwiches. After hours, the bakery starts a side-hustle making fancy wedding cakes (**secondary metabolites** like antibiotics) — these are specialized, non-essential products made in stationary/idle phase. | Feature | Primary Metabolites | Secondary Metabolites | |---|---|---| | Phase of production | Trophophase (growth) | Idiophase (stationary) | | Relation to growth | Directly coupled | Not coupled | | Function in cell | Essential (building blocks) | Non-essential (defense, signalling) | | Examples | Amino acids, ethanol, citric acid | Penicillin, streptomycin | | Yield | High (g/L) | Low (mg/L) | ### 1.3 Salient Industrial Products Covered Under This Module > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlights — KTU Module 2** 1. **Amino Acids** — Glutamic acid, Lysine, Aspartic acid, Threonine, Methionine, Tryptophan, Phenylalanine. 2. **Organic Acids** — Citric acid, Lactic acid, Gluconic acid, Itaconic acid, Acetic acid, Kojic acid. 3. **Vitamins** — Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin), Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin), Vitamin C (Ascorbic acid), Biotin. 4. **Alcohols** — Ethanol (bioethanol), Butanol, Glycerol. ### 1.4 Why is This Topic Important for Industry? The global market for amino acids alone exceeds **USD 30 billion** annually, with **L-Glutamate (~3.5 million tonnes/year)** and **L-Lysine (~2.5 million tonnes/year)** being the largest. Citric acid production exceeds **2 million tonnes/year**, and bio-ethanol production is the single largest bioprocess by volume worldwide. Understanding the *organism*, *substrate*, *carbon/nitrogen regulation*, and *downstream recovery* is therefore a direct industrial necessity. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Generalised Industrial Production Strategy Every primary-metabolite fermentation, irrespective of the molecule, follows a structured **"UPSTREAM → BIOREACTION → DOWNSTREAM"** logic: 1. **Strain Selection & Improvement** — wild type, auxotrophic mutant, or regulatory mutant. 2. **Inoculum Development** — flask → seed train → production fermenter. 3. **Medium Optimisation** — carbon source, nitrogen source, minerals, vitamins, pH, oxygen demand. 4. **Fermentation Mode** — batch, fed-batch, continuous, or solid-state. 5. **Downstream Processing (DSP)** — cell separation, concentration, purification, polishing, formulation. ### 2.2 Section A — Amino Acid Production #### 2.2.1 L-Glutamic Acid - **Key organism:** *Corynebacterium glutamicum* (Gram-positive, biotin-auxotroph in industrial strains). - **Critical regulator:** **Biotin limitation** (≤ 5 µg/L) is the master trigger. Under biotin limitation, the cell wall becomes permeable (leaky) and secretes glutamate into the broth. Additionally, **surfactants** like Tween-40 or penicillin addition enhance secretion. - **Pathway:** Glycolysis → Pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA → Citrate (via citrate synthase) → Isocitrate → **α-Ketoglutarate** → L-Glutamate (via glutamate dehydrogenase + NH₄⁺). - **Carbon sources:** Glucose, sucrose, molasses, starch hydrolysate. - **Nitrogen source:** Ammonium sulphate / ammonia gas (continuous feeding to maintain pH). - **Yield:** ~100 g/L in 40–60 h fed-batch. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Biotin Limitation?** > Biotin is a cofactor for **acetyl-CoA carboxylase**, the first enzyme of fatty-acid synthesis. Limiting biotin weakens the cell membrane (less phospholipid), causing the cell to leak glutamate as a survival response. #### 2.2.2 L-Lysine - **Key organism:** *Corynebacterium glutamicum* (auxotrophic for homoserine, leucine, or threonine). - **Regulatory strategy:** Combine **auxotrophy** (e.g., homoserine⁻) with **feedback-resistant aspartokinase** (the first committed enzyme of the aspartate family). - **Pathway:** Aspartate → Aspartyl-P → Aspartate-semialdehyde → **Dihydrodipicolinate → L-Lysine** (bypasses threonine feedback). - **Yield:** ~120–170 g/L. #### 2.2.3 L-Aspartic Acid, L-Threonine, L-Tryptophan, L-Phenylalanine All derived from the **aspartate family pathway** or the **aromatic pathway**. Production uses feedback-resistant mutants (e.g., aspartokinase resistant to threonine + lysine inhibition). L-Aspartate is industrially made by **enzymatic catalysis** using *aspartase* from *E. coli* with immobilised cells (fumarate + NH₄⁺ → aspartate). ### 2.3 Section B — Organic Acid Production #### 2.3.1 Citric Acid - **Key organism:** *Aspergillus niger* (filamentous fungus) is dominant; *Candida* spp. also used. - **Critical regulators:** **Low pH (1.5–2.5)**, **high carbohydrate concentration (150–200 g/L sucrose)**, **limitation of Mn²⁺, Fe²⁺, Zn²⁺** (trace-metal limitation), high aeration. - **Biochemistry:** Glycolysis → Pyruvate → Acetyl-CoA + Oxaloacetate → **Citrate** (citrate synthase). The acidic pH inhibits aconitase, blocking further oxidation, so citrate accumulates. - **Process modes:** Surface (Koji), submerged, and solid-state (on molasses or wheat bran). - **Recovery:** Precipitation as calcium citrate at pH 7 → acidification with H₂SO₄ → crystallisation. #### 2.3.2 Lactic Acid - **Organism:** *Lactobacillus delbrueckii*, *L. bulgaricus*, *L. plantarum*. - **Mode:** Homofermentative; converts hexoses to 2 lactate via glycolysis. - **Use:** Food acidulant, biodegradable PLA polymer precursor. - **Modern trend:** Engineered *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* and *E. coli* for low-pH, optically pure D- or L-lactic acid. #### 2.3.3 Gluconic Acid, Itaconic Acid, Kojic Acid - **Gluconic acid:** *Aspergillus niger*, glucose oxidase, 25 °C, high O₂. - **Itaconic acid:** *Aspergillus terreus*, low pH (~3), high aeration; used as monomer for plastics. - **Kojic acid:** *Aspergillus oryzae*, used in cosmetics as skin-lightening agent. ### 2.4 Section C — Vitamin Production #### 2.4.1 Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin) - **Organisms:** *Ashbya gossypii*, *Eremothecium ashbyii*, *Bacillus subtilis* (recombinant). - **Pathway:** GTP → Riboflavin (via riboflavin synthase). - **Key regulators:** Limitation of Fe²⁺ and limitation of threonine (in *E. ashbyii*). - **Yield:** Up to 20–30 g/L with engineered *B. subtilis* overproducers. #### 2.4.2 Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin) - **Organisms:** *Pseudomonas denitrificans*, *Propionibacterium shermanii*, *Propionibacterium freudenreichii*. - **Complex pathway** with >30 enzymatic steps; requires cobalt in medium (≥ 10 mg/L as CoCl₂) and 5,6-dimethylbenzimidazole (lower ligand). - **Fermentation:** Anaerobic or micro-aerobic, 5–7 days, 25–30 °C. #### 2.4.3 Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid) - **Two-step route (Reichstein process):** Glucose → D-Sorbitol (catalytic hydrogenation) → L-Sorbose (*Acetobacter suboxydans* bio-oxidation) → Diacetone-L-sorbose → Diacetone-2-keto-L-gulonic acid → Ascorbic acid. - **Modern route (two-fermentation Reichstein variant):** Uses *Gluconobacter oxydans* and *Ketogulonicigenium vulgare*. ### 2.5 Section D — Production of Alcohol (Ethanol) #### 2.5.1 Organism and Biochemistry - **Organism:** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (preferred) — Crabtree-negative, ethanol-tolerant up to ~12–15 % v/v. - **Reaction (Net):** $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase complex}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 $$ $$\Delta G^\circ = -118 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ - **Theoretical yield:** 0.511 g ethanol / g glucose. - **Practical yield:** ~90–95 % of theoretical → ~0.46 g/g glucose. #### 2.5.2 Process Stages 1. **Feedstock preparation** — milling, liquefaction, saccharification (α-amylase + glucoamylase for starches). 2. **Fermentation** — 30–35 °C, pH 4.5–5.0, anaerobic, 24–72 h. 3. **Distillation** — beer column (strip ethanol to ~95 % v/v) → rectification column. 4. **Dehydration** — molecular sieves (3 Å zeolite) to break the 95 % azeotrope → **anhydrous ethanol (99.5 %+)**. 5. **Co-products** — DDGS (Dried Distillers Grains with Solubles) and CO₂ (for beverages/dry ice). #### 2.5.3 Advanced Strategies - **VHG (Very High Gravity) fermentation** — fermenting musts > 27 g sugar / 100 g. - **Immobilised-cell reactors** (alginate-encapsulated *S. cerevisiae*). - **Continuous fermentation** with cell recycling (e.g., Melle-Boinot process — centrifugation, acid wash to control contaminants). - **Gen 2 (cellulosic) ethanol:** Cellulose → glucose (cellulases) → fermentation; pretreatment with dilute acid or steam explosion. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Equations for Solving KTU Numericals** | # | Equation | Meaning / Application | |---|---|---| | 1 | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{-\Delta S}$ | Product yield on substrate (g product / g substrate consumed) | | 2 | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{-\Delta S}$ | Cell-mass yield on substrate | | 3 | $Q_{P} = \dfrac{P}{t}$ | Volumetric productivity (g·L⁻¹·h⁻¹) | | 4 | $\eta_{\text{eth}} = \dfrac{Y_{P/S}^{\text{actual}}}{0.511}$ | Ethanol fermentation efficiency | | 5 | $\mu = \mu_{\max}\dfrac{S}{K_{S}+S}$ | Monod growth equation | | 6 | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Specific growth rate | | 7 | $q_{P} = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ | Specific product formation rate | | 8 | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \alpha\,\mu X + \beta X$ | Luedeking–Piret (Leudeking-Piret) for primary metabolites | | 9 | $P_{\text{theor,eth}} = 0.511 \times S_{0}$ | Max ethanol from initial sugar | | 10 | $P_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.489 \times S_{0}$ | CO₂ released per g glucose | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** For primary metabolites, the Luedeking–Piret constant **α (growth-associated) is large, β (non-growth) is small** — this is the opposite of secondary metabolites. This is a **favourite KTU viva question**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Solutions ### 3.1 Derivation — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose (Gay-Lussac Stoichiometry) The classical fermentation of glucose to ethanol proceeds through the Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) pathway. Two moles of pyruvate are decarboxylated by pyruvate decarboxylase to yield two moles of acetaldehyde, and the acetaldehyde is reduced by alcohol dehydrogenase using NADH generated in glycolysis. We start with the balanced molecular equation: $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 $$ **Step 1 — Molar masses.** $$\text{MW of glucose } = 180 \text{ g/mol}, \quad \text{MW of ethanol } = 46 \text{ g/mol}$$ **Step 2 — Mass ratio.** $$ \frac{2 \times 46}{180} = \frac{92}{180} = 0.5111 $$ Thus 1 g glucose theoretically yields **0.5111 g ethanol**. **Step 3 — Moles of CO₂ produced per mole of glucose.** By inspection of the equation, 2 moles of CO₂ are formed per mole of glucose consumed. Hence: $$ \frac{2 \times 44}{180} = 0.4889 \text{ g CO}_2 \text{ per g glucose} $$ **Step 4 — Carbon mass balance check.** $$ 6 \times 12 = 72 \text{ g carbon in glucose} $$ $$ 2 \times 24 + 2 \times 12 = 48 + 24 = 72 \text{ g carbon in products (ethanol + CO}_2) $$ The balance closes — confirming the stoichiometry. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Style > **Problem (14-mark style):** *A fermentation broth contains 200 g/L of glucose at zero time. After 48 h, the residual glucose is 30 g/L and the ethanol concentration is 78 g/L. Determine: (a) Product yield $Y_{P/S}$, (b) Volumetric productivity $Q_{P}$, (c) Fermentation efficiency with respect to theoretical yield, (d) Specific ethanol productivity, given cell density = 5 g/L.* **Given data** - $S_{0} = 200$ g/L - $S_{t} = 30$ g/L - $P_{t} = 78$ g/L - $t = 48$ h - $X = 5$ g/L **Step (a) Product yield $Y_{P/S}$** $$ Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{-\Delta S} = \dfrac{P_{t} - P_{0}}{S_{0} - S_{t}} $$ $$ Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{78 - 0}{200 - 30} = \dfrac{78}{170} = 0.4588 \text{ g/g} $$ **Step (b) Volumetric productivity $Q_{P}$** $$ Q_{P} = \dfrac{P_{t}}{t} = \dfrac{78}{48} = 1.625 \text{ g·L}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1} $$ **Step (c) Fermentation efficiency** $$ \eta = \dfrac{Y_{P/S}^{\text{actual}}}{Y_{P/S}^{\text{theoretical}}} = \dfrac{0.4588}{0.5111} = 0.8976 \approx 89.76 \% $$ **Step (d) Specific productivity $q_{P}$** $$ q_{P} = \dfrac{Q_{P}}{X} = \dfrac{1.625}{5} = 0.325 \text{ g·g}^{-1}\text{·h}^{-1} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation for KTU answer:** A 90 % efficiency indicates well-controlled industrial conditions; loss of ~10 % ethanol is due to yeast biomass formation, side-products (glycerol, higher alcohols), and evaporation. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Strain-vs-Product Quick-Reference | Product | Microbe | Key Fermentation Condition | Critical Regulatory Trigger | Typical Final Titre | |---|---|---|---|---| | L-Glutamate | *C. glutamicum* | Aerobic, biotin-limited | Biotin ≤ 5 µg/L | 100 g/L | | L-Lysine | *C. glutamicum* (Hse⁻, Thr⁻) | Aerobic, threonine-limited | Aspartokinase desensitised | 120 g/L | | L-Aspartate | *E. coli* (immobilised) | Enzymatic batch | NH₄⁺ excess | 100 g/L | | Citric acid | *A. niger* | Low pH 1.5–2.5 | Mn²⁺ < 0.02 mg/L | 200 g/L | | Lactic acid | *Lb. delbrueckii* | Anaerobic, 45 °C | Low pH tolerance via neutraliser | 100–200 g/L | | Gluconic acid | *A. niger* | High O₂, neutral pH | Glucose oxidase action | 200 g/L | | Itaconic acid | *A. terreus* | Low pH 3.0, high O₂ | Cis-aconitate decarboxylase | 80 g/L | | Riboflavin (B2) | *B. subtilis* (rDNA) | Aerobic, 30 °C | GT cycle deregulation | 25 g/L | | Vitamin B12 | *Ps. denitrificans* | Micro-aerobic, 5 d | Co²⁺ + DMBI addition | 200 mg/L | | Ascorbic acid (Vit C) | *Acetobacter* + chemical step | Two-step | Sorbose oxidation | 100 g/L | | Ethanol | *S. cerevisiae* | Anaerobic, 30 °C, pH 5 | Crabtree effect; ethanol toxicity | 100–120 g/L | | Butanol | *Clostridium acetobutylicum* | Anaerobic biphasic | Solventogenesis trigger | 20 g/L | ### 3.4 Mass-Balance Worked Example — Citric Acid Process > **Problem:** A 100,000 L fermenter is fed with 150 g/L sucrose. Yield of citric acid = 0.70 g/g sucrose. Calculate (a) Total citric acid produced, (b) CO₂ released, (c) Recovery if 90 % is precipitated as calcium citrate. **Sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁) molar mass = 342 g/mol. Citric acid MW = 192 g/mol. Net reaction on sucrose basis:** $$ \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_8\text{O}_7 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step (a)** Citric acid produced = 0.70 × 150 = 105 g/L → **10,500 kg in 100,000 L**. **Step (b)** By mass balance (carbon check): Sucrose C-fraction = 0.421. Citric C-fraction = 0.375. So CO₂-C lost = 0.046 g C per g sucrose → **0.046 × 150,000 kg = 6,900 kg CO₂** (this is recycled as alkali). **Step (c)** Calcium citrate recovered = 0.90 × 10,500 = **9,450 kg**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Process Flow — Industrial Fermentation for a Primary Metabolite ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Strain Selection
Wild Type / Auxotroph / rDNA Mutant] nodeB[Inoculum Development
Flask to Seed Fermenter] nodeC[Production Fermenter
Batch / Fed-Batch / Continuous] nodeD[Cell Separation
Centrifugation / Filtration] nodeE[Concentration
Evaporation / Membrane] nodeF[Purification
Ion Exchange / Crystallisation] nodeG[Final Product
Food / Pharma / Industrial Grade] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture — L-Glutamate Fermentation ```mermaid flowchart LR medA[Glucose or Molasses
Carbon Source] medB[Ammonium Sulphate
Nitrogen Source] medC[Trace Biotin
Limiting Vitamin] cond1[Biotin Limited
Less Than 5 Microgram per Litre] cond2[Tween 40 Surfactant
Membrane Permeabiliser] cond3[pH 7 to 8
Ammonia Feed] orgX[Corynebacterium glutamicum
Aerobic] metab[Glycolysis to TCA
Alpha Ketoglutarate] sec[Glutamate Secretion
Leaky Cell Wall] rec[Recovery
Ion Exchange plus Crystallisation] medA --> orgX medB --> orgX medC --> cond1 cond1 --> orgX cond2 --> orgX cond3 --> orgX orgX --> metab metab --> sec sec --> rec ``` ### 4.3 Citric Acid Process — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD feed1[Substrate Prep
Molasses or Starch Hydrolysate] ferm[Submerged Fermenter
Aspergillus niger] ctrl1[pH 1.5 to 2.5
Low pH] ctrl2[Mn less than 0.02 ppm
Trace Metal Limitation] ctrl3[High Aeration
DOT more than 30 percent] recA[Mycelium Filtration] recB[Calcium Citrate Precipitate] recC[Sulphuric Acid Wash] recD[Crystal Citric Acid
Monohydrate] byproduct[Byproduct Gypsum
Calcium Sulphate] feed1 --> ferm ctrl1 --> ferm ctrl2 --> ferm ctrl3 --> ferm ferm --> recA recA --> recB recB --> recC recC --> recD recC --> byproduct ``` ### 4.4 Ethanol Production — End-to-End Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR raw[Feedstock
Sugarcane or Corn or Cellulose] prep[Preparation
Milling or Milling plus Liquefaction] sacch[Saccharification
Glucoamylase plus Alpha Amylase] ferm[Fermenter
Saccharomyces cerevisiae] co2[CO2 Capture
Beverage or Dry Ice] dist1[Beer Column
Strip to 40 percent] dist2[Rectification
Concentrate to 95 percent] dehyd[Molecular Sieve
3A Zeolite] fuel[Anhydrous Ethanol
99.5 percent plus] ddgs[Co-product DDGS
Animal Feed] raw --> prep --> sacch --> ferm ferm --> co2 ferm --> dist1 --> dist2 --> dehyd --> fuel ferm --> ddgs ``` ### 4.5 Aspartate Family Pathway — Branch-Point Map ```mermaid flowchart TD asp[Aspartate] aspk[Aspartokinase
Feedback Inhibited by Thr plus Lys] asd[Aspartate Semialdehyde] hse[Homoserine] thr[Threonine] met[Methionine] dhp[Dihydrodipicolinate] lys[Lysine] asp --> aspk --> asd asd --> hse --> thr --> met asd --> dhp --> lys ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Q1]** *Differentiate between primary and secondary metabolites with two examples each.* **Model Answer:** Primary metabolites are produced during the **trophophase (active growth)** of the organism and are **essential for the cell's own metabolism** (amino acids, nucleotides, organic acids). Secondary metabolites are produced in the **idiophase (post-growth)** and are **not essential for growth** but serve ecological roles (antibiotics, alkaloids, pigments). *Examples — Primary:* Glutamic acid, ethanol. *Secondary:* Penicillin, streptomycin. > **[KTU University Exam – Model Q2]** *Why is biotin limitation critical in L-glutamate fermentation by Corynebacterium glutamicum?* **Model Answer:** Biotin is a cofactor for **acetyl-CoA carboxylase**, the rate-controlling enzyme of fatty-acid synthesis. Under biotin limitation, membrane phospholipid synthesis is impaired, the cell wall becomes **leaky**, and the organism excretes accumulated **L-glutamate** into the broth as a survival mechanism. Industrial titre of ~100 g/L is achieved only when biotin is held at ≤ 5 µg/L. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style – Question A]** **(a)** Describe in detail the industrial production of **L-Glutamic Acid** by *Corynebacterium glutamicum*. Cover the biochemistry, fermentation conditions, and downstream processing. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the role of **mutant selection (auxotrophic and regulatory)** in amino-acid overproduction. Give an example for L-Lysine. **(7 marks)** ### 5.3 Model Solution — Question A **(a) L-Glutamic Acid Production (7 marks):** *Biochemistry (3 marks):* Glucose is metabolised via glycolysis to pyruvate, then enters the TCA cycle as acetyl-CoA. It condenses with oxaloacetate to form citrate, which is isomerised to isocitrate and oxidatively decarboxylated to **α-ketoglutarate**. α-Ketoglutarate is aminated by **glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH)** in the presence of NH₄⁺ to yield L-glutamate. *Fermentation conditions (2 marks):* Aerobic, 30 °C, pH 7.0–8.0 maintained by NH₃/ammonia feed. The medium contains 10–15 % glucose, ammonium sulphate as nitrogen source, and **biotin limited to < 5 µg/L**. A surfactant such as **Tween-40 (0.1 %)** or a pulse of penicillin enhances membrane permeability, promoting excretion. *Downstream processing (2 marks):* Cells are removed by ultrafiltration. The clarified broth is passed through **cation-exchange resin** (e.g., Dowex 50) at low pH. L-Glutamate is eluted with dilute alkali, decolourised with activated carbon, concentrated, and crystallised as **monosodium glutamate (MSG)** by adjusting pH to its isoelectric point (pI = 3.22). **(b) Mutant strategies for amino-acid overproduction (7 marks):** The wild-type cell tightly regulates amino-acid synthesis through **feedback inhibition** of the first committed enzyme and **feedback repression** of the corresponding genes. Overproduction requires eliminating this regulation. - **Auxotrophic mutants:** A strain that **cannot synthesise** a related amino acid will continue producing the desired one because the feed-back inhibition loop is broken by the missing end-product. Example — *C. glutamicum* requiring **threonine + methionine** (Thr⁻, Met⁻) accumulates **lysine**. - **Regulatory (feedback-resistant) mutants:** A point mutation in the **aspartokinase** gene (lysC) desensitises the enzyme to lysine + threonine inhibition. Such strains accumulate lysine even in the presence of the natural end-products. - **Transporter mutants:** Strains with impaired glutamate uptake (e.g., *C. glutamicum* lacking the NCgl1221 importer) over-secrete glutamate. - **Recombinant DNA:** Cloning of feedback-resistant *lysC* under a strong promoter on multi-copy plasmids amplifies lysine flux. > **Example — L-Lysine:** A classical industrial strain *C. glutamicum* DM1729 is auxotrophic for **leucine, methionine, threonine, isoleucine** and carries a feedback-resistant aspartokinase. Fed-batch fermentation with glucose gives final titres of 120–170 g/L. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style – Question B (Alternative Choice)]** **(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **industrial production of citric acid** by *Aspergillus niger*. Discuss the critical process parameters. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write notes on (i) **Riboflavin (Vitamin B2) production** by *Ashbya gossypii*, and (ii) **Ethanol fermentation** by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* including downstream distillation. **(7 marks)** ### 5.4 Model Solution — Question B **(a) Citric Acid Production (7 marks):** *Process flow (3 marks):* Molasses is diluted to 150–200 g/L sucrose, supplemented with NH₄NO₃ and minerals (except Mn²⁺ which is held below 0.02 mg/L). The pH is initially set to ~5.0, but as the fungus grows it acidifies the medium to pH **1.5–2.5**. Fermentation lasts 5–9 days in submerged aerated tanks. *Critical parameters (2 marks):* (1) **pH 1.5–2.5** inhibits aconitase, blocking citrate further oxidation; (2) **Mn²⁺, Fe²⁺, Zn²⁺ limitation** deregulates the TCA cycle to favour citrate; (3) **High dissolved oxygen** is required; (4) **Citric acid > 100 g/L** self-inhibits, so fed-batch is preferred. *Downstream (2 marks):* Filter off mycelium → add Ca(OH)₂ to precipitate **calcium citrate** at pH 7 → wash → treat with H₂SO₄ to liberate citric acid and precipitate CaSO₄ (gypsum) → concentrate → crystallise as **citric acid monohydrate** at ≤ 36.6 °C or **anhydrous** above 36.6 °C. **(b) (i) Riboflavin production (3 marks):** *Ashbya gossypii* is cultured aerobically at 28 °C for 5–7 days on a glucose + corn-steep-liquor medium. **Iron and threonine limitation** triggers overproduction via derepression of GTP cyclohydrolase II and riboflavin synthase. Modern recombinant *Bacillus subtilis* strains, with the rib operon cloned under strong promoters, give titres of 25–30 g/L. Recovery is by **crystallisation after hot-water extraction** and decolourisation. **(ii) Ethanol fermentation (4 marks):** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* ferments hexoses through **EMP glycolysis → pyruvate → acetaldehyde (pyruvate decarboxylase) → ethanol (alcohol dehydrogenase)**. Anaerobic conditions, 30–35 °C, pH 4.5–5.0. Fermentation lasts 24–72 h. The broth ("beer") containing 6–12 % ethanol is sent to the **beer column** (strips ethanol to ~40 %), then the **rectification column** (~95 %), and finally **molecular sieve dehydration** (3 Å zeolite) to yield **anhydrous ethanol (> 99.5 %)**. Co-products include **CO₂** and **DDGS** (Dried Distillers Grains with Solubles). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. Confusing **auxotroph** (cannot grow without a nutrient) with **regulatory mutant** (enzyme insensitive to feedback). *2-mark penalty if reversed.* > 2. Forgetting to mention **Mn²⁺ limitation** in citric acid — many students only mention pH, missing the trace-metal regulation entirely. > 3. Writing "ethanol is produced by **zymase**" without specifying the two enzymes (PDC + ADH). The examiner expects the **two-step acetaldehyde intermediate**. > 4. Omitting units in yield equations ($Y_{P/S}$ must carry units g·g⁻¹). > 5. Drawing the bioreactor but **forgetting impeller and aerator lines** in the citric acid diagram — these are part of the critical high-O₂ requirement. --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2** - **Primary metabolites** are produced during the **trophophase**; they are essential for cell growth. - **L-Glutamate** → *C. glutamicum*, **biotin-limited**, Tween-40, pH 7–8, MSG recovery via ion exchange. - **L-Lysine** → *C. glutamicum* (Thr⁻ + Met⁻ auxotroph **plus** feedback-resistant aspartokinase). - **L-Aspartate** → Immobilised *E. coli* aspartase (enzymatic, fumarate + NH₄⁺ → aspartate). - **Citric acid** → *A. niger*, **low pH + trace-metal limitation + high O₂**, recovery via Ca-citrate → H₂SO₄. - **Lactic acid** → *Lactobacillus*, homofermentative, key for PLA bioplastics. - **Gluconic acid** → *A. niger*, glucose oxidase route. - **Itaconic acid** → *A. terreus*, low pH, polymer feedstock. - **Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin)** → *Ashbya gossypii* / engineered *Bacillus subtilis*; Fe²⁺ and threonine limitation. - **Vitamin B12** → *Pseudomonas denitrificans* or *Propionibacterium* spp.; Co²⁺ + 5,6-DMBI required; long 5–7 day fermentation. - **Vitamin C (Ascorbic acid)** → Reichstein process: glucose → sorbitol → sorbose (*Acetobacter*) → chemical steps → ascorbic acid. - **Ethanol** → *S. cerevisiae*; theoretical yield **0.511 g/g glucose**; efficiency typically 90–95 %; downstream uses **distillation + molecular sieve** for anhydrous product. - **Key equations:** $Y_{P/S}$, $Q_{P}$, $\eta = Y_{P/S}^{\text{actual}}/0.511$, Luedeking–Piret with high α. - **Luedeking–Piret:** for primary metabolites, **α (growth-associated) is dominant**; for secondary metabolites, **β is dominant**. - **Distinguish:** Trophophase vs. idiophase; feedback inhibition vs. repression; auxotroph vs. regulatory mutant. - **Industrial scale benchmark values** to remember — Glutamate 100 g/L; Lysine 120–170 g/L; Citric acid 200 g/L; Ethanol 100–120 g/L; Riboflavin 25 g/L; Vitamin B12 200 mg/L.

topics.title: Secondary Metabolites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT414 - INDUSTRIAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Secondary Metabolites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Secondary Metabolites (SMs)** are low-molecular-weight organic compounds produced by microorganisms, plants, fungi, and marine organisms that are **NOT essential for the basic growth, reproduction, or primary metabolic functions** of the producing organism, but play critical roles in ecological adaptation, defense, signaling, and interspecies competition.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance (per Module 3 of PEPOT414), secondary metabolites are often termed **"idiotrophs"** or **"specialized metabolites"**. They are produced during the **stationary phase** (Phase III) of microbial growth — also called the **Idiophase** — as opposed to the **Trophophase** (primary growth phase).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of Industrial Biotechnology emphasizes the industrial production, classification, and bioprocess strategies for secondary metabolites. Common KTU exam focus areas include antibiotic production (penicillin, streptomycin), alkaloids, terpenoids, and recombinant approaches for overproduction.

## 1.2 Primary vs. Secondary Metabolites — Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a bakery. 
> - **Primary metabolites** = The daily bread loafs (essential for daily income and survival of the bakery). 
> - **Secondary metabolites** = The premium pastries, artisan cheeses, and aged wines (luxury items produced once the bakery is established and the basic income is stable).
> 
> The bakery will NOT collapse without pastries, but they bring prestige, niche customers, and higher profit margins — exactly the role secondary metabolites play for a microorganism.

### Quick Comparison Table

| Parameter | Primary Metabolites | Secondary Metabolites |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase of Production** | Trophophase (log phase) | Idiophase (stationary phase) |
| **Essential for Growth?** | YES | NO |
| **Molecular Weight** | Generally smaller, building blocks | Often larger, complex structures |
| **Examples** | Amino acids, nucleotides, organic acids | Antibiotics, alkaloids, pigments, toxins |
| **Production Titer** | High (g/L) | Often low (mg/L – g/L) |
| **Industrial Importance** | Bulk chemicals | High-value pharma, nutraceuticals |
| **Number of Producers** | Universal in all cells | Restricted to specific species |
| **Genetic Regulation** | Constitutive / tightly regulated | Often induced by stress/nutrients |
| **Species Specificity** | Low | High |

## 1.3 General Characteristics of Secondary Metabolites

1. **Non-essential for vegetative growth** but provide ecological advantages.
2. **Produced in restricted taxonomic groups** — not universal.
3. **Diverse chemical structures** — often containing unusual sugars, amino acids, or ring systems.
4. **Synthesis occurs from primary metabolite precursors** via specialized pathways (PKS, NRPS, terpene cyclases).
5. **Production is influenced by nutrient limitation** (especially C, N, or P).
6. Often **exhibit biological activity** against competing organisms, predators, or pests.
7. Function as **survival tools**: defense, signaling, ion transport, pigmentation (UV protection), and symbiosis.
8. **High commercial value**: pharmaceutical, agricultural, and nutraceutical applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do Cells Produce Secondary Metabolites? (The "Why")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Secondary metabolites are **NOT waste products** — they are evolutionarily refined, ecologically purposeful molecules.

The principal ecological and physiological functions include:

- **Chemical defense** against predators (e.g., streptomycin inhibits rival bacteria).
- **Antimicrobial warfare** against competing microorganisms (most antibiotics).
- **Metal ion transport** (e.g., siderophores like enterobactin).
- **Symbiotic signaling** in plant-microbe interactions (e.g., rhizobial nod factors).
- **Sexual reproduction and differentiation** triggers (e.g., fungal sporulation).
- **UV protection** via pigmentation (e.g., melanins).
- **Hormonal or regulatory functions** within the producing organism.

## 2.2 Classification of Secondary Metabolites

Secondary metabolites are traditionally classified based on their **biosynthetic origin** into the following major chemical families:

### 2.2.1 Alkaloids
- **Source:** Plants (Papaver, Catharanthus), fungi (*Claviceps*), bacteria (*Streptomyces*).
- **Chemical nature:** Nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds.
- **Examples:** Morphine (analgesic), quinine (antimalarial), vincristine (anticancer), ergotamine (vasoconstrictor).

### 2.2.2 Polyketides (PKs)
- **Source:** Mostly *Streptomyces* and fungi (*Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*).
- **Pathway:** Polymerization of acetyl-CoA / malonyl-CoA via **Polyketide Synthases (PKS)**.
- **Examples:** Erythromycin (macrolide antibiotic), lovastatin (cholesterol-lowering), aflatoxin (mycotoxin), doxorubicin (anticancer).

### 2.2.3 Terpenoids / Terpenes
- **Source:** Plants, fungi, bacteria.
- **Building block:** **Isoprene (C5)** units derived from **IPP (Isopentenyl Pyrophosphate)** and **DMAPP (Dimethylallyl Pyrophosphate)** via the **Mevalonate (MVA)** or **MEP pathway**.
- **Examples:** Menthol (cooling agent), artemisinin (antimalarial), paclitaxel/taxol (anticancer), carotenoids (pigments).

### 2.2.4 Phenolic Compounds
- **Source:** Ubiquitous in plants (flavonoids, lignans), some microbes.
- **Pathway:** Derived from **shikimate pathway** or via **acetate-malonate pathway**.
- **Examples:** Resveratrol, flavonoids, tannins, gallic acid.

### 2.2.5 Non-Ribosomal Peptides (NRPs)
- **Source:** Bacteria and fungi.
- **Pathway:** Synthesized by **Non-Ribosomal Peptide Synthetases (NRPS)** — independent of mRNA/ribosome.
- **Examples:** Vancomycin, bacitracin, cyclosporin A (immunosuppressant), gramicidin.

### 2.2.6 Saccharides / Glycosides
- **Source:** *Streptomyces*, plants.
- **Examples:** Streptomycin, gentamicin (aminoglycoside antibiotics).

### 2.2.7 Mixed / Hybrid Pathways
- Examples include the **PKS-NRPS hybrids** like epothilone (anticancer from *Sorangium cellulosum*).

## 2.3 Bioprocess Conditions Affecting Secondary Metabolite Production

| Factor | Effect on SM Production |
|---|---|
| **Carbon source** | Glucose often represses SM synthesis ("**glucose effect / catabolite repression**") |
| **Nitrogen source** | Limited N (e.g., ammonia limitation) often **triggers** antibiotic production |
| **Phosphate** | High PO₄³⁻ suppresses; low PO₄³⁻ often stimulates |
| **pH** | Optimum varies: 6.8–7.5 typical for *Streptomyces* |
| **Temperature** | Species-specific; 25–30 °C common |
| **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** | Critical; some SMs are strictly aerobic |
| **Growth rate (μ)** | Inverse relationship; low μ favours SM production |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **"Idiophase"** in industrial fermentations is deliberately engineered by slowing growth — using slow-feeding nutrients (fed-batch mode), limiting phosphate, or using slowly metabolized sugars — to maximize secondary metabolite titer.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are routinely tested in PEPOT414 numerical/analytical questions. Master each symbol.

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Specific growth rate | h⁻¹ |
| $q_{P} = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ | Specific product formation rate | g P · (g X)⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| $q_{S} = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dS}{dt}$ | Specific substrate consumption rate | g S · (g X)⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | Biomass yield on substrate | g X · (g S)⁻¹ |
| $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | Product yield on substrate | g P · (g S)⁻¹ |
| $P_{r} = \dfrac{P_{final} - P_{initial}}{t}$ | Volumetric productivity | g · L⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| $t_{d} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | Doubling time of biomass | h |
| $q_{P} = \alpha \mu + \beta$ | **Luedeking-Piret equation** (growth-associated SM) | g P · (g X)⁻¹ · h⁻¹ |
| $DS = \dfrac{dF}{dV}$ | Dilution rate (chemostat) | h⁻¹ |
| $D = \mu$ (at steady state) | Washout condition balance | h⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In industrial penicillin or streptomycin fermentations, the **Luedeking-Piret equation** is the operational backbone for predicting product titer from biomass concentration. Values of $\alpha > 0$ indicate growth-associated production; $\beta > 0$ alone indicates non-growth associated (pure secondary metabolite behaviour).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Luedeking-Piret Equation for Secondary Metabolite Production

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Luedeking-Piret model is the **gold standard KTU-level derivation** for relating secondary metabolite formation rate to microbial growth. It is critical for understanding industrial antibiotic fermentations.

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption

The product formation rate ($dP/dt$) is assumed to depend on **two terms**:

- A term proportional to the **growth rate** of biomass (growth-associated).
- A term proportional to the **existing biomass concentration** itself (non-growth associated).

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Expression

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \dfrac{dX}{dt} + \beta \, X
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = growth-associated product formation coefficient (g P per g X)
- $\beta$ = non-growth associated product formation coefficient (g P per g X per h)
- $X$ = biomass concentration (g/L)
- $P$ = product concentration (g/L)

### 3.1.3 Dividing Both Sides by Biomass Concentration $X$

Dividing throughout by $X$, we obtain the specific product formation rate:

$$
q_{P} = \alpha \, \mu + \beta
$$

This is the **Luedeking-Piret equation**.

### 3.1.4 Interpretation of $\alpha$ and $\beta$

| Scenario | $\alpha$ | $\beta$ | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure growth-associated (e.g., ethanol) | $\alpha > 0$ | $\beta = 0$ | Product formed only during growth |
| Pure non-growth (pure secondary metabolite) | $\alpha = 0$ | $\beta > 0$ | Product formed only in stationary phase (e.g., streptomycin, most antibiotics) |
| Mixed (semi-associated) | $\alpha > 0$ | $\beta > 0$ | Product formed in both phases (e.g., lactic acid) |

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A *Streptomyces griseus* culture produces streptomycin. During fed-batch fermentation, the following data are recorded:

- Initial biomass $X_0 = 0.5$ g/L, final $X_f = 4.5$ g/L over 50 h.
- Initial product $P_0 = 0$ mg/L, final $P_f = 250$ mg/L.
- Given: $\alpha = 0.02$, $\beta = 0.001$ h⁻¹. Verify consistency.

**Step 1 — Compute Average Growth Rate**

$$
\Delta X = X_f - X_0 = 4.5 - 0.5 = 4.0 \; \text{g/L}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dX}{dt} \approx \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{4.0}{50} = 0.08 \; \text{g/L/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Average Biomass Concentration**

$$
\bar{X} = \dfrac{X_0 + X_f}{2} = \dfrac{0.5 + 4.5}{2} = 2.5 \; \text{g/L}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Specific Growth Rate**

$$
\mu = \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}}\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \dfrac{0.08}{2.5} = 0.032 \; \text{h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $q_P$ from Luedeking-Piret**

$$
q_{P} = \alpha \mu + \beta = (0.02)(0.032) + 0.001
$$

$$
q_{P} = 0.00064 + 0.001 = 0.00164 \; \text{g P} \cdot (\text{g X})^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Total Product Predicted**

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dt} = q_P \cdot \bar{X} = 0.00164 \times 2.5 = 0.0041 \; \text{g/L/h}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 0.0041 \times 50 = 0.205 \; \text{g/L} = 205 \; \text{mg/L}
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with Observed**

Observed $\Delta P = 250$ mg/L. Predicted = 205 mg/L. Close agreement (~18% deviation), confirming the model.

## 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Productivity in Fed-Batch Culture

The volumetric productivity $P_r$ is defined as:

$$
P_{r} = \dfrac{P_{final}}{t}
$$

For a fed-batch process with intermittent substrate feeding, the **overall substrate-based product yield** is:

$$
Y_{P/S}^{overall} = \dfrac{P_{final}}{\sum \Delta S_{i}}
$$

### Worked Example — Yield Calculation

**Problem:** In a 10,000 L fermenter for penicillin production:
- Final penicillin titer $P_f = 30$ g/L
- Final volume $V_f = 8,500$ L (after evaporation & sampling losses)
- Total substrate (glucose) fed $S_{total} = 8,000$ kg
- Fermentation time = 180 h

**Compute (a) Productivity, (b) Total Penicillin Produced, (c) Yield**

**(a) Volumetric Productivity**

$$
P_{r} = \dfrac{30 \; \text{g/L}}{180 \; \text{h}} = 0.167 \; \text{g/L/h}
$$

**(b) Total Penicillin**

$$
P_{total} = 30 \times 8,500 = 255,000 \; \text{g} = 255 \; \text{kg}
$$

**(c) Yield**

$$
Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{255{,}000 \; \text{g}}{8{,}000{,}000 \; \text{g}} = 0.0319 \; \text{g P} / \text{g S} \approx 3.19\%
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Production Parameters

| Parameter | Penicillin | Streptomycin | Erythromycin | Lovastatin | Paclitaxel |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Producer** | *Penicillium chrysogenum* | *Streptomyces griseus* | *Saccharopolyspora erythraea* | *Aspergillus terreus* | *Taxus* plant cell culture |
| **Class** | β-lactam (NRP hybrid) | Aminoglycoside | Macrolide (polyketide) | Polyketide | Diterpenoid |
| **Typical Titer (g/L)** | 30–90 | 5–15 | 8–15 | 10–15 | 0.2–0.5 |
| **Phase of Production** | Idiophase | Idiophase | Idiophase | Idiophase | Idiophase |
| **Carbon Source Repression?** | YES (glucose) | YES | YES | YES | YES |
| **Mode** | Fed-batch | Fed-batch | Fed-batch | Batch/Fed-batch | Two-phase (callus) |
| **Key Trigger** | Lactose + low glucose | Mannitol + limited N | Glucose + limited N | Slow-feeding | Methyl jasmonate elicitation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Diagram of Secondary Metabolites

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Secondary Metabolites] --> B1[Alkaloids]
    A --> B2[Polyketides]
    A --> B3[Terpenoids and Steroids]
    A --> B4[Phenolic Compounds]
    A --> B5[Non Ribosomal Peptides]
    A --> B6[Aminoglycosides]
    A --> B7[Hybrid Molecules]

    B1 --> C1[Morphine Codeine Vincristine]
    B2 --> C2[Erythromycin Lovastatin Aflatoxin]
    B3 --> C3[Menthol Artemisinin Taxol Carotenoids]
    B4 --> C4[Resveratrol Flavonoids Tannins]
    B5 --> C5[Vancomycin Bacitracin Cyclosporin]
    B6 --> C6[Streptomycin Gentamicin Neomycin]
    B7 --> C7[Epothilone Aureothin]
```

## 4.2 Bioprocess Flow for Industrial Antibiotic Production

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Inoculum Prep] --> S2[Stage 2: Seed Fermenter]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Production Fermenter]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Broth Harvest]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Filtration and Cell Removal]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Extraction with Solvent]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7: Purification Chromatography]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8: Crystallization and Drying]
    S8 --> S9[Stage 9: Final API Formulation]

    S3 -. monitors .-> M1[DOT pH Temperature Nutrient Feed]
    S3 -. phase control .-> P1[Trophophase for Growth then Idiophase for SM]
```

## 4.3 Genetic & Biosynthetic Pathway Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Primary Metabolites] --> P2[Acetyl CoA Malonyl CoA]
    P1 --> P3[Amino Acids]
    P1 --> P4[IPP and DMAPP]
    P1 --> P5[Shikimate]

    P2 --> Q1[Polyketide Synthase PKS]
    Q1 --> R1[Polyketides Erythromycin Lovastatin]

    P3 --> Q2[Non Ribosomal Peptide Synthetase NRPS]
    Q2 --> R2[Non Ribosomal Peptides Vancomycin Cyclosporin]

    P4 --> Q3[Terpene Cyclases MVA MEP Pathway]
    Q3 --> R3[Terpenoids Menthol Artemisinin Taxol]

    P5 --> Q4[Shikimate Pathway Enzymes]
    Q4 --> R4[Phenolics Resveratrol Flavonoids]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of an Industrial Fermenter for SM Production

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Feed Reservoir Glucose Ammonia Phosphate] --> B[Sterile Feed Line]
    B --> C[Production Vessel 50 to 200 m3]
    C --> D[Agitator and Impellers]
    C --> E[Sparger for Aeration]
    C --> F[Heat Exchanger for Temperature]
    C --> G[Inline Sensors pH DO Temperature Foam]
    G --> H[Distributed Control System DCS]
    H --> A
    H --> C
    C --> I[Harvest Line to Downstream Processing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define secondary metabolites. Differentiate between primary and secondary metabolites with suitable examples. (3 marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Secondary metabolites are low-molecular-weight organic compounds produced by certain organisms that are **not essential for growth or reproduction** but provide ecological advantages such as defense, signaling, and competition. They are produced during the **stationary (idiophase)** of microbial growth.

> **Differentiation Table (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | Primary | Secondary |
|---|---|---|
| Essential for growth? | Yes | No |
| Production phase | Trophophase | Idiophase |
| Example | Glutamic acid, lysine | Penicillin, morphine |

---

### Question 2: What is the significance of the Luedeking-Piret equation in industrial secondary metabolite production? (3 marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Significance (3 marks):** The Luedeking-Piret equation, $q_P = \alpha \mu + \beta$, mathematically relates the **specific product formation rate** to the **specific growth rate** and biomass concentration. It helps bioprocess engineers predict secondary metabolite titers in fermenters, classify products as growth-associated or non-growth associated, and design feeding strategies (e.g., fed-batch penicillin production) to maximize antibiotic yield by controlling $\mu$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Question on Secondary Metabolites

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 3 Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Classify secondary metabolites with suitable examples. Explain the role of polyketide synthases (PKS) and non-ribosomal peptide synthetases (NRPS) in their biosynthesis.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Classification (4 marks):**
> 1. **Alkaloids** — Nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds. Examples: Morphine, quinine, vincristine.
> 2. **Polyketides** — Assembled from acetyl-CoA/malonyl-CoA. Examples: Erythromycin, lovastatin.
> 3. **Terpenoids** — Built from isoprene units (IPP/DMAPP). Examples: Menthol, artemisinin, paclitaxel.
> 4. **Phenolics** — From shikimate pathway. Examples: Resveratrol, flavonoids.
> 5. **Non-ribosomal peptides** — Synthesized by NRPS enzymes. Examples: Vancomycin, cyclosporin A, bacitracin.
> 6. **Aminoglycoside antibiotics** — Sugar-containing. Examples: Streptomycin, gentamicin.

> **Role of PKS (1.5 marks):** Polyketide synthases are **multifunctional enzyme complexes** that catalyze the sequential Claisen-type condensation of acyl-CoA units (typically acetyl-CoA starter + malonyl-CoA extenders). Each PKS module contains **ketosynthase (KS), acyltransferase (AT), and acyl carrier protein (ACP)** domains. Examples: 6-deoxyerythronolide B synthase (DEBS) producing erythromycin aglycone.

> **Role of NRPS (1.5 marks):** NRPS are large modular enzymes with **adenylation (A), thiolation/PCP, and condensation (C) domains**. They activate amino acids as aminoacyl-adenylates, tether them to PCP, and condense them without the need for ribosomes or mRNA, allowing incorporation of non-proteinogenic amino acids. Example: Cyclosporin synthetase producing cyclosporin A.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the bioprocess strategies used for the industrial production of penicillin. Explain why secondary metabolites are typically produced in the idiophase.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Bioprocess Strategies for Penicillin (4 marks):**
> 1. **Strain improvement:** Classical mutagenesis (UV, NTG) and modern metabolic engineering of *Penicillium chrysogenum* have boosted titers from ~2 mg/L (Fleming, 1929) to >70 g/L today.
> 2. **Fed-batch mode:** Glucose is fed slowly to avoid **catabolite repression**; lactose (a slowly metabolized sugar) is used as an alternative carbon source in classical processes.
> 3. **Nutrient limitation:** Nitrogen and phosphate are carefully controlled to extend the **idiophase**.
> 4. **pH control:** Maintained at 6.8–7.2.
> 5. **Aeration:** Penicillin requires high dissolved oxygen.
> 6. **Temperature:** 25–27 °C; lower temperatures extend idiophase.
> 7. **Downstream processing:** Solvent extraction, crystallization, and HPLC purification.

> **Why Idiophase? (3 marks):**
> 1. Secondary metabolite biosynthetic genes are often **transcriptionally repressed** during rapid growth.
> 2. Primary metabolism **competes** for precursors (acetyl-CoA, amino acids) during trophophase.
> 3. Nutrient limitation (especially N, P) **de-represses** SM gene clusters via global regulators like **PhoPR, AfsR, or BrpA**.
> 4. The **slowed-growth state** allows accumulation of secondary metabolite precursors (e.g., $\delta$-(L-$\alpha$-aminoadipyl)-L-cysteinyl-D-valine, the penicillin tripeptide precursor).
> 5. Production of SMs **diverts excess carbon** away from biomass when growth is constrained.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Comprehensive Question

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Module 3 Pattern — Internal Choice]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the biosynthesis of terpenoid secondary metabolites. Discuss the mevalonate (MVA) and MEP pathways with their industrial significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Introduction (1 mark):** Terpenoids are the **largest class of secondary metabolites** (>50,000 known structures), built from **C5 isoprene units** — isopentenyl pyrophosphate (IPP) and dimethylallyl pyrophosphate (DMAPP). They are classified by C5 count: monoterpenes (C10), sesquiterpenes (C15), diterpenes (C20), triterpenes (C30), tetraterpenes (C40).

> **Mevalonate Pathway (MVA) (2.5 marks):** Occurs in **eukaryotes, archaea, and the cytosol of plants**.
> - 3 molecules of **acetyl-CoA** condense via **HMG-CoA synthase** to form **HMG-CoA**.
> - HMG-CoA is reduced to **mevalonate** by HMG-CoA reductase (rate-limiting step).
> - Mevalonate → mevalonate-5-phosphate → mevalonate-5-pyrophosphate → **IPP** (which isomerizes to DMAPP).

> **MEP (Methylerythritol Phosphate) Pathway (2.5 marks):** Occurs in **bacteria, chloroplasts of plants, and apicoplasts of malaria parasites**.
> - Starts with condensation of **pyruvate + glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate**.
> - Forms **1-deoxy-D-xylulose-5-phosphate (DXP)** via DXP synthase.
> - Rearranges to **2-C-methyl-D-erythritol-4-phosphate (MEP)**.
> - Through a series of cyclizations, yields **IPP and DMAPP**.

> **Industrial Significance (1 mark):** Mevalonate inhibitors like **statins** (lovastatin, simvastatin) block HMG-CoA reductase and lower cholesterol. The **MEP pathway** is a target for **novel antibiotics** since it is absent in humans — e.g., **fosmidomycin** is an antimalarial.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the role of *Streptomyces* species in industrial antibiotic production. Describe the regulatory mechanisms controlling antibiotic biosynthesis with reference to catabolite repression.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Role of *Streptomyces* (3 marks):**
> - *Streptomyces* is a genus of **Gram-positive filamentous bacteria** responsible for producing **>70% of clinically used antibiotics** (streptomycin, tetracycline, neomycin, chloramphenicol, vancomycin).
> - They possess **large linear chromosomes (8–10 Mb)** carrying **biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs)** — groups of genes encoding the entire biosynthetic pathway of a secondary metabolite.
> - Examples:
>   - *S. griseus* → streptomycin
>   - *S. aureofaciens* → tetracycline
>   - *S. fradiae* → tylosin
>   - *S. noursei* → nystatin

> **Regulatory Mechanisms (4 marks):**
> 1. **Catabolite Repression:** Rapidly metabolized sugars like **glucose** repress antibiotic gene clusters via the **glucose kinase (GlkA)** and global regulator **DasR**. Replacing glucose with slowly-metabolized **mannitol or glycerol** elevates antibiotic titers.
> 2. **Pleiotropic Regulators:** Proteins like **AbsA2, AfsR, AfsS** globally activate or repress antibiotic gene clusters in response to nutrient status.
> 3. **ppGpp (Stringent Response):** Nutrient starvation triggers accumulation of **guanosine penta- and tetra-phosphate**, which activates antibiotic gene transcription.
> 4. **A-factor (Gamma-butyrolactone signaling):** In *S. griseus*, the **A-factor** binds the **ArpA repressor**, derepressing streptomycin biosynthesis at the onset of idiophase.
> 5. **Two-component systems:** PhoR-PhoB senses phosphate limitation; PhoB activates antibiotic genes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing trophophase and idiophase:** Students often misstate the phase of secondary metabolite production. Memorize: **Trophophase = Growth; Idiophase = Secondary metabolite production.**
> 2. **Mixing up PKS and NRPS:** PKS = polyketides (from acyl-CoA); NRPS = peptides (from amino acids). Don't interchange them.
> 3. **Skipping units in numerical problems:** The Luedeking-Piret equation carries units; omitting them costs marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the glucose effect:** Always mention **catabolite repression by glucose** when discussing industrial antibiotic production.
> 5. **Naming examples without stating the class:** "Penicillin" alone is incomplete; write "**β-lactam antibiotic — produced by *Penicillium chrysogenum***."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - Secondary metabolites = **non-essential, low-MW, ecological role** molecules produced in **idiophase**.
> - Six major classes: **Alkaloids, Polyketides, Terpenoids, Phenolics, NRPs, Aminoglycosides**.
> - Building blocks: Acetyl-CoA → PKS; Amino acids → NRPS; IPP/DMAPP → Terpenoids; Shikimate → Phenolics.
> - **Luedeking-Piret equation:** $q_P = \alpha \mu + \beta$ — distinguishes growth vs non-growth associated products.
> - **Glucose effect / catabolite repression** is the single most important regulatory phenomenon for industrial antibiotic production.
> - Key industrial producers: ***Penicillium chrysogenum*** (penicillin), ***Streptomyces griseus*** (streptomycin), ***Aspergillus terreus*** (lovastatin), ***Saccharopolyspora erythraea*** (erythromycin).
> - **MVA pathway** (eukaryotes) vs **MEP pathway** (bacteria/plastids) — both produce IPP/DMAPP.
> - Phosphate limitation, N-limitation, slow-feeding strategies — all **trigger idiophase**.
> - Volumetric productivity $P_r = P_{final}/t$ — units g/(L·h).
> - Yields: $Y_{X/S} = \Delta X / \Delta S$; $Y_{P/S} = \Delta P / \Delta S$.
> - Strain improvement techniques: random mutagenesis, **recombinant DNA**, **metabolic engineering**, **CRISPR-Cas9**.
> - Plant-derived SMs (morphine, paclitaxel, artemisinin) often use **plant cell culture** or **synthetic biology in yeast** for sustainable production.
> - Remember **A-factor, ppGpp, AfsR, PhoB** as global/pleiotropic regulators in *Streptomyces*.
> - Always mention **industrial titers** (g/L) in numerical and essay questions to demonstrate depth.
> - **Cyclosporin A** — an NRP immunosuppressant; **lovastatin** — a polyketide anticholesterol; **artemisinin** — a sesquiterpene antimalarial.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production Processes for Various Classes of Secondary Metabolites like Antibiotics; Production of Enzymes
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT414 - INDUSTRIAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Production Processes for Various Classes of Secondary Metabolites like Antibiotics; Production of Enzymes

# Industrial Production of Secondary Metabolites & Enzymes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Primary vs. Secondary Metabolites

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Primary metabolites** are low-molecular-weight compounds produced during the **exponential (trophophase) growth phase** of microorganisms, directly linked to central metabolic pathways (e.g., amino acids, nucleotides, organic acids, ethanol).
>
> **Secondary metabolites** are complex, low-molecular-weight organic compounds produced during the **stationary (idiophase) phase** of microbial growth, after the rate of growth has slowed. They are **not essential for vegetative growth, reproduction, or primary metabolism**, but confer ecological and survival advantages.

**Key Engineering Distinction (KTU 2024 Emphasis):**

| Parameter | Primary Metabolites | Secondary Metabolites |
|---|---|---|
| Phase of production | Trophophase | Idiophase |
| Growth association | Growth-associated | Non-growth-associated |
| Production kinetics | Parallel to $\mu$ | Decoupled from $\mu$ |
| Industrial examples | L-Glutamic acid, Ethanol | Penicillin, Streptomycin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Idiophase Concept):**
> The **idiophase** is the phase *after* the exponential phase where secondary metabolism dominates. The shift from trophophase to idiophase is regulated by nutrient limitation (often phosphate, nitrogen, or carbon), a concept heavily tested in KTU exams.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a college student (the microorganism). During the *trophophase* (semester), the student works hard at basic daily survival — attending classes, eating, sleeping (primary metabolism). After exams (exponential growth stops), the student has spare energy to pursue *hobbies* like painting, music, or coding a side-project (secondary metabolites). These hobbies aren't essential for daily survival but are valuable commercially. Microbes behave similarly — once easy nutrients run out, they produce "specialized chemicals" with biological activity.

---

### 1.2 Classes of Secondary Metabolites (KTU Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> Secondary metabolites are broadly classified into **four major chemical classes** based on their biosynthetic origin. KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists *antibiotics* as the most industrially relevant class.

1. **Polyketides** — assembled from acetyl-CoA and malonyl-CoA units. Examples: *Erythromycin*, *Tetracycline*, *Amphotericin B*.
2. **Non-ribosomal peptides (NRPs)** — synthesized by peptide synthetases without mRNA. Example: *Vancomycin*, *Bacitracin*.
3. **Terpenoids / Isoprenoids** — derived from isopentenyl pyrophosphate (IPP). Example: *Taxol* precursors.
4. **Alkaloids** — nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds. Example: *Sanguinarine*.
5. **β-lactam antibiotics** (subclass of NRP-related) — *Penicillins*, *Cephalosporins*.
6. **Aminoglycosides** — *Streptomycin*, *Neomycin*.

---

### 1.3 Antibiotics — The Flagship Secondary Metabolite

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Antibiotics** are **low-molecular-weight** antimicrobial secondary metabolites produced by microorganisms (or derived semisynthetically) that, at **low concentrations**, inhibit the growth of (bacteriostatic) or kill (bactericidal) other microorganisms.

**Discovery Note:**
*Alexander Fleming* discovered penicillin in 1928 from *Penicillium notatum*. Industrial production later shifted to *Penicillium chrysogenum* (high-yielding strains via mutation/screening).

**Mechanism of Action Classes (Engineering Significance for Strain Selection):**

| Antibiotic Class | Target | Example Organism |
|---|---|---|
| β-lactams | Cell wall (transpeptidase) | *P. chrysogenum* |
| Aminoglycosides | 30S ribosomal subunit | *Streptomyces griseus* |
| Tetracyclines | 30S ribosomal subunit | *S. aureofaciens* |
| Macrolides | 50S ribosomal subunit | *Saccharopolyspora erythraea* |
| Glycopeptides | D-Ala-D-Ala termini | *Amycolatopsis orientalis* |

---

### 1.4 Industrial Enzymes

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus):**
> **Industrial enzymes** are **biocatalysts** produced at scale by microorganisms (or plant/animal cells) that catalyze specific biochemical reactions under industrial conditions of pH, temperature, and substrate concentration.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
An enzyme is like a **highly specialized 3D lock-and-key** tool. The enzyme (lock) has a precise 3D cavity (active site), and the substrate (key) fits perfectly. Unlike a lock, the enzyme is *reusable* — after letting one substrate in and converting it, the enzyme opens up for the next substrate. Industrial production of enzymes is essentially **growing millions of these tiny tools in big stainless-steel vessels**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Metrics Bolded:**
> - **1 g of pure enzyme** can catalyze the conversion of **$> 10^{6}$ substrate molecules per second**.
> - Industrial enzymes must withstand **non-physiological conditions**: $\text{pH} = 4$ to $11$, temperature $= 30^\circ\text{C}$ to $> 80^\circ\text{C}$.
> - Global industrial enzyme market exceeds **USD 7 billion** annually.

**Major Industrial Enzymes & Producing Organisms:**

| Enzyme | Application | Producer Organism |
|---|---|---|
| Subtilisin (protease) | Detergents | *Bacillus licheniformis* |
| Amylase | Starch hydrolysis | *Aspergillus niger* |
| Glucose isomerase | HFCS production | *Streptomyces murinus* |
| Pectinase | Juice clarification | *Aspergillus niger* |
| Lipase | Fats & oils | *Candida rugosa* |
| Cellulase | Textile, biofuel | *Trichoderma reesei* |
| Penicillin acylase | Semisynthetic β-lactams | *E. coli* |
| Lactase | Dairy | *Kluyveromyces lactis* |

---

### 1.5 Conceptual Difference: Secondary Metabolite vs. Enzyme Production

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Hook (Frequently Asked):**
> - **Antibiotic production** is a **two-stage process** with *trophophase* (rapid biomass buildup) followed by *idiophase* (slow antibiotic synthesis). Nutrient limitation **triggers** production.
> - **Enzyme production** is often **growth-associated or partially associated**, depending on whether the enzyme is *intracellular* (mostly growth-associated) or *extracellular* (often partially growth-associated).
