## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*Define monoclonal antibodies. Mention the role of Köhler and Milstein in its development.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Monoclonal antibodies are homogeneous, monospecific antibody molecules produced by a single clone of B-lymphocytes, all directed against a single epitope of an antigen (1 mark). They are produced using hybridoma technology, developed in **1975 by Georges Köhler and César Milstein** at the MRC Laboratory of Molecular Biology, Cambridge (1 mark). They were awarded the **Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1984** (along with Niels K. Jerne) for this breakthrough, which revolutionized diagnostics, therapeutics, and biological research (1 mark).

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]**
*What is HAT medium? Why is it used in hybridoma technology?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
HAT medium is a selective growth medium containing **H**ypoxanthine, **A**minopterin, and **T**hymidine (1 mark). Aminopterin blocks the *de novo* DNA synthesis pathway, forcing cells to use the salvage pathway requiring hypoxanthine and the enzyme **HGPRT** (Hypoxanthine-Guanine Phosphoribosyltransferase) (1 mark). Myeloma cells used in fusion are HGPRT-deficient mutants, so they die in HAT medium. Only **B-lymphocyte–myeloma hybridomas** (with functional HGPRT from B-cell and immortality from myeloma) survive, making HAT a powerful selection tool (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

**Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 4 Choice A]**
*(a)* Describe in detail the production of monoclonal antibodies by hybridoma technology, with a clear note on cell fusion and HAT selection. **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* Discuss the different types of monoclonal antibodies and their therapeutic applications. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Production of Monoclonal Antibodies by Hybridoma Technology (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Immunization (1 mark):** A BALB/c mouse is injected intraperitoneally with the target antigen emulsified in Freund's adjuvant at 2–3 week intervals (typically 3–4 doses). The final booster is given 3–4 days before spleen harvest to maximize activated B-cells.

**Step 2 — Spleen Cell Harvest and Myeloma Preparation (1 mark):** The spleen is aseptically removed, and a single-cell suspension of B-lymphocytes is prepared. In parallel, HGPRT-deficient myeloma cells (e.g., SP2/0, NS0) are cultured in log phase.

**Step 3 — Cell Fusion (1 mark):** B-lymphocytes are mixed with myeloma cells in a 5:1 to 10:1 ratio, and fusion is induced using **40–50% PEG 1450** for ~1 minute, or by electroporation. PEG dehydrates cell membranes, causing them to fuse into heterokaryons.

**Step 4 — HAT Selection (2 marks):** The fused cell mixture is cultured in **HAT medium** (Hypoxanthine, Aminopterin, Thymidine). Aminopterin blocks *de novo* nucleotide synthesis. Cells must use the salvage pathway (requiring HGPRT enzyme). Unfused myeloma cells (HGPRT−) die. Unfused B-cells (short-lived) die. Only B-myeloma hybridomas (HGPRT+ and immortal) survive and proliferate as discrete colonies in 96-well plates.

**Step 5 — Screening, Cloning, and Production (2 marks):** Supernatants from each well are screened for desired antibody specificity by ELISA. Positive wells are cloned twice by **limiting dilution** (0.3 cells/well) to ensure monoclonality. The monoclonal hybridoma is then expanded either in vitro (bioreactor) or in vivo (mouse ascites), and mAbs are purified by **Protein A affinity chromatography**.

**(b) Types of Monoclonal Antibodies and Therapeutic Applications (7 Marks):**

| Type | Description | Example | Therapeutic Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Murine (-omab)** | Fully mouse-derived; high immunogenicity, short half-life | Muromonab-CD3 | Acute transplant rejection |
| **Chimeric (-ximab)** | Mouse variable + Human constant; less immunogenic | Rituximab | Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, RA |
| **Humanized (-zumab)** | Only mouse CDRs grafted; minimal immunogenicity | Trastuzumab (Herceptin) | HER2+ breast cancer |
| **Fully Human (-umab)** | 100% human; from phage display or transgenic mice | Adalimumab (Humira) | RA, Crohn's, psoriasis |

**Other Therapeutic Applications (1 mark):**
- **Oncology:** Cetuximab (colorectal cancer), Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF).
- **Autoimmune:** Infliximab (TNF-α inhibitor for Crohn's), Ocrelizumab (multiple sclerosis).
- **Infectious disease:** Palivizumab (RSV in neonates).
- **Cardiology:** Abciximab (anti-platelet during angioplasty).

**Advantages of Modern mAbs (1 mark):** High specificity, low toxicity, predictable pharmacokinetics, ability to be conjugated to toxins/radioisotopes for targeted therapy (ADC — Antibody-Drug Conjugates).

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 4 Choice B]**
*(a)* Explain the principle and mechanism of HAT selection in hybridoma technology. Why is myeloma cell line HGPRT-deficient? **(7 Marks)**
*(b)* Compare the in vivo (ascites) and in vitro (bioreactor) methods of monoclonal antibody production, listing advantages and disadvantages of each. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Principle and Mechanism of HAT Selection (7 Marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** HAT selection exploits the metabolic difference between normal cells and HGPRT-deficient myeloma cells. The medium forces cells to use the hypoxanthine-guanine salvage pathway for nucleotide synthesis, eliminating all cells that lack functional HGPRT.

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
- **Aminopterin** blocks *de novo* synthesis of purines and thymidine by inhibiting dihydrofolate reductase.
- **Hypoxanthine** serves as a purine precursor in the salvage pathway.
- **Thymidine** bypasses the blocked dTMP synthesis step.
- The salvage pathway requires **HGPRT** to convert hypoxanthine → IMP → AMP, GMP.
- Myeloma cells are **HGPRT-deficient** (selected by growth in 8-azaguanine or 6-thioguanine), so they cannot use hypoxanthine and die.
- B-cells have functional HGPRT but cannot proliferate indefinitely.
- Only **B-myeloma hybridomas** have both functional HGPRT and immortality → survive.

**Why Myeloma is HGPRT-Deficient (2 marks):** The deficiency is deliberately induced or selected in the parent myeloma cell line (e.g., by mutation using 8-azaguanine, which is toxic only to HGPRT+ cells). This pre-existing mutation is the cornerstone of the selection strategy, ensuring that any HGPRT+ survivor in HAT must have acquired the gene from the fused B-cell, providing a powerful positive selection for hybridomas.

**(b) Ascites vs. Bioreactor Production (7 Marks):**

**Ascites (In Vivo) Method (3.5 Marks):**
- **Principle:** Hybridoma cells are injected intraperitoneally into a primed mouse (e.g., pristane-treated), where they grow as an ascites tumor, producing high mAb concentrations in the ascitic fluid.
- **Yield:** 5–10 mg/mL, ~40 mg per mouse.
- **Advantages:** High mAb concentration; well-established; useful for small-scale research.
- **Disadvantages:** Severe animal welfare concerns; **banned for human therapeutic mAb production in EU**; contamination by mouse viruses, immunoglobulins, and proteases; expensive at scale; ethical issues.

**Bioreactor (In Vitro) Method (3.5 Marks):**
- **Principle:** Hybridoma cells are grown in stirred-tank, hollow-fiber, or perfusion bioreactors in serum-free or chemically defined media.
- **Yield:** 20–200 mg/L (suspension); up to 1–10 g/L in perfusion bioreactors.
- **Advantages:** Scalable to industrial volumes (1000–10,000 L); serum-free media reduce contamination; regulatory compliant; reproducible batch quality; lower long-term cost; no animal use.
- **Disadvantages:** Lower mAb concentration per volume than ascites; requires sophisticated process control (pH, DO, glucose); shear stress may damage cells; longer process development time.

**Conclusion:** Modern industry universally prefers in vitro bioreactor methods due to regulatory, ethical, and scalability reasons.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common 1-mark killer:** Students often write "myeloma cells die in HAT" without explaining *why* (HGPRT deficiency + aminopterin block). Always state the *mechanism*.
> 2. **Confusion zone:** Don't confuse **HAT** (Hypoxanthine, Aminopterin, Thymidine) with **HAT** as in histone acetyltransferase. In biotechnology context, HAT *always* means the selection medium.
> 3. **Suffix trap:** Memorize the four mAb suffixes: **-omab, -ximab, -zumab, -umab**. Students often swap "zumab" and "ximab." Quick trick: **xi** = chi**m**eric → **xi**mab. **zu** = hu**m**anized (from German *zusammen*, "together") → **zu**mab.
> 4. **Antibody structure pitfall:** Always draw the antibody with disulfide bonds and label the Fab vs. Fc regions. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for a correctly labeled diagram.
> 5. **Limiting dilution step:** Examiners expect you to mention that limiting dilution is **repeated at least twice** to ensure monoclonality. Just saying "cloned by limiting dilution" without repetition loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Adjuvant name:** Always specify **Freund's adjuvant** (complete or incomplete) — not just "adjuvant." A specific name = full mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Monoclonal antibodies** = single specificity, single clone, single epitope. **Polyclonal** = multi-specificity, multi-clone, multiple epitopes.
- **Hybridoma = B-cell (specificity) + Myeloma (immortality)**, fused using **PEG 1450** or **electroporation**.
- **HAT medium** = Hypoxanthine + Aminopterin + Thymidine; selects for B-myeloma hybridomas only.
- **Myeloma cells used are HGPRT-deficient** (HGPRT = Hypoxanthine-Guanine Phosphoribosyltransferase).
- **Aminopterin** blocks *de novo* nucleotide synthesis (inhibits DHFR).
- **Salvage pathway** requires hypoxanthine + HGPRT to make IMP → AMP, GMP.
- **Screening** is done by **ELISA**, not by HAT (HAT selects for hybridomas, ELISA identifies the *correct* antibody).
- **Limiting dilution** at 0.3 cells/well uses Poisson statistics to ensure ~85% monoclonal probability; repeat **twice**.
- **Four mAb types:** Murine (-omab) → Chimeric (-ximab) → Humanized (-zumab) → Fully Human (-umab).
- **Köhler and Milstein (1975)** won the **Nobel Prize in 1984** for hybridoma technology.
- **Mouse strain:** BALB/c (inbred) is the standard.
- **Adjuvant:** Freund's complete (first dose) + Freund's incomplete (boosters).
- **Purification:** Protein A affinity chromatography (binds IgG Fc region).
- **In vivo method:** Mouse ascites; **banned** for human therapeutics in EU.
- **In vitro method:** Stirred-tank or hollow-fiber bioreactors, serum-free media.
- **IgG MW = 150 kDa** (2H + 2L = 2×50 + 2×25 = 150).
- **Antibody valence = 2** (bivalent).
- **Examples to remember:** Rituximab (CD20), Trastuzumab (HER2), Adalimumab (TNF-α), Cetuximab (EGFR), Bevacizumab (VEGF).
- **Ascites yield:** ~40 mg/mouse; **Bioreactor yield:** ~200 g/batch (1000 L scale).
- **CDR H3 is the most variable** region and most often determines antigen specificity.
- **Applications:** Diagnostics (pregnancy, blood typing), Therapeutics (cancer, autoimmune), Research (Western blot, IHC, FACS).
- **ADC (Antibody-Drug Conjugate):** mAb linked to cytotoxic drug for targeted cancer therapy (e.g., Trastuzumab-emtansine).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Insulin Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT414 - INDUSTRIAL BIOTECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Human Insulin Production # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Human Insulin Production (KTU 2024 Definition):** > Human Insulin Production refers to the large-scale biomanufacturing of biosynthetic human insulin using **Recombinant DNA (rDNA) Technology** by inserting the human insulin gene (or its synthetic analogues) into suitable microbial host systems — most notably *Escherichia coli* (prokaryotic) and *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (eukaryotic) — followed by fermentation, recovery, purification, and formulation into therapeutic dosage forms. In the context of the KTU **PEPOT414 – Industrial Biotechnology** syllabus, this topic represents the **flagship case study** of the pharmaceutical-biotech industry, demonstrating how a once animal-derived (porcine/bovine) therapeutic molecule was successfully transitioned to a **microbial fermentation product**, revolutionizing diabetes mellitus treatment worldwide. ### 1.1.1 Biochemical Identity of Insulin | Parameter | Specification | |---|---| | **Molecular Weight** | **5808 Da** (mature human insulin) | | **Amino Acid Residues** | 51 (21 in A-chain, 30 in B-chain) | | **Chain Architecture** | Heterodimer: A-chain + B-chain | | **Disulfide Bonds** | 2 interchain + 1 intrachain (A6–A11) | | **Gene Location** | Chromosome **11p15.5** (INS gene) | | **Therapeutic Use** | Type 1 & advanced Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lock and Key Factory" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "Photocopying a Master Key":** > Imagine a locksmith (the **microbial host cell**) who cannot make a master key (insulin) on his own. Scientists extract the *blueprint* of the key (the **insulin gene**) from human pancreatic cells and hand it to the locksmith. The locksmith then reads the blueprint and manufactures **millions of identical keys** (insulin molecules) inside his workshop (the **fermenter/bioreactor**). Finally, the keys are collected, polished (purified), packed, and dispatched to patients who need them to "unlock" glucose uptake in their cells. This is precisely the essence of recombinant insulin production — *transferring the genetic blueprint* from a human cell to a microorganism that can amplify production at industrial scale. ## 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Focus Point:** KTU examiners frequently ask about the *pioneering companies* and *year of commercialization*. Memorize the two landmark milestones: > - **1982** — **Eli Lilly** launched **Humulin®** (first rDNA human insulin) using *E. coli*. > - **1987** — **Novo Nordisk** launched rDNA insulin using *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (yeast). Prior to 1982, insulin was exclusively extracted from **pancreases of slaughtered pigs and cows** (~**50,000 animal pancreases** were required to produce insulin for **one diabetic patient for one year**). The rDNA revolution ended this dependency. ## 1.4 Why Recombinant Insulin is Superior > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Critical Advantages of rDNA Human Insulin over Animal Insulin:** > 1. **Purity** — Free from animal pancreatic contaminants (e.g., proinsulin, glucagon, somatostatin impurities). > 2. **Immunogenicity** — Eliminates antibody formation seen with porcine/bovine insulin due to amino acid differences (porcine differs in 1 AA; bovine in 3 AA). > 3. **Unlimited Supply** — Microbial fermentation enables **virtually unlimited scale-up**, freeing diabetes care from slaughterhouse yield constraints. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Molecular Architecture of Proinsulin The key to understanding recombinant insulin production lies in grasping the **proinsulin precursor** concept. ### 2.1.1 The Proinsulin Molecule Human insulin is biosynthesized in pancreatic **β-cells** of the **Islets of Langerhans** as a single-chain precursor called **preproinsulin**: $$\text{Preproinsulin} \xrightarrow{\text{signal peptidase}} \text{Proinsulin}$$ **Proinsulin** consists of three continuous segments along a single polypeptide chain: - **B-chain** (30 residues) — at the N-terminus - **C-peptide** (Connecting peptide, ~31 residues) — middle region - **A-chain** (21 residues) — at the C-terminus The three disulfide bridges form **before** enzymatic cleavage of C-peptide: $$\text{Proinsulin} \xrightarrow{\text{prohormone convertases (PC1/3, PC2)}} \text{Mature Insulin} + \text{C-peptide}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is the C-peptide important for recombinant production?** > The C-peptide holds the A and B chains in the correct spatial orientation so that disulfide bonds form accurately. In recombinant systems, scientists exploited this by engineering the **C-peptide** as a natural linker. ## 2.2 The Two Industrial Production Strategies ### 2.2.1 Strategy A — Separate Chain Method (Eli Lilly, 1978) This is the **classical Eli Lilly approach** using *E. coli*: **Step-by-step logic:** 1. Synthesize the **A-chain gene** and **B-chain gene** **separately** (via chemical oligonucleotide synthesis or cDNA). 2. Insert each gene into a separate **plasmid vector** (e.g., **pBR322** derivatives). 3. Transform each plasmid into a **separate strain of *E. coli***. 4. Ferment both strains independently → produce **β-galactosidase-insulin fusion proteins** (as inclusion bodies). 5. Harvest inclusion bodies → **lyse cells** → solubilize with **urea / guanidine-HCl**. 6. Cleave fusion protein with **cyanogen bromide (CNBr)** at methionine residues. 7. **Sulfitolysis** (Na₂SO₃ + Na₂S₄O₆) protects thiol groups as S-sulfonates. 8. **Mix A and B chains** in correct ratio → disulfide bond formation occurs via **redox shuffling** (glutathione system: GSH/GSSG). 9. **Purify** by **reverse-phase HPLC** and **ion-exchange chromatography**. ### 2.2.2 Strategy B — Proinsulin Method (Novo Nordisk, 1987) This method uses **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** (yeast): **Step-by-step logic:** 1. Insert the **entire proinsulin gene** (A + C-peptide + B) into a yeast expression vector (e.g., **pYES2** based, with **GAL1 promoter** or **ADH1 promoter**). 2. Transform into *S. cerevisiae*. 3. Ferment yeast → yeast secretes proinsulin into the medium (or intracellularly). 4. Purify proinsulin → **enzymatic in-vitro conversion** to mature insulin using **trypsin** + **carboxypeptidase B** (replaces natural prohormone convertases). 5. Final purification → formulation. > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Aid:** "**A & B = America (Eli Lilly, E. coli)** ; **Proinsulin = Pure (Novo, Yeast)**" ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Unit | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Insulin Mature Form | $\text{A-chain (21)} + \text{B-chain (30)} = 51$ residues | Da = 5808 | Defines target product identity | | Disulfide Bonds | $\text{A6–A11 (intra)} + \text{A7–B7} + \text{A20–B19 (inter)}$ | — | Critical for bioactivity; require redox control | | CNBr Cleavage Site | $\text{Met–X} \xrightarrow{\text{CNBr, pH~2}} \text{Homoserine lactone + X}$ | — | Used in Lilly method for fusion cleavage | | Biomass Productivity | $P = \frac{C_{insulin} \cdot V}{t}$ | mg/(L·h) | Fermenter output metric | | Specific Yield | $Y_{P/X} = \frac{\text{Insulin produced (g)}}{\text{Biomass (g)}}$ | g/g | Strain efficiency indicator | | Volumetric Yield | $Y_{V} = C_{P,\text{final}} - C_{P,\text{initial}}$ | g/L | Reactor performance benchmark | | Dilution Rate (Chemostat) | $D = \frac{F}{V}$ | h$^{-1}$ | Continuous fermentation control | | Biomass Doubling | $t_{d} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu_{max}}$ | h | *E. coli* doubling ~**20 min** in optimal media | | Mass Balance (Reactor) | $\frac{d(PV)}{dt} = r_{P} V$ | g/h | Used for kinetic modeling | | Redox Potential | $E_{h}$ for S–S formation: **$-100$ to $-200$ mV** | mV | Critical in folding refolding step | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems:** > - *E. coli* fermentation: pH = **6.8–7.4**, T = **37°C**, dissolved O₂ > **30% saturation**. > - Yeast fermentation: pH = **5.0–6.0**, T = **30°C**. > - Inclusion body solubilization: **8 M urea** or **6 M guanidine-HCl**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The recombinant insulin production platform established: - The **template** for every modern therapeutic biologic (EPO, tPA, growth hormone, monoclonal antibodies, vaccines). - **Bioreactor design principles** for high-cell-density culture (HCDC) of *E. coli* — achieving **OD₆₀₀ > 100**, with dry cell weights > **50 g/L**. - **Downstream processing (DSP) trains** combining centrifugation, microfiltration, ultrafiltration, and multi-step chromatography. - The **regulatory framework** (FDA, EMA) for biosimilar approval pathways. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Insulin Mass Balance in a Fed-Batch Fermenter In industrial insulin fermentation, a **fed-batch mode** is typically employed to avoid **catabolite repression** (glucose suppresses the *lac* or *trp* promoter). We derive the volumetric productivity. ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup Consider an *E. coli* fed-batch fermentation with: - Initial volume $V_{0} = 1.0 \text{ L}$ - Initial glucose concentration $S_{0} = 5 \text{ g/L}$ - Feed glucose concentration $S_{f} = 400 \text{ g/L}$ - Feed rate $F = 0.05 \text{ L/h}$ (constant) - Specific growth rate $\mu = 0.45 \text{ h}^{-1}$ (post-induction, controlled by exponential feed) - Yield coefficient $Y_{X/S} = 0.40 \text{ g biomass / g glucose}$ - Insulin content in cell = **15%** of total cellular protein; total cellular protein = **50%** of DCW. - Final biomass $X_{f} = 35 \text{ g/L}$ ### 3.1.2 Derivation **Step 1 — Find final fermentation time using mass balance** For fed-batch, assuming exponential feed maintains constant $\mu$: $$V(t) = V_{0} \cdot e^{\mu t}$$ The total biomass produced is governed by substrate consumption: $$X_{f} \cdot V_{f} - X_{0} \cdot V_{0} = Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_{f} \cdot F \cdot t)$$ Assuming negligible initial biomass ($X_{0} \approx 0$): $$35 \cdot V_{f} = 0.40 \cdot (400 \cdot 0.05 \cdot t)$$ $$35 \cdot V_{f} = 8 \cdot t \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Step 2 — Volume balance during fed-batch** $$\frac{dV}{dt} = F \implies V_{f} = V_{0} + F \cdot t = 1.0 + 0.05t \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $t$ and $V_{f}$ simultaneously** Substituting (ii) into (i): $$35 \cdot (1.0 + 0.05t) = 8t$$ $$35 + 1.75t = 8t$$ $$35 = 6.25t$$ $$t = 5.6 \text{ hours}$$ Therefore: $$V_{f} = 1.0 + 0.05 \times 5.6 = 1.28 \text{ L}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate total insulin produced** Insulin fraction per cell: $$f_{insulin} = 0.50 \times 0.15 = 0.075 \text{ g insulin / g DCW}$$ Total insulin mass: $$M_{insulin} = X_{f} \cdot V_{f} \cdot f_{insulin} = 35 \times 1.28 \times 0.075 = 3.36 \text{ g}$$ **Step 5 — Volumetric productivity** $$P_{volumetric} = \frac{M_{insulin}}{V_{f} \cdot t} = \frac{3.36}{1.28 \times 5.6} = 0.469 \text{ g/(L·h)}$$ $$\boxed{P_{volumetric} = 0.469 \text{ g/(L·h)}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Logic Summary:** > $$\text{Glucose consumed} \xrightarrow{\times Y_{X/S}} \text{Biomass} \xrightarrow{\times V_f} \text{Total cells} \xrightarrow{\times f_{insulin}} \text{Insulin mass} \xrightarrow{\div (V_f \cdot t)} \text{Productivity}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Disulfide Bond Refolding Yield The **rate-limiting step** in recombinant insulin production is correct disulfide bond formation. Let us model the refolding equilibrium. ### 3.2.1 Equilibrium Setup In the *in-vitro* refolding step, the A and B chains are mixed in a redox buffer containing **GSH (reduced glutathione)** and **GSSG (oxidized glutathione)**. The reaction: $$\text{A-(SH)}_2 + \text{B-(SH)}_2 \xrightleftharpoons[k_{off}]{k_{on}} \text{Insulin (S–S bonds)} + 4 \text{ H}^+$$ Equilibrium constant: $$K_{eq} = \frac{[\text{Insulin}][\text{H}^{+}]^{4}}{[\text{A-(SH)}_{2}][\text{B-(SH)}_{2}]}$$ ### 3.2.2 Refolding Yield Calculation Let initial chain concentration $a_{0} = b_{0} = 0.5 \text{ mM}$ (equimolar), and let $x$ = insulin formed at equilibrium. At equilibrium: - $[\text{A-(SH)}_{2}] = a_{0} - x = 0.5 - x$ - $[\text{B-(SH)}_{2}] = b_{0} - x = 0.5 - x$ - $[\text{Insulin}] = x$ At pH = 8.5, $[\text{H}^{+}] = 10^{-8.5} = 3.16 \times 10^{-9} \text{ M}$ Given $K_{eq} = 1.0 \times 10^{20} \text{ M}^{4}$ (literature value for insulin refolding): $$K_{eq} = \frac{x \cdot (3.16 \times 10^{-9})^{4}}{(0.5 - x)^{2}}$$ $$1.0 \times 10^{20} = \frac{x \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-34}}{(0.5 - x)^{2}}$$ $$(0.5 - x)^{2} = \frac{x \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-34}}{1.0 \times 10^{20}} = x \times 10^{-54}$$ Since $x$ is approximately 0.5 mM (nearly complete folding): $$(0.5 - x)^{2} = 0.5 \times 10^{-54} = 5 \times 10^{-55}$$ $$(0.5 - x) = 7.07 \times 10^{-28} \text{ M (negligible)}$$ $$\boxed{x \approx 0.5 \text{ mM} \implies \text{Refolding Yield} \approx 99.99\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The enormous $K_{eq}$ thermodynamically drives insulin refolding to completion. However, **kinetic traps** (misfolded intermediates, aggregation) limit the *practical* refolding yield to **20–40%** per cycle — a major industrial bottleneck. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Eli Lilly Method (Separate Chains, *E. coli*) | Novo Nordisk Method (Proinsulin, Yeast) | |---|---|---| | Host Organism | *Escherichia coli* (prokaryote) | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (eukaryote) | | Vector | pBR322-based plasmid | Yeast shuttle vector (2µ origin) | | Promoter | *trp* or *lac* UV5 | *ADH1* or *GAL1* | | Product Form | Cytoplasmic inclusion bodies | Secreted into periplasm/medium | | Protein Folding | Requires *in-vitro* refolding | Native folding in vivo | | Disulfide Bonds | Manual *in-vitro* | Native enzymatic | | Cleavage Step | CNBr (cyanogen bromide) | Trypsin + Carboxypeptidase B | | Post-Translational Mods | **None** (prokaryote) | **Possible** (eukaryote) | | Fermentation T | 37°C | 30°C | | Scale-up Complexity | Higher (refolding) | Lower (secretion) | | Yield | Lower (15–20%) | Higher (40–60%) | | Regulatory Status | **First approved (1982)** | Approved 1987 | | Cost | Moderate | Slightly higher | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Insulin Molecular Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following diagram illustrates the **molecular structure of proinsulin** and its conversion to mature insulin, including the disulfide bonding pattern. ```mermaid graph LR A["B CHAIN
30 AA Residues"] --> B["DISULFIDE BOND
B7 to A7"] A --> C["DISULFIDE BOND
B19 to A20"] D["A CHAIN
21 AA Residues"] --> B D --> C D --> E["INTRACHAIN S-S
A6 to A11"] F["C PEPTIDE
31 AA Residues
CONNECTOR"] -.->|enzymatic cleavage| A F -.->|enzymatic cleavage| D A --> G["MATURE INSULIN
51 Residues
MW 5808 Da"] D --> G ``` ## 4.2 Recombinant Insulin Production Workflow — Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TD subgraph upstream["UPSTREAM PROCESSING"] A1["Human Insulin Gene Isolation
cDNA from Pancreatic mRNA"] --> A2["Cloning into Expression Vector
Plasmid pBR322 or pYES2"] A2 --> A3["Transformation into Host
E coli or S cerevisiae"] A3 --> A4["Master Cell Bank
MCB Establishment"] A4 --> A5["Inoculum Train
Flask to Pilot Bioreactor"] end subgraph midstream["MIDSTREAM FERMENTATION"] A5 --> B1["Production Bioreactor
Fed Batch 10000 to 50000 L"] B1 --> B2["Induction Phase
IPTG or Galactose"] B2 --> B3["Cell Harvest
Centrifugation MF"] end subgraph downstream["DOWNSTREAM PROCESSING"] B3 --> C1["Cell Disruption
High Pressure Homogenizer"] C1 --> C2["Inclusion Body Recovery
Solubilization 8M Urea"] C2 --> C3["Refolding
Redox Shuffling GSH GSSG"] C3 --> C4["Chromatographic Purification
RP HPLC Ion Exchange"] C4 --> C5["Enzymatic Cleavage
Trypsin Carboxypeptidase B"] C5 --> C6["Polishing Chromatography
Size Exclusion"] end subgraph fillup["FINAL FORMULATION"] C6 --> D1["Crystallization
Zinc Insulin Crystals"] D1 --> D2["Formulation
Injectable Solution Suspension"] D2 --> D3["Quality Control
HPLC MS Bioassay"] D3 --> D4["Final Product
Humulin Novolin Insulin Glargine"] end ``` ## 4.3 Comparison Flow — Lilly vs Novo Method ```mermaid graph TD L1["LILLY METHOD E COLI"] --> L2["Separate A and B Chain Genes"] L2 --> L3["Two Separate Fermentations"] L3 --> L4["Isolation of Fusion Proteins"] L4 --> L5["CNBr Cleavage at Methionine"] L5 --> L6["Sulfitolysis Protection"] L6 --> L7["Refolding A plus B Chains"] L7 --> L8["Mature Insulin Humulin"] N1["NOVO METHOD YEAST"] --> N2["Proinsulin Gene A-C-B"] N2 --> N3["Single Yeast Fermentation"] N3 --> N4["Secreted Proinsulin"] N4 --> N5["Enzymatic Cleavage Trypsin CPB"] N5 --> L8 L8 --> FIN["BIOSYNTHETIC HUMAN INSULIN
FDA APPROVED PHARMA GRADE"] ``` ## 4.4 Bioreactor Configuration for High-Cell-Density Culture (HCDC) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph bioreactor["INDUSTRIAL BIOREACTOR FOR INSULIN"] P1["pH Probe
6.8 to 7.4"] --> CTRL P2["DO Probe
30 percent saturation"] --> CTRL P3["Temperature Probe
37 degree C"] --> CTRL P4["Foam Sensor"] --> CTRL CTRL["Distributed Control System
DCS DeltaV"] --> A1 A1["Agitator Rushton Impeller
200 to 500 rpm"] --> MIX A2["Aeration Sparger
0.5 to 2 VVM"] --> MIX MIX["Mixing and O2 Transfer Zone"] --> CULT CULT["Culture Broth
OD600 greater than 100
DCW greater than 50 g per L"] CULT --> H1["Harvest Line"] H1 --> DOWN["Downstream Processing"] end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **Q1.** Define recombinant human insulin. Why is recombinant insulin preferred over animal-derived insulin? **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Recombinant human insulin is a biosynthetic polypeptide hormone of **51 amino acid residues** (molecular weight **5808 Da**) produced by inserting the human insulin gene into a microbial host (*E. coli* or *S. cerevisiae*) via recombinant DNA technology, followed by fermentation, purification, and formulation. **Reasons for preference over animal insulin:** 1. **Purity:** Free from animal pancreatic contaminants (proinsulin, glucagon). 2. **Immunogenicity:** Human sequence avoids antibody formation (porcine differs in **1 amino acid** at B30; bovine in **3 amino acids**). 3. **Supply:** Microbial fermentation is **unlimited**; animal source limited by slaughterhouse yield. 4. **Ethical:** No dependence on animal slaughter. 5. **Consistency:** Batch-to-batch uniformity is superior. *(Word limit ~150–200 words; 3 marks as per KTU 2024 scheme.)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Q2.** What is proinsulin? Explain its role in the biosynthesis of mature insulin. **Model Answer:** **Proinsulin** is the single-chain biosynthetic precursor of mature insulin, containing three segments in a continuous polypeptide: - **B-chain** (30 residues, N-terminal) - **C-peptide** (Connecting peptide, ~31 residues, middle) - **A-chain** (21 residues, C-terminal) **Role in biosynthesis:** - The C-peptide acts as a **molecular scaffold** holding the A and B chains in correct spatial orientation. - This allows the three **disulfide bonds** (A6–A11, A7–B7, A20–B19) to form accurately. - Subsequently, **prohormone convertases (PC1/3, PC2)** and **carboxypeptidase E** cleave out the C-peptide, releasing mature insulin. In **recombinant systems**, scientists mimic this by expressing proinsulin in yeast and cleaving it enzymatically *in vitro* with **trypsin** and **carboxypeptidase B**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` > **Q.A (a)** Describe the **Eli Lilly method** for the production of recombinant human insulin using *Escherichia coli*. Add a note on the **CNBr cleavage** step. **(7 marks)** > > **Q.A (b)** Explain the **downstream processing** of recombinant insulin with a focus on **inclusion body solubilization** and **refolding**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Eli Lilly Method (7 marks):** 1. **Gene Synthesis (1 mark):** The cDNA encoding the A-chain (21 AA) and B-chain (30 AA) of human insulin is synthesized using **reverse transcription** of pancreatic mRNA, followed by chemical oligonucleotide synthesis to remove introns. 2. **Cloning (2 marks):** Each chain gene is **separately** ligated into a plasmid vector (e.g., **pBR322** with *trp* or *lac* promoter) and **fused to the β-galactosidase gene** to form a fusion protein that protects the insulin chains from proteolytic degradation. 3. **Transformation & Fermentation (1 mark):** Each recombinant plasmid is transformed into a separate *E. coli* strain. Cells are cultured in large bioreactors (~**10,000 L**) under controlled conditions (37°C, pH 7.0, aerobic). 4. **Inclusion Body Formation (1 mark):** The fusion proteins accumulate as **insoluble inclusion bodies** within the cytoplasm, protecting them from host proteases. 5. **CNBr Cleavage (2 marks):** - The harvested cells are lysed; inclusion bodies are solubilized in **8 M urea**. - **Cyanogen bromide (CNBr)** cleaves specifically at the **carboxyl side of methionine residues** under **acidic conditions (pH ~2)**. - Methionine is converted to **homoserine lactone**, releasing the free A and B chains. - A methionine residue is **engineered at the junction** of each chain with β-galactosidase for this purpose. **Part (b) — Downstream Processing (7 marks):** 1. **Cell Disruption (1 mark):** High-pressure homogenization or French press at **15,000–30,000 psi**. 2. **Inclusion Body Solubilization (2 marks):** - Denaturants: **8 M urea** or **6 M guanidine-HCl**. - Reducing agents: **β-mercaptoethanol** or **DTT** to break incorrect disulfides. - **Sulfitolysis** using Na₂SO₃ + Na₂S₄O₆ converts cysteines to S-sulfonates (–S–SO₃⁻), preventing random disulfide formation. 3. **Refolding (2 marks):** - The S-sulfonate-protected A and B chains are mixed in **equimolar ratio**. - Redox shuffling buffer: **GSH/GSSG** (reduced/oxidized glutathione) at pH 8.0–8.5. - Correct disulfide bonds form **A6–A11 (intra), A7–B7, A20–B19 (inter)**. - **Practical refolding yield: 20–40%** per cycle due to aggregation. 4. **Purification (2 marks):** - **Reverse-phase HPLC** for initial capture. - **Ion-exchange chromatography** (cation exchange) for intermediate purification. - **Size-exclusion chromatography** for final polishing. - **Crystallization** with **Zn²⁺** to form zinc-insulin hexamers. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` > **Q.B (a)** Compare the **Eli Lilly (separate-chain) method** with the **Novo Nordisk (proinsulin) method** for recombinant insulin production. **(7 marks)** > > **Q.B (b)** A fed-batch fermentation yields **X = 40 g/L** biomass, with **15%** of total protein being insulin and cellular protein being **50%** of dry cell weight. If the final volume is **1.5 L** and fermentation time is **6 h**, calculate the **volumetric productivity** in mg/(L·h). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Comparison (7 marks):** | Aspect | Eli Lilly Method | Novo Nordisk Method | |---|---|---| | Host | *E. coli* | *S. cerevisiae* | | Gene strategy | Two separate chains | Single proinsulin gene | | Product | Inclusion bodies (intracellular) | Secreted into medium/periplasm | | Cleavage | CNBr (chemical) | Trypsin + Carboxypeptidase B (enzymatic) | | Folding | *In-vitro* refolding | Native *in-vivo* folding | | Yield | Lower (15–20%) | Higher (40–60%) | | Cost | Moderate | Higher | | Year of approval | **1982** | **1987** | | Purity | Excellent | Excellent | | Scale-up | More complex | Simpler | (7 marks — table + brief explanation of 3–4 key differences.) **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** Given: - $X = 40 \text{ g/L}$ - Insulin fraction of total protein = 15% = 0.15 - Protein fraction of DCW = 50% = 0.50 - $V_f = 1.5 \text{ L}$ - $t = 6 \text{ h}$ **Step 1: Insulin fraction per unit biomass** $$f_{insulin} = 0.50 \times 0.15 = 0.075 \text{ g insulin / g DCW}$$ **Step 2: Insulin concentration in broth** $$C_{P} = X \cdot f_{insulin} = 40 \times 0.075 = 3.0 \text{ g/L}$$ **Step 3: Total insulin mass** $$M_{insulin} = C_{P} \cdot V_{f} = 3.0 \times 1.5 = 4.5 \text{ g}$$ **Step 4: Volumetric productivity** $$P_{volumetric} = \frac{M_{insulin}}{V_{f} \cdot t} = \frac{4.5}{1.5 \times 6} = \frac{4.5}{9.0} = 0.5 \text{ g/(L·h)}$$ $$\boxed{P_{volumetric} = 0.5 \text{ g/(L·h)} = 500 \text{ mg/(L·h)}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–3 marks each):** > 1. **Forgetting disulfide bonds:** Insulin has **3 disulfide bonds (1 intra + 2 inter)**, not 2. Many students write only 2. > 2. **Confusing proinsulin with preproinsulin:** Preproinsulin contains a **signal peptide** (24 AA) that is removed in the ER. Proinsulin is the post-signal-peptide form. > 3. **Mixing up methods:** The **Eli Lilly method uses TWO fermentations** (one for A-chain, one for B-chain); the **Novo method uses ONE** (proinsulin). This is a very common 2-mark trap. > 4. **CNBr specificity:** Always state the cleavage is at the **C-terminal side of methionine**; don't write "after methionine" ambiguously. > 5. **Refolding yield:** Practical refolding yield is **20–40%**, not 100%. The thermodynamic $K_{eq}$ may favor product, but kinetic traps dominate. > 6. **Units in numericals:** Always convert final productivity to **mg/(L·h)** if asked; KTU examiners deduct for unit errors. > 7. **Year confusion:** **1982 = Eli Lilly (Humulin)**; **1987 = Novo Nordisk**. Do not interchange. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Human Insulin Production** - [ ] Insulin has **51 amino acids**: A-chain (21) + B-chain (30), MW = **5808 Da**. - [ ] **3 disulfide bonds**: A6–A11 (intra), A7–B7 (inter), A20–B19 (inter). - [ ] **Proinsulin = B-C-A single chain**; C-peptide is cleaved by **PC1/3, PC2** enzymes. - [ ] **Eli Lilly (1982)** — Two separate chains, *E. coli*, **CNBr** cleavage, **β-gal fusion**. - [ ] **Novo Nordisk (1987)** — Proinsulin in yeast, **enzymatic cleavage** (trypsin + CPB). - [ ] **Inclusion bodies** form due to overexpression in *E. coli*; solubilized in **8 M urea**. - [ ] **Refolding** uses GSH/GSSG redox buffer at pH 8.0–8.5. - [ ] **Purification train:** RP-HPLC → ion-exchange → size-exclusion → Zn-crystallization. - [ ] **Fed-batch fermentation** avoids catabolite repression; $\mu = 0.3$–$0.5 \text{ h}^{-1}$. - [ ] *E. coli*: 37°C, pH 7.0; *S. cerevisiae*: 30°C, pH 5.5. - [ ] Animal insulin: porcine (1 AA diff) and bovine (3 AA diff) at B30. - [ ] **rDNA insulin advantages:** purity, no immunogenicity, unlimited supply, ethical. - [ ] Volumetric productivity formula: $P = (X \cdot f_{insulin}) / t$ in g/(L·h). - [ ] HCDC of *E. coli* achieves **OD₆₀₀ > 100** and **DCW > 50 g/L**. - [ ] **CNBr mechanism:** Met-X → Homoserine lactone + X (acidic, pH 2). - [ ] Regulatory: **Humulin®** (Eli Lilly), **Novolin®** (Novo Nordisk) — first biosimilars. - [ ] Insulin analogues: **Lispro, Aspart, Glargine, Detemir** (engineered via site-directed mutagenesis). - [ ] **Trypsin cleavage sites in proinsulin:** at Arg-Arg (B-C junction) and Lys-Arg (C-A junction). - [ ] **Total product yield** = (Biomass × Protein content × Insulin fraction × Recovery efficiency). - [ ] Recovery efficiency in industry: typically **60–75%** of theoretical maximum.

topics.title: Bioreactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT414 - INDUSTRIAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bioreactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bioreactor (Biological Reactor / Fermenter):** A bioreactor is a closed, aseptic vessel or system of vessels engineered to provide a precisely controlled physico-chemical environment (temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, agitation, nutrient supply) for the growth of microorganisms, plant cells, animal cells, or enzymatic reactions, leading to the production of biomass, metabolites, bioconversion of substrates, or production of recombinant products on an industrially viable scale.

A bioreactor is, in essence, the **heart of any bioprocess industry**—it is the engineered device in which the biological transformation (catalyzed by living cells or enzymes) is carried out under optimized conditions. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically stresses that a bioreactor is more than a container; it integrates **mechanical, chemical, biological, and process-control engineering principles**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Bioreactor as a "Specialized Kitchen Reactor"**
> Imagine a master chef preparing a high-end dish inside a fully automated, climate-controlled, sterile "smart kitchen." Every parameter—temperature, humidity, ingredient-addition timing, stirring speed, oxygen supply (analogous to airflow for an aerobic microbe)—is monitored by sensors and regulated by feedback controllers. A bioreactor operates on the same principle: the **microorganism is the "chef,"** the **substrate/nutrients are the "ingredients,"** the **stirrer is the "spatula,"** and the **computerized control loop is the "head chef"** ensuring that no parameter drifts from the optimal setpoint.

### Key Functional Requirements of a Bioreactor
- **Aseptic operation** (exclusion of contaminating organisms)
- **Adequate mass transfer** of gases (especially O₂) and nutrients
- **Effective heat removal** to dissipate metabolic heat
- **Controlled shear environment** to avoid cell damage
- **Homogeneous mixing** to eliminate concentration/temperature gradients
- **Sterilizable design** (typically Stainless Steel SS-316L)
- **Instrumentation and control** of pH, DO, temperature, foam, agitation, feed

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Working volume (V_w):** typically **70–80 %** of total vessel volume
- **Aspect ratio (H/D):** typically **1.0 – 3.0** (commonly **2.0** for STRs)
- **Operating temperature:** typically **30 °C – 37 °C** for mesophilic cultures
- **Volumetric mass-transfer coefficient (k_L a):** typically **100 – 1000 h⁻¹** for industrial STRs
- **Sterilization temperature:** **121 °C** at **15 psi** for **20 min** (standard autoclave cycle)
- **Standard material of construction:** **SS-316L** stainless steel

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus emphasizes that the choice of bioreactor configuration is dictated by the **cell type** (bacterial, yeast, fungal, plant, animal), the **product** (intracellular, extracellular, volatile), and the **kinetics** (growth-associated, non-growth-associated). This is a frequently tested concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Bioreactors

Bioreactors are broadly classified based on **mode of operation**, **mixing mechanism**, and **cell type**.

### A. Based on Mode of Operation
1. **Batch Bioreactor** – closed system; all nutrients added at time zero; product harvested at end.
2. **Fed-Batch Bioreactor** – nutrients added intermittently or continuously; volume changes with time; no outflow until harvest.
3. **Continuous Bioreactor** – constant inflow of fresh medium and outflow of spent medium at steady state (Chemostat, Turbidostat).

### B. Based on Mixing Mechanism
1. **Mechanically Stirred Tank Reactor (STR / CSTR)** – uses Rushton, pitched-blade, or marine impellers.
2. **Airlift Bioreactor (ALB)** – uses rising bubbles for circulation; low-shear, ideal for shear-sensitive cells.
3. **Bubble Column Bioreactor** – simple sparged column; no mechanical agitation.
4. **Packed Bed Bioreactor** – cells immobilized on support; substrate flows through the bed.
5. **Fluidized Bed Bioreactor** – solid support particles suspended by upward flow; used for high-cell-density continuous operation.
6. **Membrane Bioreactor** – integrates reaction with membrane separation.
7. **Photobioreactor** – provides controlled illumination for photosynthetic cultures (algae, cyanobacteria).
8. **Wave / Disposable Bioreactor** – single-use, rocking-platform systems (WAVE, Sartorius, Cytiva).

## 2.2 Design and Operating Parameters

### 2.2.1 Aeration and Agitation
Aeration supplies oxygen and removes CO₂; agitation provides mixing and enhances mass transfer. The key parameters are:
- **Sparger type:** porous (fine bubbles) vs. orifice (coarse bubbles)
- **Aeration rate (v_vm):** volume of air per volume of liquid per minute (typically **0.5 – 2.0 vvm**)
- **Impeller speed (N):** typically **100 – 500 rpm**
- **Impeller diameter (D):** typically **D/T = 1/3** (for Rushton turbines)

### 2.2.2 Mass Transfer in Bioreactors (THE MOST IMPORTANT CONCEPT)

The limiting step in aerobic bioprocesses is the transfer of oxygen from the gas bubble → liquid → cell. This is governed by the **Two-Film Theory (Lewis & Whitman, 1924)**.

The **volumetric oxygen transfer rate (OTR)** is given by:

$$\text{OTR} = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

where:
- $k_L a$ = volumetric mass transfer coefficient (h⁻¹)
- $C^*$ = saturation dissolved oxygen concentration (typically 6–8 mg/L in broth at 30 °C)
- $C_L$ = actual dissolved oxygen in the broth

At **steady state**, OTR = OUR (oxygen uptake rate):

$$k_L a (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} \cdot X$$

where $q_{O_2}$ = specific oxygen uptake rate (mg O₂ / g cells · h) and $X$ = cell concentration (g/L).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Industrial bioreactor design ensures that $k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L) \geq q_{O_2} \cdot X_{\max}$. The **critical dissolved oxygen concentration** below which growth becomes oxygen-limited is typically **10–30 % of saturation (≈ 0.6 – 2.4 mg/L)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use \vert instead of \vert \vert for absolute values inside tables to avoid markdown breakage.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units / Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{OTR} = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$ | Oxygen transfer rate | mg O₂ / L · h |
| 2 | $\text{OUR} = q_{O_2} \cdot X$ | Oxygen uptake rate | mg O₂ / L · h |
| 3 | $\text{OTR} = \text{OUR}$ (steady state) | Mass-transfer balance | mg O₂ / L · h |
| 4 | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}}$ | Power number (dimensionless) | 5.0 – 6.0 (Rushton, turbulent) |
| 5 | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}$ | Un-gassed power input | W (Watts) |
| 6 | $P_g \approx 0.5 \cdot P$ (rule of thumb) | Gassed power | W |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^{2}}{\mu}$ | Impeller Reynolds number | $\geq 10^{4}$ (turbulent) |
| 8 | $k_L a = k \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)^{a} \cdot v_s^{b}$ | Empirical $k_L a$ correlation | h⁻¹; $a \approx 0.4$–$0.7$ |
| 9 | $D = \dfrac{F_{in}}{V}$ | Dilution rate (continuous) | h⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{D} = \dfrac{V}{F_{in}}$ | Residence time | h |
| 11 | $D_{cr} = \mu_{\max} \cdot \left(1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{K_s}{K_s + S_{in}}}\right)$ | Critical dilution (wash-out) | h⁻¹ |
| 12 | $\text{P/V} = \text{constant}$ (scale-up) | Common scale-up criterion | W / m³ |
| 13 | $N \cdot D = \text{constant}$ | Tip-speed scale-up | m / s |
| 14 | $k_L a = \text{constant}$ | Mass-transfer scale-up | h⁻¹ |
| 15 | $T_{sterilization} = 121^{\circ}\text{C}$ at 15 psi | Standard autoclave cycle | 20 min (liquids) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Recombinant protein, monoclonal antibody, and vaccine production (e.g., CHO cells in stirred-tank or wave bioreactors).
- **Food & beverage industry:** Beer, wine, vinegar, yogurt, citric acid via large STRs.
- **Enzymes and biocatalysts:** Packed-bed and membrane bioreactors for continuous enzymatic conversions.
- **Wastewater treatment:** Activated-sludge aeration tanks (essentially airlift/bubble-column bioreactors).
- **Biofuels:** Ethanol via continuous yeast fermenters; biodiesel via enzymatic transesterification in membrane reactors.
- **Algal biotechnology:** Photobioreactors for Spirulina, Chlorella, and high-value carotenoids (astaxanthin).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Power Requirement (P) in a Stirred-Tank Bioreactor

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Buckingham Pi Analysis

The un-gassed power consumption $P$ depends on the following variables:
- $N$ (impeller speed, s⁻¹), $D$ (impeller diameter, m), $\rho$ (density, kg/m³), $\mu$ (viscosity, Pa·s), $g$ (gravity, m/s²).

By **dimensional analysis**, two dimensionless groups emerge:

$$N_p = f(Re, Fr)$$

where $N_p$ is the **power number**, $Re$ the **Reynolds number**, and $Fr$ the **Froude number**. For unbaffled tanks, $Fr$ is significant; for baffled tanks (standard industrial configuration), surface vortexing is suppressed, and $N_p$ becomes a function of $Re$ only.

**Step 1: Define the Power Number**

$$N_p = \frac{P}{\rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}}$$

This is the ratio of the **imposed drag force to the inertial force** of the impeller.

**Step 2: Define the Impeller Reynolds Number**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^{2}}{\mu}$$

**Step 3: In the Turbulent Regime ($Re \geq 10^{4}$)**
$N_p$ becomes a constant depending on impeller type:
- Rushton turbine (6-blade disc): $N_p \approx 5.5$ – $6.0$
- Pitched-blade (45°): $N_p \approx 1.3$ – $1.5$
- Marine propeller: $N_p \approx 0.3$ – $0.5$

**Step 4: Calculate Power**

Re-arranging $N_p$:

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}$$

### 3.1.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> A 50 L working-volume STR uses two Rushton turbines (D = 0.10 m), impeller speed 300 rpm, broth density 1050 kg/m³, broth viscosity $1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate the un-gassed power and verify the flow regime.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Convert N to SI units.

$$N = 300 \, \text{rpm} = \frac{300}{60} = 5.0 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

Step 2: Calculate $Re$.

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^{2}}{\mu} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1050 \times 5.0 \times (0.10)^{2}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1050 \times 5.0 \times 0.01}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{52.5}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= 4.375 \times 10^{4}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $Re = 4.375 \times 10^{4} \geq 10^{4}$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

Step 3: Select $N_p$ for a Rushton turbine in turbulent regime: $N_p = 5.5$.

Step 4: Calculate $P$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5} \\[4pt]
&= 5.5 \times 1050 \times (5.0)^{3} \times (0.10)^{5} \\[4pt]
&= 5.5 \times 1050 \times 125 \times 10^{-5} \\[4pt]
&= 5.5 \times 1050 \times 0.00125 \\[4pt]
&= 7.219 \, \text{W (per impeller)}
\end{aligned}$$

For **two Rushton turbines** (standard for H/D = 2):

$$P_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 7.219 \approx 14.44 \, \text{W}$$

Step 5: Power per unit volume (a key scale-up metric).

$$\frac{P}{V} = \frac{14.44}{0.050} = 288.8 \, \text{W/m}^{3}$$

This lies in the typical industrial range of **100 – 1000 W/m³**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the k_L a Correlation (Van't Riet, 1979)

For a coalescing batch STR, the empirical correlation is:

$$k_L a = k \cdot \left(\frac{P}{V}\right)^{0.47} \cdot v_s^{0.67}$$

where:
- $k$ = constant (≈ 2.6 × 10⁻² for non-coalescing; ≈ 2.0 × 10⁻³ for coalescing broths)
- $P/V$ = gassed power per unit volume (W/m³)
- $v_s$ = superficial gas velocity (m/s)

**Significance:** This shows that $k_L a$ increases more steeply with $P/V$ than with $v_s$. Hence, **agitation is more effective than aeration** for enhancing oxygen transfer, but aeration is cheaper to provide.

## 3.3 Derivation of Critical Dilution Rate (Wash-Out Criterion)

In a chemostat (continuous stirred-tank bioreactor, CSTR), the steady-state mass balance on biomass gives:

$$D \cdot X = \mu \cdot X$$

Therefore, at steady state:

$$D = \mu$$

Using the Monod equation:

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{\max} \cdot S}{K_s + S}$$

Setting $D = \mu_{\max}$ (the **wash-out point**, where cells are flushed out faster than they can grow), and substituting $S = S_{in}$ at the inlet (since at wash-out $S \to S_{in}$):

$$D_{cr} = \mu_{\max} \cdot \frac{S_{in}}{K_s + S_{in}}$$

For $S_{in} \gg K_s$ (typical for industrial media):

$$D_{cr} \approx \mu_{\max}$$

**Engineering Implication:** The operating dilution rate $D$ must be kept below $D_{cr}$ to maintain a viable population; typically $D \approx 0.3 \mu_{\max}$ is chosen to maximize productivity.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Bioreactor Selection Guide

| Criterion | Batch / Fed-Batch STR | Airlift | Bubble Column | Packed Bed | Photobioreactor | Membrane |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cell type** | Bacteria, yeast, fungi, animal cells | Shear-sensitive (plant, animal) | Bacteria, yeast | Immobilized cells/enzymes | Microalgae, cyanobacteria | Any |
| **Shear level** | High (Rushton) – Moderate (pitched) | Low | Low–Moderate | Very low | Low | Low |
| **Mixing** | Mechanical + sparging | Bubbles only | Sparging only | Flow-driven | Sparging / pumping | Cross-flow |
| **O₂ transfer** | Excellent ($k_L a$ high) | Moderate | Moderate | Limited (needs pre-aeration) | Good (with CO₂) | Variable |
| **Operating mode** | Batch / Fed-batch | Continuous / Batch | Continuous | Continuous | Batch / Continuous | Continuous |
| **Scale-up** | Well-established | Moderate | Easy | Easy (modular) | Difficult (light penetration) | Moderate |
| **Product examples** | Antibiotics, mAbs, enzymes | Plant-cell metabolites | Beer, SCP | High-fructose syrup (glucose isomerase) | Astaxanthin, β-carotene | Lactic acid, ethanol |
| **Capital cost** | High | Moderate | Low | Moderate | High (for tubular) | High |
| **KTU relevance** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Stirred-Tank Bioreactor (STR)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Feed Reservoir Sterile Media] --> B[Sterile Inlet Piping with Steam Block]
    B --> C[Vessel Body SS-316L Jacketed]
    C --> D[Working Volume 70 to 80 percent]
    D --> E[Sparger Ring or Porous at Bottom]
    E --> F[Filtered Air Supply 0.2 micron]
    F --> G[Exhaust Gas Outlet through Condenser]
    D --> H[Impeller Assembly Rushton or Pitched]
    H --> I[Motor Drive with Magnetic Coupling]
    D --> J[Jacket for Temperature Control]
    J --> K[Cooling Water Circulator and Chiller]
    D --> L[Sensor Array pH, DO, T, Foam, Level, OD]
    L --> M[Process Control System DCS or PLC]
    M --> N[Control Loops PID for pH, DO, T, N, F]
    D --> O[Harvest Line Bottom or Side]
    O --> P[Downstream Processing Separation and Purification]
    D --> Q[Sampling Port Aseptic]
    M --> R[Operator HMI and Data Historian]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flow: Continuous vs. Fed-Batch vs. Batch Operation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Operating Mode Decision] --> B{Batch Process}
    A --> C{Fed-Batch Process}
    A --> D{Continuous Process}
    B --> B1[All nutrients added at t zero]
    B1 --> B2[No feed in, no harvest out during run]
    B2 --> B3[Entire broth harvested at end]
    C --> C1[Initial charge plus intermittent feed]
    C1 --> C2[Volume increases with time]
    C2 --> C3[Harvest only at end]
    D --> D1[Steady feed F in]
    D1 --> D2[Steady harvest F out at equal rate]
    D2 --> D3[Volume and concentrations constant at steady state]
    B3 --> E[Common for Antibiotics and mAbs]
    C3 --> F[Common for Recombinant Protein and PHA]
    D3 --> G[Common for SCP and Ethanol]
```

## 4.3 Airlift Bioreactor Internal Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Air Inlet through Sparger] --> B[Bottom Riser Zone]
    B --> C[Rising Bubbles Create Low Density Slug]
    C --> D[Top Section Gas Liquid Separator]
    D --> E[Degassed Liquid Higher Density]
    E --> F[Downcomer Zone Liquid Descends]
    F --> G[Bottom Loop Closes back to Riser]
    G --> B
    D --> H[Exhaust Gas Outlet through Filter]
    D --> I[Harvest Port]
    J[Sensor Array DO, pH, T, Foam] --> K[Process Control PLC]
    K --> L[Control Air Flow Rate and Temperature]
```

## 4.4 Mass-Transfer Pathway — Oxygen from Bubble to Cell

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Gas Bubble Bulk Gas Phase] --> B[Gas Liquid Interface]
    B --> C[Liquid Film around Bubble]
    C --> D[Bulk Liquid Broth]
    D --> E[Liquid Film around Cell]
    E --> F[Cell Membrane]
    F --> G[Intracellular Cytochrome Oxidase]
    G --> H[O2 used in Electron Transport Chain]
    H --> I[ATP Synthesis and Respiration]
    B -.->|kL resistance| C
    C -.->|Mixing control| D
    D -.->|Diffusion control| E
    E -.->|Cellular uptake| F
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the diagram:** The rate-limiting step in this chain (for an aerobic, fast-metabolizing culture) is almost always the **gas → liquid transfer** (steps B → C → D), i.e., $k_L a (C^* - C_L)$. This is precisely why $k_L a$ is the single most important design and scale-up parameter in aerobic bioreactor engineering.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *Define a bioreactor. List any four important functions that a bioreactor must perform.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A bioreactor is a closed, aseptic vessel designed to provide a controlled environment (pH, temperature, dissolved oxygen, agitation, nutrient supply) for biological reactions catalyzed by living cells or enzymes.
Four essential functions:
1. Maintain **asepsis** to prevent contamination.
2. Provide **adequate mixing** for homogeneous nutrient, gas, and heat distribution.
3. Ensure **sufficient mass transfer** of oxygen and other gases.
4. Enable **sterilization in place (SIP)** and **cleaning in place (CIP)**.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
> *Differentiate between a chemostat and a turbidostat.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Chemostat | Turbidostat |
|---|---|---|
| Control variable | Dilution rate (D) is fixed; nutrient is limiting | Cell density (turbidity) is fixed; D is varied automatically |
| Operating regime | Steady state at sub-maximum $\mu$ | Steady state at $\mu \approx \mu_{\max}$ |
| Sensor requirement | Only flow meters | Optical density sensor + feedback controller |
| Application | Kinetic studies, steady continuous production | Selection of fast-growing mutants, high-density cultures |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
> *(a)* With a neat labelled diagram, describe the design and operation of a **stirred-tank bioreactor (STR)**. Discuss the role of baffles, impellers (Rushton turbine), and spargers. (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* A pilot-scale STR has a working volume of **500 L**, impeller diameter **0.20 m**, impeller speed **250 rpm**, broth density **1080 kg/m³**, viscosity **1.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**, and uses **two Rushton turbines** ($N_p = 5.5$ in the turbulent regime).
> Calculate:
> (i) The impeller Reynolds number. (2 marks)
> (ii) The un-gassed power input (total, both impellers). (3 marks)
> (iii) The gassed power input (using the rule of thumb $P_g \approx 0.5 \, P$). (2 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Design and Operation of STR (7 marks)**

A stirred-tank bioreactor (STR) is a cylindrical vessel (H/D ≈ 2) with a rounded bottom, fitted with:
- **Baffles (4 vertical, width = T/10):** prevent vortex formation and improve mixing.
- **Impellers (typically 2–4 Rushton disc turbines):** provide radial flow; placed at H/3 and 2H/3.
- **Sparger (porous or ring-type):** at the bottom, just below the lower impeller; supplies sterile air.
- **Jacket / coil:** for temperature control.
- **Sensor ports:** pH, DO, temperature, foam, level, OD, gas analyzer.
- **Top plate:** fitted with exhaust gas condenser, foam control (mechanical or chemical antifoam), media addition, sampling ports.
- **Drive:** magnetic coupling to maintain asepsis.
- **Construction:** SS-316L, with all internal surfaces polished to Ra ≤ 0.4 μm for CIP/SIP.
- **Sterilization:** in-place by steam at **121 °C, 15 psi** for ≥ 20 min.
- **Operation:** batch, fed-batch, or continuous.

Diagrammatic representation as per Section 4.1 above carries full credit (3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for explanation).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

Convert units: $N = 250/60 = 4.167 \, \text{s}^{-1}$.

**(i) Impeller Reynolds number (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^{2}}{\mu} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1080 \times 4.167 \times (0.20)^{2}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1080 \times 4.167 \times 0.04}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{180.0}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
&= 1.20 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $Re = 1.20 \times 10^{5} \geq 10^{4}$, the flow is **fully turbulent**. ✓

**(ii) Un-gassed power (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{one}} &= N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5} \\[4pt]
&= 5.5 \times 1080 \times (4.167)^{3} \times (0.20)^{5} \\[4pt]
&= 5.5 \times 1080 \times 72.34 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
&\approx 5.5 \times 1080 \times 0.02315 \\[4pt]
&\approx 137.5 \, \text{W (per impeller)}
\end{aligned}$$

For **two Rushton turbines**:

$$P_{\text{total}} \approx 2 \times 137.5 = 275 \, \text{W}$$

**(iii) Gassed power (2 marks):**

Using the standard rule of thumb $P_g \approx 0.5 \, P$:

$$P_g \approx 0.5 \times 275 = 137.5 \, \text{W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common error 1:** Failing to convert rpm to s⁻¹. (–1 mark)
> - **Common error 2:** Forgetting to multiply by the number of impellers. (–1 mark)
> - **Common error 3:** Applying the wrong $N_p$ value. Use $N_p = 5.5$ for **6-blade Rushton disc turbine** in turbulent flow; for a **4-blade pitched turbine** use $N_p \approx 1.3$. (–1 mark)
> - **Common error 4:** Not stating the flow regime (laminar / transition / turbulent) after computing $Re$. (–0.5 mark)
> - **Common error 5:** Mixing up the gassed vs. un-gassed power equation. (–1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**
> *(a)* Explain the **two-film theory of oxygen transfer** in a bioreactor. Derive the expression for **steady-state dissolved oxygen concentration** in terms of $k_L a$, $q_{O_2}$, $X$, and $C^*$. (7 marks)
>
> *(b)* Define **scale-up** of a bioreactor. Explain the commonly used scale-up criteria: **constant P/V**, **constant $N \cdot D$**, and **constant $k_L a$**, with their industrial significance. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Two-Film Theory and Steady-State DO (7 marks)**

**Two-Film Theory (Lewis & Whitman, 1924):** A gas-liquid interface is visualized as two stagnant films — one on the gas side and one on the liquid side — across which mass transfer occurs by **molecular diffusion**. Outside these films, the bulk phases are assumed well-mixed. The **liquid-side resistance** dominates for sparingly soluble gases like O₂, CO₂.

The rate of transfer across the liquid film:

$$N_A = k_L \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

where $k_L$ is the local liquid-side mass-transfer coefficient (m/s) and $(C^* - C_L)$ is the **concentration driving force**. Multiplying by the interfacial area per unit volume $a$ (m²/m³):

$$\text{OTR} = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

**Steady-state derivation:**

The rate at which oxygen is supplied by transfer must equal the rate at which cells consume it.

$$k_L a \, (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} \cdot X$$

Solving for $C_L$:

$$\boxed{C_L = C^* - \frac{q_{O_2} \cdot X}{k_L a}}$$

**Engineering significance:**
- $C_L$ is controlled by balancing **biological demand** ($q_{O_2} \cdot X$) against **physical supply** ($k_L a$).
- If $X$ is high and $k_L a$ is low, $C_L$ may drop below the critical value, causing oxygen limitation.
- Industrial practice: maintain $C_L \geq 30$ % of $C^*$ (i.e., ≥ 2 mg/L) for most cultures.

**(b) Scale-Up Criteria (7 marks)**

**Definition:** Scale-up is the procedure by which a successful laboratory-scale process (typically 1–10 L) is translated to a production-scale bioreactor (10,000 – 200,000 L) while preserving the critical hydrodynamic, mass-transfer, and biological performance.

**Three commonly used criteria:**

| Criterion | Equation | Rationale / Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant $P/V$** | $\dfrac{P_1}{V_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{V_2}$ | Maintains same power dissipation per unit volume; preserves mixing intensity and $k_L a$. Most widely used criterion (especially for aerobic cultures). |
| **Constant $N \cdot D$** (constant tip speed) | $N_1 D_1 = N_2 D_2$ | Maintains same maximum shear at the impeller tip; used for **shear-sensitive cultures** (plant cells, animal cells, filamentous fungi). |
| **Constant $k_L a$** | $k_L a_1 = k_L a_2$ | Preserves oxygen-transfer capacity; used for highly aerobic processes where O₂ supply is rate-limiting. |

**Industrial significance:**
- These criteria are **mutually conflicting**; no single criterion works universally. Engineers use **judicious combinations** (e.g., constant $P/V$ in the lower range + constant $N \cdot D$ for shear).
- Pilot-plant studies (typically 100 – 1000 L) are used to validate scale-up predictions before full-scale production.
- Scale-down approaches (using multiple small reactors mimicking large-scale gradients) are increasingly used to de-risk scale-up.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls for Part B(b):**
> - Confusing $N \cdot D$ (tip speed) with $N \cdot D^2$ (shear rate). They are NOT the same. (–1 mark)
> - Stating "constant $P/V$" without mentioning that it preserves $k_L a$ proportionality. (–0.5 mark)
> - Failing to note that scale-up criteria are conflicting and that a compromise approach is needed. (–1 mark)
> - Omitting pilot-plant validation as a critical step. (–0.5 mark)

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Bioreactors (Module 4)**

- ⭐ **Bioreactor** = closed, aseptic, controlled vessel for biological transformations.
- ⭐ **Standard material:** SS-316L; **aspect ratio (H/D)** = 1–3; **working volume** = 70–80 %.
- ⭐ **Modes of operation:** Batch → Fed-Batch → Continuous (CSTR/Chemostat).
- ⭐ **Sterilization standard:** 121 °C at 15 psi for 20 min.
- ⭐ **Master equation:** $\text{OTR} = k_L a (C^* - C_L) = q_{O_2} \cdot X$.
- ⭐ **Power equation:** $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$.
- ⭐ **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho N D^2 / \mu$; turbulent if $Re \geq 10^4$.
- ⭐ **Rushton turbine $N_p$ ≈ 5.5** (turbulent); **pitched-blade $N_p$ ≈ 1.3**; **marine $N_p$ ≈ 0.3**.
- ⭐ **Gassed power ≈ 0.5 × un-gassed power** (rule of thumb).
- ⭐ **$k_L a$ correlation:** $k_L a = k (P/V)^a v_s^b$; agitation is more effective than aeration (higher exponent).
- ⭐ **Scale-up criteria (mutually conflicting):** constant $P/V$, constant $N \cdot D$, constant $k_L a$, constant mixing time, constant Re.
- ⭐ **Wash-out condition:** $D_{cr} \approx \mu_{\max}$ when $S_{in} \gg K_s$.
- ⭐ **Chemostat vs. Turbidostat:** D-controlled vs. OD-controlled continuous culture.
- ⭐ **Baffles (4, width T/10)** prevent vortex and enhance mixing in STR.
- ⭐ **Rushton turbine** = radial flow, high $N_p$, high shear; **Pitched-blade** = mixed flow, lower shear, suited to animal cells.
- ⭐ **Airlift bioreactor** = low-shear, ideal for plant cells, animal cells, and shear-sensitive cultures.
- ⭐ **Photobioreactor** = provides controlled illumination for photosynthetic organisms; light penetration limits scale-up.
- ⭐ **Membrane bioreactor** = integrates reaction and membrane-based separation (e.g., for lactic acid, ethanol, peptides).
- ⭐ **Disposable / Wave bioreactors** = single-use, lower validation cost, no CIP/SIP, gaining share in mAb production.
- ⭐ **Dilution rate $D = F/V$; residence time $\tau = 1/D$.**
- ⭐ **Critical DO** for most industrial cultures ≥ 30 % saturation (≥ 2 mg/L).
- ⭐ **Damkohler number** $Da = \text{reaction rate / mass-transfer rate}$ — interpret $> 1$ as mass-transfer-limited.
- ⭐ **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Always couple bioreactor selection to the **cell type** (bacterial/yeast/fungal/plant/animal) and the **product nature** (intracellular/extracellular/volatile/shear-sensitive).
